# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**
> Define **thermal resistance** for steady-state heat conduction. State the expressions for the conductive thermal resistance of a plane wall, a hollow cylinder, and a hollow sphere.

**Model Answer:**

Thermal resistance $R_{cond}$ is the opposition offered by a medium to steady one-dimensional heat flow, defined as the ratio of the temperature difference across the medium to the steady heat transfer rate through it. By direct analogy with Ohm's law, $Q = \Delta T / R_{cond}$.

**Expressions:**

$$\text{Plane wall: } R_{cond} = \frac{L}{kA}$$

$$\text{Hollow cylinder: } R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$$

$$\text{Hollow sphere: } R_{cond} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)$$

Where $k$ is the thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $A$ the cross-sectional area (m²), $L$ the wall thickness (m), and $r_1, r_2$ the inner and outer radii (m).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**
> What is the **critical radius of insulation**? Write the expression and state the physical significance.

**Model Answer:**

The critical radius of insulation is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat transfer rate from a cylindrical (or spherical) surface is **maximum**, beyond which adding more insulation reduces Q. It arises because increasing the outer radius simultaneously increases the external surface area (lowering convective resistance) and increases conductive resistance — these two effects oppose each other.

**Expression:**

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_{out}}$$

where $k_{ins}$ is the thermal conductivity of the insulation and $h_{out}$ is the external convective heat transfer coefficient. Its significance: for cylinders with bare radius less than $r_{cr}$, adding insulation initially *increases* heat loss. This concept is vital in cable design and small-pipe insulation.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Plane Wall

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 marks]**

A furnace wall is constructed of three layers:
- 115 mm of fireclay brick ($k_1 = 1.04$ W/m·K)
- 115 mm of insulating brick ($k_2 = 0.15$ W/m·K)
- 230 mm of building brick ($k_3 = 0.69$ W/m·K)

The inner and outer surface temperatures are $T_1 = 1370°C$ and $T_4 = 50°C$ respectively. The cross-sectional area is $1.5$ m².

**Compute:**
- **(a)** The rate of heat loss per square metre of wall area (7 marks).
- **(b)** The temperature at each interface (i.e., $T_2$ between fireclay and insulating brick, and $T_3$ between insulating and building brick) (7 marks).

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.115$ m, $k_1 = 1.04$ W/m·K; $L_2 = 0.115$ m, $k_2 = 0.15$ W/m·K; $L_3 = 0.230$ m, $k_3 = 0.69$ W/m·K; $A = 1.5$ m²; $T_1 = 1370°C$; $T_4 = 50°C$.

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute individual thermal resistances:**

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.115}{1.04 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.115}{1.56} = 0.0737 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.115}{0.15 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.115}{0.225} = 0.5111 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.230}{0.69 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.230}{1.035} = 0.2222 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 0.0737 + 0.5111 + 0.2222 = 0.8070 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Heat loss rate:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{1370 - 50}{0.8070} = \frac{1320}{0.8070} = 1635.7 \text{ W}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 4 — Compute temperature drop across each layer:**

$$\Delta T_1 = Q \cdot R_1 = 1635.7 \times 0.0737 = 120.55°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = Q \cdot R_2 = 1635.7 \times 0.5111 = 836.07°C$$

$$\Delta T_3 = Q \cdot R_3 = 1635.7 \times 0.2222 = 363.46°C$$

**Step 5 — Interface temperatures:**

$$T_2 = T_1 - \Delta T_1 = 1370 - 120.55 = 1249.45°C$$

$$T_3 = T_2 - \Delta T_2 = 1249.45 - 836.07 = 413.38°C$$

**Check:** $T_4 = T_3 - \Delta T_3 = 413.38 - 363.46 = 49.92 \approx 50°C$ ✓

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget that interface temperature is found by **subtracting the temperature drop** layer by layer from the hot side, not adding. The drop across each layer equals $Q \times R_i$.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Always **convert thicknesses to metres** (mm × 10⁻³) before substitution. A 115 mm = 0.115 m slip is the most common 2-mark loss.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Composite Cylinder with Critical Radius Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 marks]**

A steam pipe of outer diameter 6 cm and surface temperature $T_s = 200°C$ is to be insulated with a material of thermal conductivity $k_{ins} = 0.05$ W/m·K. The surrounding air is at $T_\infty = 25°C$ with a convective heat transfer coefficient $h = 10$ W/m²·K. Take pipe length $L = 1$ m (per metre of pipe).

**Compute:**
- **(a)** The critical radius of insulation and the heat loss rate at the critical radius (7 marks).
- **(b)** The heat loss rate when insulation thickness is 4 cm, and verify the behaviour with respect to the critical radius (7 marks).

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Bare pipe outer radius $r_1 = 0.03$ m; $T_s = 200°C$; $k_{ins} = 0.05$ W/m·K; $T_\infty = 25°C$; $h = 10$ W/m²·K; $L = 1$ m.

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Critical radius of insulation:**

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h} = \frac{0.05}{10} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Heat loss at $r_2 = r_{cr} = 0.005$ m:**

Total resistance:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_2 L)}$$

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(0.005/0.03)}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1} = \frac{\ln(0.1667)}{0.3142} = \frac{-1.7918}{0.3142} = -5.7030 \text{ K/W}$$

**Magnitudes only — absolute value of conduction resistance:**

$$R_{cond} = 5.7030 \text{ K/W}$$

**Convective resistance:**

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.005 \times 1} = \frac{1}{0.3142} = 3.1831 \text{ K/W}$$

**Total:**

$$R_{total} = 5.7030 + 3.1831 = 8.8861 \text{ K/W}$$

**Heat rate:**

$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{200 - 25}{8.8861} = \frac{175}{8.8861} = 19.69 \text{ W}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 3 — With insulation thickness 4 cm, $r_2 = 0.03 + 0.04 = 0.07$ m:**

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(0.07/0.03)}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1} = \frac{\ln(2.333)}{0.3142} = \frac{0.8473}{0.3142} = 2.6967 \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.07 \times 1} = \frac{1}{4.3982} = 0.2273 \text{ K/W}$$

**Total:**

$$R_{total} = 2.6967 + 0.2273 = 2.9240 \text{ K/W}$$

**Heat rate:**

$$Q = \frac{175}{2.9240} = 59.85 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Verification with respect to critical radius:**

Since $r_2 = 0.07$ m $\gg r_{cr} = 0.005$ m, the operating point is **far to the right** of the critical radius. Here, adding insulation is the **expected behaviour** — resistance increases and heat loss decreases *compared to the bare pipe* but, importantly, the bare pipe already has heat loss around $Q_{bare} \approx T_s/(\text{no extra resistance}) = $ extremely high.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall #3 (Composite Cylinder):** Students frequently confuse the **two log terms**. Always check that the ratio inside $\ln(\cdot)$ is the **outer radius over the inner radius of that specific layer**, and the $k$ in the denominator is the conductivity of **that layer's material**.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall #4 (Critical Radius):** Many students compute $r_{cr}$ but then forget to use it as the radius to evaluate the *maximum* heat loss. They mistakenly think $r_{cr}$ is the *minimum* insulation. Always plot a mental graph: $Q$ rises until $r = r_{cr}$, then falls.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Areas (Module 1 Composite Walls/Cylinders):**
>
> 1. **Wrong units:** $k$ must be in W/m·K, length in m, area in m².
> 2. **Confusing inner/outer radius:** The log ratio is always $r_{outer}/r_{inner}$ of the *same* layer.
> 3. **Using diameter instead of radius in $\ln(r_2/r_1)$:** This gives wrong answers by a factor that depends on the ratio.
> 4. **Forgetting the 4π in sphere resistance:** It does NOT cancel like the $A$ in plane wall.
> 5. **Adding resistances incorrectly in parallel paths:** Composite cylinder layers are always **in series**, never parallel.
> 6. **Not converting °C to K for temperature difference:** This is fine since we're using differences (ΔT_C = ΔT_K), but examiners still watch for sloppy notation.
> 7. **Critical radius misapplied to plane wall or sphere:** The formula $r_{cr} = k/h$ applies **only to cylinders** (and approximately to spheres with $r_{cr, sph} = 2k/h$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Density Revision Points:**
>
> ✅ **Fourier's Law:** $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$ — always include the negative sign or the direction convention explicitly.
>
> ✅ **Steady-state assumption:** $Q$ is **constant** through the body (no accumulation) — this is what lets you integrate cleanly.
>
> ✅ **Plane wall Q:** $Q = kA\Delta T / L$. **Resistance:** $L/(kA)$. **T profile: linear.**
>
> ✅ **Cylinder Q:** $Q = 2\pi k L \Delta T / \ln(r_2/r_1)$. **Resistance:** $\ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi kL)$. **T profile: logarithmic.**
>
> ✅ **Sphere Q:** $Q = 4\pi k \Delta T / (1/r_1 - 1/r_2)$. **Resistance:** $(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)/(4\pi k)$. **T profile: hyperbolic.**
>
> ✅ **Composite walls:** Resistances **add in series.** $R_{tot} = \sum R_i$. $Q = \Delta T_{overall}/R_{tot}$.
>
> ✅ **Interface temperature:** $T_{interface} = T_{prev} - Q \cdot R_{prev\, layer}$ (subtract the drop from the hot side).
>
> ✅ **Largest $\Delta T$** occurs across the layer with the **largest $R$** (always the insulator with low k).
>
> ✅ **Critical radius of insulation (cylinder):** $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_{out}$. Heat loss is **maximum** at this radius.
>
> ✅ **Insulation rule:** If $r_1 < r_{cr}$, **adding insulation first increases heat loss.** Use $r_2 > r_{cr}$ to get the expected insulation benefit.
>
> ✅ **Series vs. parallel:** Composite (multi-layer) walls/cylinders/spheres are **always in series** because the same $Q$ must pass through each layer. Parallel conduction paths exist in geometry with **multiple heat flow routes** (e.g., a fin with convection).
>
> ✅ **Units check:** $k$ in W/m·K, $L$ in m, $r$ in m, $A$ in m², $Q$ in W, $R$ in K/W, $\Delta T$ in K or °C.
>
> ✅ **Sphere vs. cylinder relationship:** $R_{sphere} = R_{cylinder} \times [1/2] \times [1/r_1 - 1/r_2]/[\ln(r_2/r_1)]$ — the ratio of resistances between the two geometries for the same $r_1, r_2$ is **less than 1**, meaning spheres conduct heat *better* for the same inner/outer radius pair.
>
> ✅ **Most common KTU trick:** Asking for **interface temperature** as a sub-part — practice the voltage-divider analogy.
>
> ✅ **Always quote the governing equation** (Fourier's Law) at the start of any derivation — this is worth 1 mark in a 14-mark question.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Critical Thickness of Insulation: Wall, Cylinder, and Sphere (Basic Numerical Problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Thickness of Insulation (r_c):** The thickness of an insulating layer on a curved (cylindrical or spherical) surface at which the **rate of heat transfer attains a maximum value**. Adding insulation beyond this critical thickness actually **increases** the heat loss rather than reducing it.

For a **plane wall**, the rate of heat transfer is a **monotonically decreasing** function of the insulation thickness — adding insulation always reduces heat loss. However, for a **cylinder** and a **sphere**, the heat transfer rate first *increases*, reaches a **maximum**, and then decreases as insulation thickness increases.

| Geometry | Critical Radius Formula | Existence of r_c |
|----------|------------------------|------------------|
| Plane Wall | Not defined | ❌ Does not exist |
| Hollow Cylinder | r_c = k / h | ✅ Exists |
| Hollow Sphere | r_c = 2k / h | ✅ Exists |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Numerical problems on critical thickness of insulation for cylinder and sphere are a **frequently tested 14-mark question** in Module 1 of PCFTT402. Students must memorize both formulas and the heat-transfer rate equations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine wrapping a **hot steel pipe** carrying steam with an **insulating wool jacket**:

- **At first**, when you wrap a thin layer, you expect heat loss to drop. But sometimes the **opposite happens** — heat loss actually goes *up*!
- This counterintuitive effect arises because adding insulation **increases the outer surface area** of the cylinder, allowing **more convective heat loss** to the surroundings.
- As long as the new surface area gained outweighs the conductive resistance added, the **net heat transfer increases**.
- There is one special radius — the **critical radius** — where the rate of heat loss is **maximum**. Beyond that, further insulation finally reduces the heat loss.
- A real-life example: An uninsulated pipe at room temperature often "sweats" (condenses moisture) more than a moderately insulated one, because the **cooler surface** of the moderately insulated pipe is below the dew point.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** The critical radius is a property of the **insulation material and the outside convection coefficient only** — it does **not** depend on the inner radius of the pipe or the temperature difference.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Thermal conductivity of insulation, **k** (W/m·K)
- Outside convective heat transfer coefficient, **h** (W/m²·K)
- Critical radius, **r_c** (m)
- Outer radius of insulation, **r₂** (m)
- Inner radius, **r₁** (m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Mechanism

The total thermal resistance between the hot fluid inside and the ambient air outside has **two components in series**:

1. **Conductive resistance** through the insulation layer.
2. **Convective resistance** at the outer surface.

When insulation thickness increases:

- The **conductive resistance** goes **up** (good — reduces heat flow).
- The **outer surface area** also goes **up** (bad — increases convective heat loss, since Q = hA·ΔT).

For a plane wall, the area is constant, so only resistance increases. For curved surfaces, the two effects **compete**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation Strategy

The standard KTU approach for solving critical-thickness problems is:

- **Step 1:** Write the expression for heat transfer rate (Q or Q/L) as a function of the outer radius r₂.
- **Step 2:** Differentiate Q with respect to r₂ and set dQ/dr₂ = 0.
- **Step 3:** Solve for r₂ → this gives the **critical radius r_c**.
- **Step 4:** Substitute r_c back into the heat-transfer equation to get the **maximum heat loss**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the formulas in this table — they account for ~80% of marks in numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Geometry | Heat Transfer Rate | Critical Radius | Total Outer Resistance |
|---|----------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Plane Wall | $Q = \dfrac{A(T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h}}$ | Not applicable | $\dfrac{L}{kA} + \dfrac{1}{hA}$ |
| 2 | Hollow Cylinder (per unit length) | $\dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi(T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2 h}}$ | $r_c = \dfrac{k}{h}$ | $\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h}$ |
| 3 | Hollow Sphere | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi(T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2^{2} h}$ | $r_c = \dfrac{2k}{h}$ | $\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}{4\pi k} + \dfrac{1}{4\pi r_2^{2} h}$ |

**Interpretation Rules for KTU Problems:**

- If $r_2 < r_c$ → increasing insulation **increases** Q (paradoxical regime).
- If $r_2 = r_c$ → Q is **maximum** at $Q_{max}$.
- If $r_2 > r_c$ → increasing insulation **decreases** Q (expected behaviour).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Processing Relevance (PCFTT402 context):**
> - Insulating **steam-jacketed kettles**, **sterilization retorts**, and **refrigeration pipelines** in food plants.
> - Preventing **condensation on cold pipes** carrying chilled water or brine.
> - Selecting the correct insulation thickness for **HTST pasteurization** heat exchangers.
> - Choosing between insulating and *not* insulating **small-diameter tubing** (e.g., r₁ < r_c) where insulation may worsen performance.

---

## 2.5 Plane Wall Behaviour — No Critical Radius

For a plane wall:

$$Q = \frac{A(T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h}}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. L:

$$\frac{dQ}{dL} = \frac{-A(T_1 - T_\infty)\left(\dfrac{1}{k}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h}\right)^{2}} < 0 \quad \text{(always negative)}$$

Hence Q **monotonically decreases** with L — no critical thickness exists.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Radius for a Hollow Cylinder

### 3.1.1 Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Length

Consider a long cylinder of inner radius $r_1$, outer insulation radius $r_2$, insulation thermal conductivity $k$, ambient temperature $T_\infty$, and inner surface temperature $T_1$.

The total thermal resistance per unit length (between $r_1$ and ambient) is:

$$R_{tot}^{''} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k} + \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 h}$$

The heat transfer rate per unit length is:

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2 h}}$$

### 3.1.2 Differentiate w.r.t. r₂ and Set to Zero

Let $f(r_2) = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2 h}$.

For maximum Q/L, the denominator f must be **minimum**, so set $df/dr_2 = 0$.

$$\frac{d}{dr_2}\left[\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{r_2 h}\right] = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2} + \frac{1}{h}\cdot\left(-\frac{1}{r_2^{2}}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^{2}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $k h r_2^{2}$:

$$h r_2 = k$$

$$\boxed{r_{c,\,cylinder} = \frac{k}{h}}$$

### 3.1.3 Maximum Heat Loss at Critical Radius

Substitute $r_2 = r_c = k/h$ into the Q/L expression:

$$\frac{Q_{max}}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_c/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_c h}}$$

Since $r_c h = k$, we get $1/(r_c h) = 1/k$, so:

$$\frac{Q_{max}}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_c/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{k}} = \frac{2\pi k (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\ln(r_c/r_1) + 1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** Once you find $r_c$, just plug it back into the standard Q equation — no need to memorize $Q_{max}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Radius for a Hollow Sphere

### 3.2.1 Heat Transfer Rate

For a sphere of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$:

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)\dfrac{1}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2^{2} h}}$$

### 3.2.2 Differentiate the Denominator

Let $g(r_2) = \left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)\dfrac{1}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2^{2} h}$.

$$\frac{dg}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k}\cdot\left(0 - \left(-\frac{1}{r_2^{2}}\right)\right) + \frac{1}{h}\cdot\left(-\frac{2}{r_2^{3}}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k r_2^{2}} - \frac{2}{h r_2^{3}} = 0$$

Multiply both sides by $k h r_2^{3}$:

$$h r_2 - 2k = 0$$

$$\boxed{r_{c,\,sphere} = \frac{2k}{h}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Behaviour at Different Insulation Levels

| Condition | Plane Wall | Cylinder | Sphere |
|-----------|------------|----------|--------|
| No insulation ($r_2 = r_1$ or L=0) | $Q_0$ | $Q_0$ | $Q_0$ |
| At critical radius $r_2 = r_c$ | Not applicable | $Q_{max}$ | $Q_{max}$ |
| Far beyond critical ($r_2 \to \infty$) | $Q \to 0$ | $Q \to 0$ | $Q \to 0$ |
| Between no insulation and $r_c$ | $Q$ decreases | $Q$ **increases** | $Q$ **increases** |
| Beyond $r_c$ | $Q$ decreases | $Q$ decreases | $Q$ decreases |

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem Statement:**
A steam pipe of outer diameter 60 mm is insulated with a material of thermal conductivity k = 0.06 W/m·K. The steam temperature is 180°C and the ambient air is at 30°C with h = 12 W/m²·K. Calculate:
**(a)** The critical radius of insulation.
**(b)** Heat loss per metre length of pipe **without any insulation**.
**(c)** Heat loss per metre length **at the critical radius**.
**(d)** Heat loss per metre length when insulation thickness is such that $r_2 = 2r_c$.

**Given Data:**
- $r_1 = 0.030$ m, $T_1 = 180°\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 30°\text{C}$
- $k = 0.06$ W/m·K, $h = 12$ W/m²·K
- $\Delta T = 150$ K

### Part (a): Critical Radius

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.06}{12} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

### Part (b): Heat Loss Without Insulation

With $r_2 = r_1 = 0.030$ m:

$$\frac{Q_0}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2 h}}$$

The conduction term $\ln(r_2/r_1) = 0$ (since $r_2 = r_1$):

$$\frac{Q_0}{L} = \frac{2\pi (150)}{\dfrac{1}{0.030 \times 12}} = \frac{300\pi}{2.7778} = 339.3 \text{ W/m}$$

### Part (c): Heat Loss at r₂ = r_c = 0.005 m

$$\frac{Q_{max}}{L} = \frac{2\pi (150)}{\dfrac{\ln(0.005/0.030)}{0.06} + \dfrac{1}{0.005 \times 12}}$$

Computing each term:

$$\ln\left(\frac{0.005}{0.030}\right) = \ln(0.1667) = -1.7918$$

$$\frac{-1.7918}{0.06} = -29.863 \quad \Rightarrow \text{abs value} = 29.863 \text{ (resistance term)}$$

$$\frac{1}{0.005 \times 12} = \frac{1}{0.06} = 16.667$$

Sum of denominator terms: $29.863 + 16.667 = 46.530$

$$\frac{Q_{max}}{L} = \frac{300\pi}{46.530} = 20.26 \text{ W/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly take $| \ln(r_2/r_1) \vert$ as a positive number and ADD it directly. Since the natural log of a number less than 1 is **negative**, you must keep the sign. The denominator in the formula is the **sum of two positive resistance terms** — handle signs carefully.

### Part (d): Heat Loss at r₂ = 2r_c = 0.010 m

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (150)}{\dfrac{\ln(0.010/0.030)}{0.06} + \dfrac{1}{0.010 \times 12}}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{0.010}{0.030}\right) = \ln(0.3333) = -1.0986$$

$$\frac{1.0986}{0.06} = 18.310$$

$$\frac{1}{0.010 \times 12} = \frac{1}{0.12} = 8.333$$

Sum: $18.310 + 8.333 = 26.643$

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{300\pi}{26.643} = 35.37 \text{ W/m}$$

### Result Summary

| Case | Outer radius r₂ (m) | Q/L (W/m) | Observation |
|------|---------------------|-----------|-------------|
| No insulation | 0.030 | 339.3 | Highest |
| At critical radius | 0.005 | 20.26 | Lowest |
| At 2 × r_c | 0.010 | 35.37 | Higher than at r_c |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the paradox:** Heat loss at $r_2 = 2r_c$ is **higher** than at $r_2 = r_c$! This confirms that the critical radius does NOT minimize heat transfer — it **maximizes** it. The actual minimum heat transfer occurs at the largest practical insulation radius.
