# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define critical radius of insulation. Why does it not exist for a plane wall?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical radius of insulation (r_c)** is the outer radius of insulation at which the rate of heat transfer from a curved surface is **maximum**. For a hollow cylinder, $r_c = k/h$ and for a hollow sphere, $r_c = 2k/h$.

> For a **plane wall**, the heat transfer area is **constant** and does not depend on insulation thickness. Hence, adding insulation only increases the conductive resistance — the convective resistance remains constant. Therefore, heat transfer rate **monotonically decreases** with insulation thickness, and no critical radius exists.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formulas, 1 mark for plane wall reasoning.)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**A small electrical wire of diameter 2 mm has a critical radius of 3 mm. If the wire is operated at 100°C in air at 25°C, comment on whether adding insulation of 4 mm thickness will increase or decrease the heat loss.**

**Model Answer:**

> The outer radius after insulation: $r_2 = 1 + 4 = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m.
> Critical radius: $r_c = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m.
> Since $r_2 = 5$ mm $> r_c = 3$ mm, we are in the **normal regime**.
> **Therefore, adding insulation will DECREASE the heat loss.**

> Further, $k$ of insulation: $k = r_c \cdot h = 0.003 \times h$ W/m·K.

*(3 marks — 2 marks for comparison, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A hot fluid at **200°C** flows through an uninsulated pipe of outer diameter **80 mm**. The pipe is to be insulated with a material having **k = 0.08 W/m·K**. The pipe is exposed to ambient air at **30°C** with a convection coefficient of **h = 10 W/m²·K**.

**(a)** Derive the expression for the critical radius of insulation for a cylindrical pipe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the critical radius, the maximum heat loss per metre at this radius, and the heat loss per metre when the insulation thickness is **30 mm**. Compare your results. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Length of an Insulated Cylinder**

For an insulated cylinder with inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, conductivity $k$ and outside convection $h$:

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2 h}}$$

**Step 2: Define the Denominator Function**

$$f(r_2) = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{r_2 h}$$

For Q/L to be **maximum**, the denominator $f(r_2)$ must be **minimum** (since numerator is constant).

**Step 3: Differentiate and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{df}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{h}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^{2}} = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

Multiply both sides by $k h r_2^{2}$:

$$h r_2 = k$$

$$\boxed{r_{c} = \frac{k}{h}}$$

**Step 5: Physical Interpretation**

The critical radius depends only on the **material property k** and the **ambient convection h**. When $r_2 < r_c$, increasing insulation **increases** Q (since the additional area outweighs the resistance gain). When $r_2 > r_c$, increasing insulation **decreases** Q normally. *(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for differentiation, 2 for final answer and interpretation.)*

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 200°\text{C}, \quad T_\infty = 30°\text{C}, \quad \Delta T = 170$ K
- $r_1 = 0.040$ m, $\quad k = 0.08$ W/m·K, $\quad h = 10$ W/m²·K
- Insulation thickness $t = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m, so $r_2 = 0.040 + 0.030 = 0.070$ m

**(i) Critical Radius:**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.08}{10} = 0.008 \text{ m} = 8 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Maximum Heat Loss per Metre (at $r_2 = r_c = 0.008$ m):**

$$\frac{Q_{max}}{L} = \frac{2\pi (170)}{\dfrac{\ln(0.008/0.040)}{0.08} + \dfrac{1}{0.008 \times 10}}$$

Compute each term:
- $\ln(0.008/0.040) = \ln(0.2) = -1.6094$
- Conduction term: $-1.6094 / 0.08 = -20.118$ → resistance magnitude = $20.118$
- Convection term: $1/(0.008 \times 10) = 1/0.08 = 12.500$
- Sum: $20.118 + 12.500 = 32.618$

$$\frac{Q_{max}}{L} = \frac{2\pi \times 170}{32.618} = \frac{1068.14}{32.618} = 32.74 \text{ W/m}$$

**(iii) Heat Loss per Metre with 30 mm Insulation ($r_2 = 0.070$ m):**

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (170)}{\dfrac{\ln(0.070/0.040)}{0.08} + \dfrac{1}{0.070 \times 10}}$$

Compute:
- $\ln(0.070/0.040) = \ln(1.75) = 0.5596$
- Conduction term: $0.5596 / 0.08 = 6.995$
- Convection term: $1/(0.070 \times 10) = 1/0.700 = 1.4286$
- Sum: $6.995 + 1.4286 = 8.4236$

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{1068.14}{8.4236} = 126.81 \text{ W/m}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Case | $r_2$ (m) | Q/L (W/m) | Comment |
|------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| At $r_c$ | 0.008 | 32.74 | Minimum (maximum insulation effect) |
| With 30 mm | 0.070 | 126.81 | Higher than at $r_c$ — but $r_2 \gg r_c$ |

> **Observation:** The 30 mm case has $r_2 = 0.070$ m $\gg r_c = 0.008$ m, well beyond the critical radius. Heat loss is higher than at $r_c$ because the surface area is 8.75× larger, but still much lower than the no-insulation case.

*(7 marks — 2 for $r_c$, 2.5 for $Q_{max}$, 2.5 for Q at 30 mm and comparison.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

A spherical stainless-steel storage tank of **outer diameter 1.2 m** contains a hot food product at **90°C**. It is insulated with a material of **k = 0.05 W/m·K**. Ambient air temperature is **30°C** and **h = 8 W/m²·K**.

**(a)** Derive the critical radius expression for a sphere. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute: (i) critical radius, (ii) heat loss at critical radius, (iii) heat loss with no insulation, and (iv) heat loss with 50 mm thick insulation. Discuss whether insulation is effective. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Heat Transfer Rate for Insulated Sphere**

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)\dfrac{1}{k} + \dfrac{1}{r_2^{2} h}}$$

**Step 2: Define Denominator**

$$g(r_2) = \left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)\frac{1}{k} + \frac{1}{r_2^{2} h}$$

Maximum Q requires minimum g.

**Step 3: Differentiate**

$$\frac{dg}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^{2}} - \frac{2}{h}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^{3}} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve**

$$\frac{1}{k r_2^{2}} = \frac{2}{h r_2^{3}} \Rightarrow h r_2 = 2k$$

$$\boxed{r_{c,\,sphere} = \frac{2k}{h}}$$

**Step 5: Note the Factor of 2**

Unlike the cylinder where $r_c = k/h$, the sphere has a factor of 2 because the **area scales as $r^{2}$** instead of $r$, leading to a different trade-off between conduction and convection.

*(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for differentiation, 2 for final expression.)*

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 90°\text{C}, \quad T_\infty = 30°\text{C}, \quad \Delta T = 60$ K
- $r_1 = 0.6$ m, $\quad k = 0.05$ W/m·K, $\quad h = 8$ W/m²·K
- Thickness $t = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m, so $r_2 = 0.6 + 0.050 = 0.650$ m

**(i) Critical Radius:**

$$r_c = \frac{2k}{h} = \frac{2 \times 0.05}{8} = \frac{0.10}{8} = 0.0125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Heat Loss at Critical Radius ($r_2 = 0.0125$ m):**

$$Q_{max} = \frac{4\pi (60)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{0.6} - \dfrac{1}{0.0125}\right)\dfrac{1}{0.05} + \dfrac{1}{(0.0125)^{2} \times 8}}$$

Compute:
- $1/0.6 = 1.6667$, $\quad 1/0.0125 = 80.0$
- Difference: $1.6667 - 80.0 = -78.333$
- Conduction term: $-78.333 / 0.05 = -1566.67$ → resistance magnitude = $1566.67$
- Convection term: $1 / (0.0125^{2} \times 8) = 1 / (0.00015625 \times 8) = 1/0.00125 = 800.0$
- Sum: $1566.67 + 800.0 = 2366.67$

$$Q_{max} = \frac{4\pi \times 60}{2366.67} = \frac{753.98}{2366.67} = 0.319 \text{ W}$$

> **Note:** Q at $r_c$ is very small because $r_1 = 0.6$ m is much larger than $r_c = 0.0125$ m. The sphere is far larger than the critical radius.

**(iii) Heat Loss with No Insulation ($r_2 = r_1 = 0.6$ m):**

$$Q_0 = \frac{4\pi (60)}{\dfrac{1}{(0.6)^{2} \times 8}} = \frac{753.98}{\dfrac{1}{2.88}} = 753.98 \times 2.88 = 2171.4 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Heat Loss with 50 mm Insulation ($r_2 = 0.650$ m):**

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (60)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{0.6} - \dfrac{1}{0.650}\right)\dfrac{1}{0.05} + \dfrac{1}{(0.650)^{2} \times 8}}$$

Compute:
- $1/0.650 = 1.5385$
- Difference: $1.6667 - 1.5385 = 0.1282$
- Conduction term: $0.1282 / 0.05 = 2.564$
- Convection term: $1 / (0.4225 \times 8) = 1/3.38 = 0.2959$
- Sum: $2.564 + 0.2959 = 2.860$

$$Q = \frac{753.98}{2.860} = 263.6 \text{ W}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Case | $r_2$ (m) | Q (W) | Reduction |
|------|-----------|-------|-----------|
| No insulation | 0.600 | 2171.4 | — |
| At $r_c$ | 0.0125 | 0.319 | (impractical) |
| 50 mm insulation | 0.650 | 263.6 | **87.9% reduction** |

> **Discussion:** Since $r_1 = 0.6$ m $\gg r_c = 0.0125$ m, the tank is always in the *normal regime*. The 50 mm insulation reduces heat loss by ~88%, which is **highly effective**. No paradox is observed.

*(7 marks — 2 for $r_c$, 1.5 for $Q_{max}$, 1.5 for $Q_0$, 2 for Q with 50 mm and discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Critical-Thickness Problems**
> 1. **Sign of the log term:** When $r_2 < r_1$ (which only happens at $r_2 = r_c$ here), $\ln(r_2/r_1) < 0$. Keep the sign in the numerator-fraction, then take absolute value for the *resistance* sum. Many students wrongly write $\ln(r_c/r_1) = +\vert\cdot\vert$ and get wrong Q.
> 2. **Forget to convert mm → m:** This is the single largest mark-loss cause in KTU valuation. Every dimension must be in **metres** before substitution.
> 3. **Plane wall vs cylinder confusion:** If $r_1$ is not given, or geometry says "slab" or "plate" → use plane wall formula. KTU frequently tests this distinction.
> 4. **Sphere vs cylinder factor:** Sphere has $r_c = 2k/h$, NOT $k/h$. The factor 2 is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 5. **Always report units:** k in W/m·K, h in W/m²·K, r in m, Q in W or W/m. Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark in valuation.
> 6. **No-insulation special case:** When $r_2 = r_1$, the conduction term becomes $\ln(1)/k = 0$, so only convection exists. Many students write a non-zero value here and lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Critical Thickness of Insulation**

- ✅ **Critical radius (cylinder):** $r_c = k/h$ — no factor.
- ✅ **Critical radius (sphere):** $r_c = 2k/h$ — factor of 2.
- ✅ **Plane wall:** No critical radius exists; Q always decreases with L.
- ✅ **Paradox:** For $r_2 < r_c$, **adding insulation INCREASES heat loss**.
- ✅ **Maximum Q** occurs at $r_2 = r_c$, not minimum Q.
- ✅ **True minimum Q** occurs at the largest practical $r_2$ (i.e., thickest insulation).
- ✅ **$r_c$ depends only on k and h** — not on $r_1$ or $\Delta T$.
- ✅ **Master the heat-transfer equation** for both cylinder and sphere (numerator constant, denominator = sum of conduction + convection resistances).
- ✅ **Units:** Convert all lengths to **metres** before substitution.
- ✅ **Small pipes (r₁ < r_c):** Insulation may **worsen** heat loss; choose carefully.
- ✅ **Large tanks (r₁ ≫ r_c):** Insulation always helps; standard design.
- ✅ **Memorize signs** when handling $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ — it is negative when $r_2 < r_1$.
- ✅ **KTU favourite numerical:** "Calculate $r_c$, Q at $r_c$, Q at given thickness, and comment" — 14 marks, 4 sub-parts.
- ✅ **Food processing applications:** steam pipes, retorts, chilled water lines, pasteurizers, sterilizer surfaces.
- ✅ **Common values:** k (insulation) = 0.02 – 0.10 W/m·K; h (air, free) = 5 – 25 W/m²·K; so $r_c$ is typically 1 – 20 mm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics and Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics and Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Heat Transfer?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Heat transfer is the branch of engineering science which deals with the rate of transfer of thermal energy (heat) between physical systems due to a temperature difference. In food processing, it refers to the science of predicting and controlling the exchange of thermal energy during unit operations such as pasteurization, sterilization, drying, baking, freezing, and evaporation.*

**Heat transfer** is fundamentally driven by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — thermal energy spontaneously flows from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature, never the reverse (without external work input).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍲

Imagine a hot bowl of **payasam** (a thick South Indian dessert) placed on a steel countertop. Within a few minutes:
- The bowl **cools down** (heat leaves the payasam).
- The countertop near the bowl becomes **slightly warm** (heat enters the countertop).

This exchange of thermal energy is **heat transfer**. The driving force is the **temperature difference (ΔT)** between the two bodies. The "speed" at which this energy moves depends on the material properties, the surface area, and the medium between them.

In food processing, controlling this rate is critical — too slow a heat transfer causes spoilage during processing; too rapid a heat transfer can scorch the product surface.

---

## 1.2 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer

Heat transfer occurs through **three distinct mechanisms**. Every food processing operation involves one or more of these modes simultaneously.

### Mode 1: Conduction 🔬

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conduction** is the transfer of heat through a material **without any bulk movement of the material itself**. Energy is transferred via molecular collisions, lattice vibrations (in solids), or electron drift. It is governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**.

**Real-world food example:** Heat traveling through the steel wall of a milk pasteurizer, or through the thickness of a frozen food slab during thawing.

### Mode 2: Convection 🌊

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convection** is the transfer of heat between a surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) due to the combined effects of **conduction within the fluid** and **bulk motion (advection) of the fluid**. It is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**.

**Real-world food example:** Hot air blowing over biscuits on a conveyor belt in an industrial oven, or steam condensing on the outer surface of a tomato sauce heat exchanger.

**Convection is of two types:**
- **Forced Convection:** Fluid motion is caused by an external agent (pump, fan, agitator). Example: Pumped milk through a plate heat exchanger.
- **Natural (Free) Convection:** Fluid motion is caused by **buoyancy forces** arising from density differences due to temperature gradients. Example: Warm air rising from a freshly baked loaf of bread.

### Mode 3: Radiation ☀️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Radiation** is the transfer of heat in the form of **electromagnetic waves (photons)** that requires **no medium** — it can occur through a perfect vacuum. All bodies above absolute zero emit radiation. It is governed by the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**.

**Real-world food example:** A pizza baked in a wood-fired oven receives a large portion of its energy via radiation from the hot oven walls and flames. Microwave ovens also use electromagnetic radiation (at 2.45 GHz) to excite water molecules inside food.

---

## 1.3 Key Driving Force and Transport Property

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental driving force for all three modes of heat transfer is the **Temperature Difference (ΔT)**, measured in **Kelvin (K)** or equivalently in **°C** (since it is a *difference*).
>
> The fundamental transport property governing the rate is the **Thermal Conductivity (k)**, measured in **W/(m·K)**.

| Property | Conduction | Convection | Radiation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Driving force | Temperature gradient (dT/dx) | Temperature difference (ΔT) | Temperature difference (T₁⁴ − T₂⁴) |
| Transport property | Thermal conductivity, **k** [W/m·K] | Heat transfer coefficient, **h** [W/m²·K] | Emissivity, **ε** (dimensionless) |
| Medium required? | Yes (material) | Yes (fluid) | **No** (can occur in vacuum) |

---

## 1.4 Units and Dimensions in Heat Transfer (KTU Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SI unit of heat transfer rate is the **Watt (W)** = **J/s** = **J·s⁻¹**.
> The dimensional formula is **[M·L²·T⁻³]**.

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Dimensional Formula |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Heat transfer rate | Q | W (J/s) | M·L²·T⁻³ |
| Heat flux | q | W/m² | M·T⁻³ |
| Thermal conductivity | k | W/(m·K) | M·L·T⁻³·K⁻¹ |
| Heat transfer coefficient | h | W/(m²·K) | M·T⁻³·K⁻¹ |
| Temperature | T | K (or °C) | K |
| Thermal diffusivity | α | m²/s | L²·T⁻¹ |
| Specific heat capacity | cₚ | J/(kg·K) | L²·T⁻²·K⁻¹ |

---

## 1.5 Why Study "Basics and Concepts" in Food Processing?

Every food preservation method is a heat transfer application:
- **Pasteurization & Sterilization:** Conduction + convection through packaging.
- **Drying:** Convection (hot air) + internal moisture diffusion.
- **Baking & Roasting:** Radiation from oven walls + conduction into food.
- **Freezing & Chilling:** Conduction through food, convection from refrigerant.
- **Evaporation & Concentration:** Conduction through heating surface, convection of vapor.

Understanding the basic laws is therefore the **foundation** for designing any food processing equipment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (The Heart of Conduction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fourier's Law (1822):** The rate of heat transfer by conduction through a homogeneous material is proportional to the **negative temperature gradient** and the **cross-sectional area perpendicular** to the direction of heat flow.

### The Vector Form (Rigorous)

$$
\vec{q} = -k \, \nabla T
$$

Where:
- $\vec{q}$ = heat flux vector [W/m²]
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material [W/(m·K)]
- $\nabla T$ = temperature gradient vector [K/m]
- The **negative sign** indicates heat flows in the direction of **decreasing** temperature (Second Law compliance).

### The Scalar Form for 1-D Steady Conduction

For a plane wall of thickness $L$ with surfaces at temperatures $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold):

$$
Q = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

Where $Q$ is the heat transfer rate in **Watts** and $A$ is the cross-sectional area in **m²**.

### Why "negative sign" matters (Conceptual "Why"):

Heat, by nature, "runs downhill" from hot to cold. Mathematically, the temperature profile $T(x)$ decreases as $x$ increases (in the direction of heat flow), so $\frac{dT}{dx} < 0$. The negative sign in Fourier's law flips this to make $q > 0$, indicating heat flowing in the positive $x$ direction. This is a **convention** to keep fluxes positive in the direction of energy flow.

---

## 2.2 Newton's Law of Cooling (The Heart of Convection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Cooling (1701):** The rate of convective heat transfer between a surface and a fluid is proportional to the **temperature difference** between the surface and the bulk fluid, and the **surface area**.

$$
Q = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)
$$

Where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient [W/(m²·K)]
- $T_s$ = surface temperature [K]
- $T_\infty$ = bulk fluid temperature [K]
- $A$ = surface area in contact [m²]

### The "Why" behind $h$:

The coefficient $h$ is **not a property of the fluid alone** — it depends on the **flow regime** (laminar/turbulent), **geometry** (flat plate, tube, sphere), **fluid properties** (density, viscosity, k, cₚ), and **velocity**. It is determined experimentally or by empirical correlations involving **dimensionless numbers** (Nusselt, Reynolds, Prandtl).

### Typical Order of Magnitude of $h$ in Food Operations:

| Situation | Approximate $h$ [W/m²·K] |
| :--- | :---: |
| Natural convection in air | 5 – 25 |
| Forced convection in air (e.g., oven) | 10 – 200 |
| Natural convection in water | 100 – 1,000 |
| Forced convection in water | 500 – 20,000 |
| Condensing steam | 5,000 – 100,000 |
| Boiling water | 2,500 – 100,000 |

---

## 2.3 Stefan–Boltzmann Law (The Heart of Radiation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stefan–Boltzmann Law (1879, 1884):** A **black body** (perfect emitter/absorber) emits thermal radiation at a rate proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute surface temperature**.

$$
Q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^4
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of the surface (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1) — dimensionless
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan–Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- $A$ = surface area [m²]
- $T$ = absolute temperature [K]

### Net Radiation Heat Exchange Between Two Surfaces:

$$
Q_{12} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)
$$

### Why "fourth power" (Conceptual "How"):

Unlike conduction and convection, radiation energy depends on the **total thermal agitation of surface molecules**. Since energy is proportional to $k_BT$ per degree of freedom and the number of electromagnetic modes is itself proportional to $T^3$, the integral yields a $T^4$ dependence. This is why doubling the absolute temperature of an oven wall increases radiated heat by a factor of **16** (2⁴) — a critical insight for baking and roasting operations.

---

## 2.4 Combined Modes — The Concept of "Overall Heat Transfer"

In real food processing equipment, all three modes act **simultaneously**. For example, heat entering a sealed retort pouch travels by:
1. **Convection** from steam to the pouch outer surface (governed by $h_o$).
2. **Conduction** through the polymer film of the pouch (governed by $k_f$).
3. **Conduction** through the food mass (governed by $k_{food}$).
4. **Convection** at the food surface inside the pouch (governed by $h_i$).

This series of resistances is combined into a single **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**:

$$
\frac{1}{U \, A} = \frac{1}{h_o \, A} + \frac{L_f}{k_f \, A} + \frac{L_{food}}{k_{food} \, A} + \frac{1}{h_i \, A}
$$

For a constant cross-sectional area $A$ (e.g., a flat pouch wall):

$$
Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{overall}
$$

This is the **electrical resistance analogy** — thermal resistances add in series, just like electrical resistors.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — it is the **minimum core** required for Module 2 numericals.

| Law / Concept | Governing Equation | Key Variable | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Fourier's Law (1-D, plane wall) | $Q = \dfrac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | $k$ | W/(m·K) |
| Fourier's Law (cylindrical) | $Q = \dfrac{2 \, \pi \, k \, L \, (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $k$, $L$ | W/(m·K), m |
| Fourier's Law (spherical) | $Q = \dfrac{4 \, \pi \, k \, (T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$ | $k$ | W/(m·K) |
| Newton's Law of Cooling | $Q = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$ | W/(m²·K) |
| Stefan–Boltzmann Law | $Q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^4$ | $\varepsilon$, $\sigma$ | —, W/(m²·K⁴) |
| Net radiation exchange | $Q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | $\varepsilon$ | — |
| Overall heat transfer (series) | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_i}$ | $U$ | W/(m²·K) |
| Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$ | $\alpha$ | m²/s |
| Heat flux | $q'' = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $q''$ | W/m² |
| Thermal resistance (conduction) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{k \, A}$ | $R$ | K/W |
| Thermal resistance (convection) | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{h \, A}$ | $R$ | K/W |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The three basic laws are not academic — they directly impact:
- **Process design:** Sizing a heat exchanger for milk pasteurization.
- **Energy auditing:** Calculating heat losses from a cold storage warehouse.
- **Process control:** Predicting the time required to chill a meat carcass from 38°C to 4°C (the "cooling time" problem).
- **Food safety:** Determining the coldest point in a retort pouch during sterilization (where *Clostridium botulinum* spores are most heat-resistant).
- **Equipment selection:** Choosing between a scraped-surface and tubular heat exchanger based on $h$ values for viscous food slurries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Flow Through a Plane Wall (From Fourier's Law)

### Problem Setup

Consider a homogeneous plane wall of thickness $L$ [m], thermal conductivity $k$ [W/(m·K)], and cross-sectional area $A$ [m²]. The two faces are maintained at uniform temperatures $T_1$ [K] (left, hot) and $T_2$ [K] (right, cold). Steady state, 1-D heat flow, constant $k$.

### Step 1: Start with Fourier's Law (Differential Form)

Fourier's law in 1-D, for heat flux in the $x$-direction:

$$
q_x = -k \, \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

> **Logic:** By definition, heat flux is the rate of heat crossing unit area. Fourier postulated it is proportional to the local temperature gradient.

### Step 2: Identify Steady State (No Heat Accumulation)

In steady state, the heat flux $q_x$ must be **constant** throughout the wall — what enters one face must leave the other (energy conservation). Therefore:

$$
\frac{dq_x}{dx} = 0
$$

### Step 3: Integrate the Differential Equation

Since $k$ is constant, separating variables:

$$
q_x \, dx = -k \, dT
$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $T = T_1$) to $x = L$ (where $T = T_2$):

$$
\int_{0}^{L} q_x \, dx = -k \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT
$$

$$
q_x \, (L - 0) = -k \, (T_2 - T_1)
$$

$$
q_x \, L = k \, (T_1 - T_2)
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Heat Flux

$$
q_x = \frac{k \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

### Step 5: Convert Heat Flux to Total Heat Transfer Rate

Multiplying both sides by the area $A$:

$$
Q = q_x \cdot A = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

> **Final form of the plane wall heat conduction equation.**

> **Physical interpretation:** $Q$ is **directly proportional** to $k$, $A$, and $\Delta T$; **inversely proportional** to $L$. Doubling wall thickness halves the heat flow — the principle behind insulation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Flow Through a Cylindrical Wall (Hollow Pipe)

### Problem Setup

A long hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, thermal conductivity $k$, and length $L$. Inner surface at $T_1$ (hot fluid), outer surface at $T_2$ (cold ambient). Steady state, 1-D radial conduction.

### Step 1: Apply Fourier's Law in Cylindrical Coordinates

For radial heat flow, the area perpendicular to heat flow is $A = 2 \, \pi \, r \, L$ (the lateral surface of a cylinder of radius $r$):

$$
Q = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dr} = -k \, (2 \, \pi \, r \, L) \, \frac{dT}{dr}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Steady State (Q is constant)

In steady state, $Q$ does not depend on $r$:

$$
\frac{Q}{2 \, \pi \, k \, L} = -r \, \frac{dT}{dr}
$$

### Step 3: Separate Variables

$$
\frac{Q}{2 \, \pi \, k \, L} \, \frac{dr}{r} = -dT
$$

### Step 4: Integrate Between Limits

Integrating from $r_1$ to $r_2$, with $T$ going from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$
\frac{Q}{2 \, \pi \, k \, L} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT
$$

$$
\frac{Q}{2 \, \pi \, k \, L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -(T_2 - T_1) = (T_1 - T_2)
$$

### Step 5: Solve for Q

$$
Q = \frac{2 \, \pi \, k \, L \, (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}
$$

> **Final form for cylindrical wall heat conduction.** Note the $\ln$ term — this is a direct consequence of area varying with $r$.

### Step 6: Express via Log Mean Radius (Optional Alternative Form)

Sometimes written using the **log-mean radius** $r_{lm} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$:

$$
Q = \frac{2 \, \pi \, k \, L \, (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} = \frac{k \, (2 \, \pi \, r_{lm} \, L) \, (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}
$$

This resembles the plane wall formula but uses a mean area evaluated at $r_{lm}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrical Resistance Analogy (Network Concept)

### Problem Setup

A composite wall consists of three layers of thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3$ and thermal conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$. Both sides exposed to fluids with convective coefficients $h_o$ and $h_i$. Find the overall resistance and overall heat transfer coefficient $U$.

### Step 1: Express Heat Flow in Each Resistive Element

For 1-D steady flow, the same $Q$ passes through all elements in series:

- Convective resistance (outer): $\Delta T_o = Q \cdot R_o$, where $R_o = \dfrac{1}{h_o \, A}$
- Conductive resistance (layer 1): $\Delta T_1 = Q \cdot R_1$, where $R_1 = \dfrac{L_1}{k_1 \, A}$
- Conductive resistance (layer 2): $\Delta T_2 = Q \cdot R_2$, where $R_2 = \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 \, A}$
- Conductive resistance (layer 3): $\Delta T_3 = Q \cdot R_3$, where $R_3 = \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 \, A}$
- Convective resistance (inner): $\Delta T_i = Q \cdot R_i$, where $R_i = \dfrac{1}{h_i \, A}$

### Step 2: Sum the Temperature Drops (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law Equivalent)

$$
\Delta T_{overall} = \Delta T_o + \Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2 + \Delta T_3 + \Delta T_i
$$

$$
T_{\infty, hot} - T_{\infty, cold} = Q \left( R_o + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_i \right)
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Q

$$
Q = \frac{T_{\infty, hot} - T_{\infty, cold}}{R_o + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_i}
$$

### Step 4: Define the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient

By definition, $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{overall}$, so:

$$
\frac{1}{U \, A} = R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_o \, A} + \frac{L_1}{k_1 \, A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 \, A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 \, A} + \frac{1}{h_i \, A}
$$

For constant $A$:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_i}
$$

> **The resistances add in series, just like electrical resistors.** The dominant resistance (largest term) controls the overall heat transfer rate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Determining the Controlling Resistance

In food equipment design, it is critical to identify which resistance **dominates**, because that is where engineers should focus improvement efforts.

| Scenario | Largest Resistance | Engineering Action |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Thin metal wall, gases on both sides | Convective ($1/h$) | Increase $h$ by adding fins, increasing velocity |
| Thick insulation, fluids on both sides | Conductive ($L/k$) of insulation | Use a material with lower $k$, or increase $L$ (up to critical radius limit) |
| Retort pouch heating (polymer + food) | Conduction in food ($L/k_{food}$) | Reduce food thickness, stir, or use agitated retort |
| Stainless steel pipe wall | Convective (steam or food) | $R$ of metal itself is negligible; focus on $h$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Steady Conduction Through a Single-Layer Wall

**Problem:** A flat stainless-steel wall of a milk pasteurizer has $k = 16 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$, thickness $L = 0.012 \, \text{m}$, area $A = 0.5 \, \text{m}^2$. The inner surface is at $T_1 = 95^\circ\text{C}$ and the outer at $T_2 = 88^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the heat loss rate.

**Step 1:** Identify the equation.

$$
Q = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

**Step 2:** Plug in values.

$$
Q = \frac{16 \times 0.5 \times (95 - 88)}{0.012}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
Q = \frac{8 \times 7}{0.012} = \frac{56}{0.012} = 4666.67 \, \text{W}
$$

> **Answer: Q ≈ 4.67 kW** — a meaningful heat loss to be recovered or insulated against.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Three Modes of Heat Transfer

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of heat transfer modes as applied in a generic food processing operation (e.g., retort sterilization of a sealed food pouch).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    heatSource["HOT SOURCE STEAM OR HEATING MEDIUM"]
    convectionOuter["CONVECTION OUTER SURFACE h_o"]
    conductionPouch["CONDUCTION POUCH FILM k_f"]
    conductionFood["CONDUCTION FOOD MASS k_food"]
    convectionInner["CONVECTION INSIDE POUCH h_i"]
    coldSource["COLD SINK FOOD CORE"]
    finalTemp["FINAL UNIFORM PRODUCT TEMPERATURE"]

    heatSource --> convectionOuter
    convectionOuter --> conductionPouch
    conductionPouch --> conductionFood
    conductionFood --> convectionInner
    convectionInner --> coldSource
    coldSource --> finalTemp
```

**Reading the diagram:** Heat flows in a single chain from the heating medium (left) to the food core (right), encountering **5 thermal resistances in series**. The slowest step (largest $R$) governs the entire rate.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Modes of Heat Transfer Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HEAT TRANSFER MODES"]

    A --> B["CONDUCTION"]
    A --> C["CONVECTION"]
    A --> D["RADIATION"]

    B --> B1["FOURIERS LAW"]
    B --> B2["NEEDS MEDIUM"]
    B --> B3["PLANE CYLINDER SPHERE GEOMETRIES"]

    C --> C1["NEWTONS LAW OF COOLING"]
    C --> C2["NEEDS FLUID MEDIUM"]
    C --> C3["FORCED VS NATURAL"]

    D --> D1["STEFAN BOLTZMANN LAW"]
    D --> D2["NEEDS NO MEDIUM"]
    D --> D3["ELECTROMAGNETIC WAVES"]
    D --> D4["EPSILON AND SIGMA DEPENDENT"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Thermal Resistance Network (Electrical Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Thot["T_infinity HOT"]
    R_conv1["R_o EQUALS 1 BY h_o A"]
    R_w1["R_wall1 EQUALS L_1 BY k_1 A"]
    R_w2["R_wall2 EQUALS L_2 BY k_2 A"]
    R_w3["R_wall3 EQUALS L_3 BY k_3 A"]
    R_conv2["R_i EQUALS 1 BY h_i A"]
    Tcold["T_infinity COLD"]

    Thot --> R_conv1
    R_conv1 --> R_w1
    R_w1 --> R_w2
    R_w2 --> R_w3
    R_w3 --> R_conv2
    R_conv2 --> Tcold
```

**Reading the network:** Each rectangle is a **thermal resistance** connected in **series**. The same heat $Q$ flows through all of them. The total resistance is the algebraic sum.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Heat Flux Direction (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["HOT BODY T_1"]
    S2["COLD BODY T_2"]
    FLOW["HEAT FLUX Q IS POSITIVE IN DIRECTION OF DECREASING T"]
    S1 --> FLOW
    FLOW --> S2
```

> **Why this matters:** The negative sign in Fourier's law ensures the heat flux vector always points in the direction of decreasing temperature. This is the **mathematical embodiment of the Second Law**.

---

## 4.5 Physical Schematic (Text-Based Engineering Drawing)

```
   STERILIZATION RETORT - HEAT FLOW PATH (SIDE VIEW)

   |========================|     <- Steam at 121 °C
   |    STEAM CHAMBER       |
   |========================|
            |  |   <-- Convection: h_o ≈ 5000 W/m²K
            |  |
   ________|__|________
  |                    |        <-- Pouch wall (polymer)
  |   FOOD PRODUCT     |            k ≈ 0.2 W/mK, L = 0.1 mm
  |   (CONDUCTION)     |        <-- Conduction + Convection
  |                    |            k_food ≈ 0.5 W/mK
  |____________________|
            |  |   <-- Center is COLDEST point
   ________|__|________         (Design criterion: lethality F₀)
  |                    |
  |  STERILE PRODUCT   |
  |____________________|
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: Define the three modes of heat transfer. State the governing law for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Heat transfer in food processing occurs by three mechanisms:

1. **Conduction:** Transfer of heat through a material without bulk movement of the medium. Governed by **Fourier's Law**, $Q = \dfrac{k \, A \, \Delta T}{L}$.

2. **Convection:** Transfer of heat between a surface and a moving fluid. Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**, $Q = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$. It is further classified as **forced** (external pumping) or **natural** (buoyancy-driven).

3. **Radiation:** Transfer of heat via electromagnetic waves, requiring no medium. Governed by the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**, $Q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4\text{)}$.

> **Conduction needs a solid/fluid medium; convection needs a fluid; radiation needs nothing.**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: Define thermal conductivity. What is its SI unit? List the approximate range of $k$ for food materials.**

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal conductivity ($k$)** is defined as the rate of heat transfer by conduction through a unit thickness of a material per unit area per unit temperature difference. It quantifies a material's ability to conduct heat.

**SI unit:** $\text{W/(m·K)}$

**Typical range for food materials:**
- High moisture foods (e.g., fruits, milk): $k \approx 0.5$ to $0.7 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$
- Frozen foods: $k \approx 1.0$ to $2.5 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$ (higher due to ice content)
- Dry granular foods (e.g., flour): $k \approx 0.1$ to $0.2 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$
- Fats and oils: $k \approx 0.15$ to $0.20 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b), each 7 marks. **Internal choice** between two complete questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** A flat stainless-steel wall of a steam-heated food processing vessel has thermal conductivity $k = 16 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$, thickness $L = 0.015 \, \text{m}$, and area $A = 2.0 \, \text{m}^2$. The hot-side surface temperature is $100^\circ\text{C}$ and the cold-side surface temperature is $90^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate:
(i) The heat flux through the wall.
(ii) The total heat transfer rate.
(iii) The temperature at the mid-plane of the wall. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **electrical resistance analogy** for heat transfer through a composite wall. State the formula for overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ for a 3-layer composite wall with convective boundaries on both sides. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
$k = 16 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$, $L = 0.015 \, \text{m}$, $A = 2.0 \, \text{m}^2$, $T_1 = 100^\circ\text{C}$, $T_2 = 90^\circ\text{C}$.

**(i) Heat flux $q$:**

$$
q = \frac{k \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{16 \times (100 - 90)}{0.015} = \frac{16 \times 10}{0.015}
$$

$$
q = \frac{160}{0.015} = 10666.67 \, \text{W/m}^2
$$

> **$q \approx 10.67 \, \text{kW/m}^2$**

**(ii) Total heat transfer rate $Q$:**

$$
Q = q \cdot A = 10666.67 \times 2.0 = 21333.33 \, \text{W}
$$

> **$Q \approx 21.33 \, \text{kW}$**

**(iii) Mid-plane temperature $T_m$:**

In steady state, the temperature distribution is **linear** in a homogeneous wall:

$$
T(x) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L} \cdot x
$$

At $x = L/2 = 0.0075 \, \text{m}$:

$$
T_m = 100 - \frac{10}{0.015} \times 0.0075 = 100 - 666.67 \times 0.0075 = 100 - 5.0
$$

$$
T_m = 95^\circ\text{C}
$$

> **$T_m = 95^\circ\text{C}$** (the arithmetic mean, since the profile is linear).

---

#### Part (b) — Electrical Resistance Analogy Explanation

**The Concept:**
The flow of heat through a composite structure is **analogous** to the flow of electric current through a series of electrical resistances.

| Heat Transfer Quantity | Electrical Analogue |
| :--- | :--- |
| Heat flow rate $Q$ [W] | Current $I$ [A] |
| Temperature difference $\Delta T$ [K] | Voltage $V$ [V] |
| Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ [K/W] | Electrical resistance $R$ [Ω] |
| Ohm's Law: $V = I R$ | Fourier's Law: $\Delta T = Q R_{th}$ |

**Composite Wall Formula (3 layers + 2 convective boundaries):**

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_i}
$$

The total thermal resistance is the **sum of individual resistances** in series, just as electrical resistors in series add. The heat flow rate is then:

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{total}} = U \, A \, \Delta T_{overall}
$$

> **Key insight:** The layer with the **largest resistance dominates** the heat transfer — improving the other layers yields little benefit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** A cylindrical steel pipe of inner radius $r_1 = 0.05 \, \text{m}$, outer radius $r_2 = 0.07 \, \text{m}$, thermal conductivity $k = 45 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$, and length $L = 3.0 \, \text{m}$ carries hot oil. The inner surface temperature is $T_1 = 150^\circ\text{C}$ and the outer surface is $T_2 = 130^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the heat loss rate through the pipe wall. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**, explain the mechanism of radiation heat transfer. A pizza oven wall is at $T_1 = 523 \, \text{K}$ ($\varepsilon_1 = 0.85$) and the pizza surface is at $T_2 = 373 \, \text{K}$ ($\varepsilon_2 = 0.6$). Estimate the net radiation heat flux received by the pizza per unit area. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Heat Loss Through Cylindrical Pipe Wall

**Given:**
$r_1 = 0.05 \, \text{m}$, $r_2 = 0.07 \, \text{m}$, $k = 45 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$, $L = 3.0 \, \text{m}$, $T_1 = 150^\circ\text{C}$, $T_2 = 130^\circ\text{C}$.

**Formula for cylindrical wall:**

$$
Q = \frac{2 \, \pi \, k \, L \, (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}
$$

**Calculate the log term:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.07}{0.05}\right) = \ln(1.4) = 0.3365
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
Q = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 45 \times 3.0 \times (150 - 130)}{0.3365}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 45 \times 3.0 \times 20}{0.3365}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{16964.6}{0.3365} \approx 50415 \, \text{W}
$$

> **$Q \approx 50.4 \, \text{kW}$** of heat is lost radially from the 3-meter pipe section.

---

#### Part (b) — Radiation Heat Transfer to Pizza

**Governing Law:**

$$
Q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)
$$

For a unit area of pizza:

$$
q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)
$$

**Assumption:** Use the **effective emissivity** for two large parallel surfaces. For simplicity, we apply the given emissivity of the oven wall ($\varepsilon = 0.85$) as a representative effective factor (a more rigorous analysis would combine $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ via $\varepsilon_{eff}^{-1} = \varepsilon_1^{-1} + \varepsilon_2^{-1} - 1$; here we approximate).

**Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:**

$$
T_1^4 = (523)^4 = 7.485 \times 10^{10} \, \text{K}^4
$$

$$
T_2^4 = (373)^4 = 1.935 \times 10^{10} \, \text{K}^4
$$

$$
T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 7.485 \times 10^{10} - 1.935 \times 10^{10} = 5.550 \times 10^{10} \, \text{K}^4
$$

**Apply Stefan–Boltzmann:**

$$
q = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.550 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
q = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 55.50 = 0.85 \times 314.7
$$

$$
q \approx 267.5 \, \text{W/m}^2
$$

> **The pizza receives approximately 267 W/m² of radiant heat flux** — this is why baking times in radiant ovens are typically very short (minutes), and why the **fourth-power dependence** makes a 50 K temperature increase in the oven wall quadruple the radiant heat flux.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Module 2 Basics:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors in Stefan–Boltzmann:** Students often use temperature in °C instead of **Kelvin (K)**. Always convert: $T[\text{K}] = T[^\circ\text{C}] + 273.15$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in Fourier's Law:** The vector form $q = -k \nabla T$ has the negative sign for a *reason*. Marks are deducted for writing $q = k \, dT/dx$ without justification.
>
> 3. **Confusing Heat Flux and Heat Rate:** Heat flux $q$ is in **W/m²**, but heat rate $Q$ is in **W**. Mixing these units loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Cylinder vs. Plane Wall Formula:** Students apply the plane wall formula to a cylindrical geometry, which is wrong. Always check if $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ should be used.
>
> 5. **Resistances in Series — Order of Magnitude Check:** If a student reports $U > h_{max}$ in a composite problem, it is a **red flag** — the overall $U$ must be less than the smallest individual $h$ or $k/L$.
>
> 6. **Emissivity Values:** Always state $\varepsilon$ (dimensionless) and never write it with units. A common error is writing $\varepsilon = 0.85 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, which is meaningless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Module 2 — Basics and Concepts: Quick-Reference Revision Checklist**

- **Three Modes of Heat Transfer:** Conduction, Convection, Radiation — and their respective governing laws: **Fourier**, **Newton**, **Stefan–Boltzmann**.

- **Fourier's Law (1-D, plane wall):**
  $Q = \dfrac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ — heat flow proportional to $\Delta T$, inversely proportional to $L$.

- **Cylindrical Wall Formula:**
  $Q = \dfrac{2 \, \pi \, k \, L \, (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ — applies to pipes, tubes, insulated vessels.

- **Spherical Wall Formula:**
  $Q = \dfrac{4 \, \pi \, k \, (T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$ — applies to storage tanks, fruit models.

- **Newton's Law of Cooling:**
  $Q = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ — $h$ is not a property but a process-dependent coefficient.

- **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:**
  $Q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^4$ — uses **absolute temperature in Kelvin**; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4\text{)}$.

- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (Series Resistances):**
  $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_i}$ — Resistances **add** in series; $U$ is always **less than** the smallest individual $h$ or $k/L$.

- **Thermal Diffusivity:**
  $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$ — measures how quickly a material's temperature changes; important in transient food processing.

- **Temperature must always be in Kelvin** for radiation calculations; differences ($\Delta T$) may be in K or °C (numerically equivalent).

- **Order of magnitude of $h$:** $h_{air} \sim 10–100$, $h_{water} \sim 500–10{,}000$, $h_{condensing steam} \sim 50{,}000$ W/m²·K.

- **In steady state**, the temperature profile is **linear** for a plane wall and **logarithmic** for a cylindrical wall.

- **Food processing applications** to recall: pasteurizer heat transfer, retort sterilization, blast freezing, hot-air drying, baking — each one dominated by a different mode of heat transfer.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU Module 2 numericals, the **most commonly tested formula** is the **composite wall with series resistances**, followed by the **cylindrical pipe heat loss**. Master these two and you have covered 70% of the typical exam marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Convection: Free and Forced Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Convection: Free and Forced Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Convection

Convection is the mode of heat transfer that occurs **between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in motion**, or **within the fluid itself** due to the bulk motion of fluid molecules. It is governed fundamentally by **Newton's Law of Cooling**, expressed as:

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where **$q$** is the rate of heat transfer (W), **$h$** is the **convective heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K), **$A$** is the surface area (m²), and **$\Delta T$** is the temperature difference between the surface and the bulk fluid (K).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Convection is broadly classified into two fundamental categories based on the **origin of fluid motion**:
> 1. **Free (Natural) Convection** — fluid motion driven by buoyancy forces arising from density differences caused by temperature gradients within the fluid itself.
> 2. **Forced Convection** — fluid motion induced by an external mechanical agency (pump, fan, blower, agitator, or compressor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Free Convection Analogy — "The Self-Stirring Soup Pot":**
Imagine a pot of vegetable soup placed on a stove without stirring. The soup at the bottom, nearest the flame, gets hot and becomes less dense. The colder, denser soup above sinks down to take its place. This natural circulation — hot rising, cold sinking — happens **without anyone stirring**. The soup "stirs itself" purely because of temperature-induced buoyancy differences. This is **free convection**.

**Forced Convection Analogy — "The Blender in Action":**
Now take the same soup, but switch on a blender (or a hand mixer). The blades forcefully push the hot and cold regions of soup together, dramatically increasing the mixing rate. The temperature equalization now happens **much faster** because an external mechanical device is driving the fluid motion. This is **forced convection**.

## 1.3 The Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$)

| Type of Convection | Typical Range of $h$ (W/m²·K) |
|:------------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| Free convection of gases | **5 – 25** |
| Free convection of liquids | **10 – 500** |
| Forced convection of gases | **10 – 200** |
| Forced convection of liquids | **50 – 20,000** |
| Boiling/Condensation | **2,500 – 100,000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The convective heat transfer coefficient **$h$** is **NOT a property of the fluid**. It is an **engineering parameter** that depends on the geometry, fluid properties, flow velocity, and temperature difference. This is why $h$ is determined through empirical correlations (Nu-Re-Pr relationships) rather than tabulated as a material property.

## 1.4 Boundary Layer Concept (Foundation)

Before diving into types, every KTU 2024 student must visualize the **convective boundary layer** — a thin fluid region adjacent to the heated surface where:
- Velocity varies from **zero at the wall** (no-slip condition) to **free-stream value** ($u_\infty$).
- Temperature varies from **$T_s$ at the wall** to **$T_\infty$** in the free stream.
- Both **velocity boundary layer** ($\delta$) and **thermal boundary layer** ($\delta_t$) develop simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> **Food Processing Linkage:** In a plate heat exchanger used for milk pasteurization, the thermal boundary layer determines how quickly heat from the hot side reaches the cold milk. A thinner boundary layer (forced convection) = faster pasteurization = better product quality.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free (Natural) Convection — Theory

### Physical Origin
Free convection arises because of the **buoyancy force** that develops in a fluid when temperature non-uniformities create **density non-uniformities**. In the presence of gravity ($g$), a parcel of warmer (less dense) fluid experiences an upward buoyant force, while a colder (denser) parcel sinks.

### Driving Force
The driving force per unit volume is given by the **buoyancy force**:
$$F_b = \rho_\infty \cdot g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where:
- **$\rho_\infty$** = density of fluid far from surface (kg/m³)
- **$g$** = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- **$\beta$** = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (1/K)
- **$T_s$** = surface temperature (K)
- **$T_\infty$** = bulk fluid temperature (K)

### The Grashof Number ($Gr$)
The dimensionless number that quantifies the ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous forces is the **Grashof Number**:

$$Gr = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2}$$

where **$L$** is the characteristic length (m) and **$\nu$** is the kinematic viscosity (m²/s).

### The Prandtl Number ($Pr$)
The ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity:

$$Pr = \frac{\mu \cdot c_p}{k} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}$$

### The Rayleigh Number ($Ra$)
A combined dimensionless number used for free convection analysis:

$$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu \cdot \alpha}$$

### Free Convection Correlations

**For a Vertical Plate (KTU Essential Correlation):**

| Flow Regime | Rayleigh Range | Nusselt Number Correlation |
|:-----------:|:--------------:|:--------------------------:|
| Laminar | $10^4 < Ra < 10^9$ | $Nu = 0.59 \cdot Ra^{1/4}$ |
| Turbulent | $10^9 < Ra < 10^{13}$ | $Nu = 0.10 \cdot Ra^{1/3}$ |

where $Nu = \dfrac{hL}{k}$.

### Critical Rayleigh Number
The transition from laminar to turbulent free convection occurs at **$Ra_{cr} \approx 10^9$**. Below this value, flow is smooth and orderly; above, it becomes chaotic with eddy formation.

## 2.2 Forced Convection — Theory

### Physical Origin
In forced convection, fluid motion is **externally induced** by mechanical equipment such as fans, pumps, blowers, or agitators. The temperature gradients are present, but they are **not** responsible for the bulk motion.

### Driving Force
The driving force is the **pressure gradient** imposed by external pumping devices, characterized by a **free-stream velocity** $u_\infty$ (m/s).

### The Reynolds Number ($Re$)
The dimensionless number for forced convection that quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot u_\infty \cdot L}{\mu} = \frac{u_\infty \cdot L}{\nu}$$

### Critical Reynolds Number
- **Laminar flow:** $Re < 5 \times 10^5$ (for flow over a flat plate)
- **Turbulent flow:** $Re > 5 \times 10^5$

### Forced Convection Correlations

**For Flow Over a Flat Plate (KTU Essential Correlations):**

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Range | Nusselt Number Correlation |
|:-----------:|:--------------:|:--------------------------:|
| Laminar (entire plate) | $Re_L < 5 \times 10^5$ | $Nu_L = 0.664 \cdot Re_L^{1/2} \cdot Pr^{1/3}$ |
| Turbulent (entire plate) | $Re_L > 5 \times 10^5$ | $Nu_L = 0.037 \cdot Re_L^{4/5} \cdot Pr^{1/3}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Food Engineering Note:** In the turbulent correlation, the exponent on Prandtl number is **1/3** for the bulk of the plate, but the local Nusselt number at the leading edge has an exponent of **1/2**. This distinction is frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Comparison Framework: Free vs. Forced Convection

| Parameter | Free Convection | Forced Convection |
|:----------|:---------------:|:-----------------:|
| **Driving Force** | Buoyancy ($\Delta\rho \cdot g$) | External pressure gradient ($u_\infty$) |
| **Governing Dimensionless Group** | Grashof Number ($Gr$) | Reynolds Number ($Re$) |
| **Typical Velocity** | 0.01 – 1 m/s | 1 – 50 m/s |
| **Typical $h$ (air)** | 5 – 25 W/m²·K | 10 – 200 W/m²·K |
| **Typical $h$ (water)** | 50 – 500 W/m²·K | 500 – 20,000 W/m²·K |
| **Equipment Required** | None | Fans, pumps, blowers, agitators |
| **Energy Cost** | Zero (passive) | High (active) |
| **Control over $h$** | Limited | Highly controllable |
| **Transition Criterion** | $Ra_{cr} \approx 10^9$ | $Re_{cr} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ |
| **Food Processing Example** | Cooling of bread loaves on racks | Air blast freezing of fish fillets |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Processing Engineering

- **Free Convection:** Storage cooling of fruits in cold rooms (no fans), setting of gelatin desserts, natural cooling of freshly baked bread, cooling of canned foods in still retort sterilization.
- **Forced Convection:** Air blast freezers for meat and seafood, plate heat exchangers for milk pasteurization, scraped surface heat exchangers for ice cream mix, fluidized bed drying of grains, spray drying of milk powder, jacketed vessels with agitators for jam manufacture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient from Newton's Law of Cooling

### Step 1: Starting Point — Differential Heat Transfer
Consider a small elemental area $dA$ on a heated surface at temperature $T_s$, exposed to a fluid at temperature $T_\infty$. The instantaneous rate of heat transfer from this element is:

$$\delta q = h \cdot dA \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

> *Explanation:* This is Newton's Law of Cooling applied to a differential area. The coefficient $h$ is defined as the proportionality constant.

### Step 2: Integration Over the Surface Area
For a finite surface of area $A$ with potentially varying $T_s$ or $h$:

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= \int_A h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot dA
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Assume Uniform Conditions (Average $h$)
In most engineering design problems, $h$, $T_s$, and $T_\infty$ are assumed constant over the surface. The equation reduces to:

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Express Heat Flux (Heat Transfer Rate Per Unit Area)

$$\begin{aligned}
q'' &= \frac{q}{A} = h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

> *Interpretation:* The heat flux $q''$ (W/m²) is linear in the temperature driving force $\Delta T = (T_s - T_\infty)$, with $h$ as the slope. This is why KTU problem statements often give $h$ and ask you to compute $q$ or $\Delta T$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Grashof Number

### Step 1: Define the Buoyancy Force
The net upward buoyancy force on a fluid element of volume $V$ whose density differs from the surrounding by $\Delta\rho$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_b &= (\rho_\infty - \rho) \cdot g \cdot V
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Express $\Delta\rho$ in Terms of Temperature Using the Boussinesq Approximation
The Boussinesq approximation states that density variations are important **only in the buoyancy term** and can be linearized:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \rho_\infty \cdot [1 - \beta \cdot (T - T_\infty)] \\
\rho_\infty - \rho &= \rho_\infty \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Back Into the Buoyancy Force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \rho_\infty \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot g \cdot V
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Define Viscous Force
For a fluid element of characteristic length $L$, the viscous (drag) force scales as:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_v &= \mu \cdot \frac{u}{L} \cdot A = \mu \cdot \frac{u}{L^2} \cdot V
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Define Characteristic Velocity from Buoyancy–Viscous Balance
Equating $F_b \sim F_v$ and solving for $u$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_\infty \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot g \cdot L^3 &\sim \mu \cdot u \\
u &\sim \frac{\rho_\infty \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot g \cdot L^3}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Construct the Grashof Number as Buoyancy/Viscous Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
Gr &= \frac{F_b}{F_v} = \frac{\rho_\infty^2 \cdot g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^3}{\mu^2} = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^3}{\nu^2}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Engineering Insight:* A high Grashof number means buoyancy forces dominate — natural circulation is vigorous, leading to higher $h$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Reynolds Number

### Step 1: Identify the Two Competing Forces
- **Inertial force:** $F_i = \rho \cdot u^2 \cdot A$
- **Viscous force:** $F_v = \mu \cdot \dfrac{u}{L} \cdot A$

### Step 2: Form the Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{F_i}{F_v} &= \frac{\rho \cdot u^2 \cdot A}{\mu \cdot u/L \cdot A} = \frac{\rho \cdot u \cdot L}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Define Reynolds Number

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \cdot u \cdot L}{\mu} = \frac{u \cdot L}{\nu}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A vertical plate 0.5 m high is maintained at a surface temperature of $80^\circ$C and is exposed to atmospheric air at $20^\circ$C. The properties of air at the film temperature of $50^\circ$C are:
- $k = 0.0274$ W/m·K
- $\nu = 1.798 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $Pr = 0.7228$
- $\beta = 1/T_f = 1/323$ K$^{-1}$

**Calculate the heat transfer coefficient and the rate of heat transfer per unit width.**

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute the Grashof Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gr &= \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2} \\
&= \frac{9.81 \cdot (1/323) \cdot (80 - 20) \cdot (0.5)^3}{(1.798 \times 10^{-5})^2} \\
&= \frac{9.81 \cdot 0.003096 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.125}{3.233 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= \frac{0.2277}{3.233 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= 7.044 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Rayleigh Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
Ra &= Gr \cdot Pr \\
&= 7.044 \times 10^{8} \cdot 0.7228 \\
&= 5.092 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Identify Flow Regime**
Since $Ra = 5.092 \times 10^{8} < 10^9$, the flow is **laminar**. Apply the laminar correlation:

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu &= 0.59 \cdot Ra^{1/4} \\
&= 0.59 \cdot (5.092 \times 10^{8})^{0.25} \\
&= 0.59 \cdot 475.55 \\
&= 280.58
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate $h$ from Nusselt Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{Nu \cdot k}{L} \\
&= \frac{280.58 \cdot 0.0274}{0.5} \\
&= 15.38 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Width**

$$\begin{aligned}
q' &= h \cdot L \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \\
&= 15.38 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 60 \\
&= 461.4 \text{ W/m}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Final Answer Format:** Always present the final answer with **units**, indicate the **flow regime** (laminar/turbulent) before citing the correlation, and verify the dimensionless number falls within the valid range of the correlation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Free vs. Forced Convection

| Decision Criterion | Free Convection Favoured | Forced Convection Favoured |
|:-------------------|:------------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| **Desired heat transfer rate** | Low to moderate | High |
| **Equipment cost constraint** | Tight budget | Adequate capital |
| **Energy cost** | Preferable (passive) | Acceptable if rate is critical |
| **Product sensitivity** | Heat-sensitive (slow heating) | Need rapid processing |
| **Scale of operation** | Small / batch | Industrial / continuous |
| **Operating environment** | Sterile / clean-room (no moving parts) | Robust to contamination |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
