# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between free convection and forced convection. Give two examples of each from food processing operations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Free Convection | Forced Convection |
|:----------|:---------------:|:-----------------:|
| **Driving Mechanism** | Buoyancy forces due to density differences caused by temperature gradients | External mechanical agency (fan, pump, blower) |
| **Fluid Velocity** | Low and self-induced | High and externally controlled |
| **Governing Dimensionless Number** | Grashof Number ($Gr$) | Reynolds Number ($Re$) |
| **Heat Transfer Coefficient $h$** | Relatively low (5–25 W/m²·K for air) | Relatively high (10–200 W/m²·K for air) |
| **Energy Input** | None (passive) | Substantial (active) |

**Food Processing Examples:**
- **Free Convection:** (i) Cooling of bread loaves on open racks in a bakery; (ii) Natural cooling of hot jam in open pans during gel formation.
- **Forced Convection:** (i) Air blast freezing of fish fillets in a cold store; (ii) Plate heat exchanger used for pasteurization of milk.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define Nusselt, Reynolds, and Prandtl numbers and state their physical significance in convective heat transfer.**

**Model Answer:**

**Nusselt Number ($Nu$):** 
$$Nu = \frac{hL}{k}$$
**Significance:** Represents the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer at the boundary. It indicates the enhancement of heat transfer due to fluid motion. A higher $Nu$ means convection dominates over pure conduction.

**Reynolds Number ($Re$):** 
$$Re = \frac{\rho u L}{\mu}$$
**Significance:** Represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in the fluid. It determines whether the flow is laminar ($Re < 5 \times 10^5$ for flat plate) or turbulent ($Re > 5 \times 10^5$).

**Prandtl Number ($Pr$):** 
$$Pr = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}$$
**Significance:** Represents the ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity. It indicates the relative thickness of velocity and thermal boundary layers:
- If $Pr > 1$: velocity boundary layer is thicker than thermal boundary layer.
- If $Pr < 1$: thermal boundary layer is thicker than velocity boundary layer.

For gases, $Pr \approx 0.7$; for water, $Pr \approx 7$; for viscous oils, $Pr \gg 100$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the flow regime** before applying a correlation (laminar vs. turbulent) — loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $T$ in °C instead of K** in the Grashof/Rayleigh formula — loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Reynolds and Grashof numbers** — one is for forced, the other for free convection. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this mix-up.
> 4. **Not verifying the Rayleigh number is within the valid range** of the cited correlation — loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer** — partial mark deduction (0.5–1 mark).
> 6. **Misapplying the Boussinesq approximation** by using density values directly instead of $\beta$ — loses 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Grashof Number** starting from the ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical hot plate at $90^\circ$C is suspended in ambient air at $25^\circ$C. The plate height is 0.4 m. Properties of air at film temperature $T_f = 57.5^\circ$C = $330.5$ K are:
- $k = 0.0281$ W/m·K
- $\nu = 1.892 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $Pr = 0.72$
- $\beta = 1/T_f = 1/330.5$ K$^{-1}$

Calculate the **heat transfer coefficient** using the appropriate free convection correlation, and the **rate of heat transfer per unit width** of the plate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Buoyancy Force Definition**
The net upward buoyancy force on a fluid element of volume $V$ whose temperature differs from the bulk by $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$:

$$F_b = (\rho_\infty - \rho) \cdot g \cdot V$$

**Step 2: Boussinesq Approximation**
$\rho_\infty - \rho = \rho_\infty \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$, hence:

$$F_b = \rho_\infty \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot g \cdot V$$

**Step 3: Viscous Force Scaling**
For characteristic length $L$:

$$F_v \sim \mu \cdot \frac{u}{L} \cdot A = \frac{\mu \cdot u \cdot V}{L^2}$$

**Step 4: Take the Ratio**

$$Gr = \frac{F_b}{F_v} = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2}$$

**Physical Significance of Each Term:**
- **$g$** — Gravitational acceleration, the source of buoyancy.
- **$\beta$** — Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient; quantifies density change per unit temperature change.
- **$(T_s - T_\infty)$** — Temperature driving force; higher $\Delta T$ produces stronger buoyancy.
- **$L^3$** — Volume scaling; larger surfaces induce stronger natural circulation.
- **$\nu^2$** — Viscous resistance; viscous fluids suppress natural circulation.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute Grashof Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gr &= \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \cdot L^3}{\nu^2} \\
&= \frac{9.81 \cdot (1/330.5) \cdot (90 - 25) \cdot (0.4)^3}{(1.892 \times 10^{-5})^2} \\
&= \frac{9.81 \cdot 0.003025 \cdot 65 \cdot 0.064}{3.579 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= \frac{0.1233}{3.579 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= 3.445 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute Rayleigh Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
Ra &= Gr \cdot Pr \\
&= 3.445 \times 10^{8} \cdot 0.72 \\
&= 2.481 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Identify Flow Regime**
Since $Ra = 2.481 \times 10^{8} < 10^{9}$, flow is **laminar**. Use $Nu = 0.59 \cdot Ra^{1/4}$.

**Step 4: Compute Nusselt Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu &= 0.59 \cdot (2.481 \times 10^{8})^{0.25} \\
&= 0.59 \cdot 396.96 \\
&= 234.21
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute $h$**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{Nu \cdot k}{L} = \frac{234.21 \cdot 0.0281}{0.4} = 16.45 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Compute Heat Transfer per Unit Width**

$$\begin{aligned}
q' &= h \cdot L \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \\
&= 16.45 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 65 \\
&= 427.7 \text{ W/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $h = 16.45$ W/m²·K; $q' = 427.7$ W/m.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Alternative Model]

**(a)** Explain the differences between **free and forced convection** with suitable sketches of velocity and thermal boundary layers. State and explain the governing dimensionless numbers for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air at $30^\circ$C flows with a velocity of 5 m/s over a flat plate maintained at $100^\circ$C. The plate is 1 m long. Properties of air at film temperature $T_f = 65^\circ$C = $338$ K are:
- $k = 0.0305$ W/m·K
- $\nu = 1.918 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $Pr = 0.70$

Calculate the **average heat transfer coefficient** over the plate and the **total heat transfer rate** if the plate width is 1 m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Differences between Free and Forced Convection:**

| Aspect | Free Convection | Forced Convection |
|:-------|:---------------:|:-----------------:|
| **Driving Force** | Buoyancy due to $\Delta\rho$ | External pump or fan |
| **Velocity Source** | Self-induced | Imposed externally |
| **Velocity Boundary Layer** | Symmetric, develops slowly | Asymmetric, develops faster |
| **Thermal Boundary Layer** | Driven by buoyancy-modified flow | Driven by external shear |
| **Governing Number** | $Gr$ or $Ra$ | $Re$ |
| **Key Parameter** | $\Delta T$ | $u_\infty$ |
| **Energy Cost** | Zero | High |

**Governing Dimensionless Numbers:**

**For Free Convection — Grashof Number:**
$$Gr = \frac{g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^3}{\nu^2}$$

This number represents the ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces. When $Gr \gg 1$, buoyancy dominates and natural circulation is strong.

**For Forced Convection — Reynolds Number:**
$$Re = \frac{u_\infty \cdot L}{\nu}$$

This number represents the ratio of inertial to viscous forces. When $Re \gg 1$, inertial effects dominate and the flow becomes turbulent.

**Boundary Layer Sketch Description:**
In **free convection**, the velocity profile is asymmetric — fluid near the hot surface rises while fluid far away is stationary. In **forced convection**, the velocity profile develops from zero at the wall to $u_\infty$ at the boundary layer edge, with the classic Blasius profile shape in laminar flow.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute Reynolds Number at Plate End**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_L &= \frac{u_\infty \cdot L}{\nu} = \frac{5 \cdot 1}{1.918 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.607 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Identify Flow Regime**
Since $Re_L = 2.607 \times 10^5 < 5 \times 10^5$, the boundary layer is **entirely laminar** over the plate length. Apply the laminar correlation:

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu_L &= 0.664 \cdot Re_L^{1/2} \cdot Pr^{1/3} \\
&= 0.664 \cdot (2.607 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \cdot (0.70)^{1/3} \\
&= 0.664 \cdot 510.59 \cdot 0.8879 \\
&= 301.01
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute $h$**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{Nu_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{301.01 \cdot 0.0305}{1} = 9.18 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute Total Heat Transfer**

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) \\
&= 9.18 \cdot (1 \times 1) \cdot (100 - 30) \\
&= 9.18 \cdot 70 \\
&= 642.6 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $h = 9.18$ W/m²·K; $q = 642.6$ W.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Convection** = heat transfer involving bulk fluid motion; governed by **$q = hA\Delta T$**.
- **Free (Natural) Convection:** motion caused by **buoyancy**; governed by **Grashof number** ($Gr$) or **Rayleigh number** ($Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$).
- **Forced Convection:** motion caused by **external agency**; governed by **Reynolds number** ($Re$).
- **Critical Transition Values:**
  - Free convection: laminar for $Ra < 10^9$, turbulent for $Ra > 10^9$.
  - Forced convection (flat plate): laminar for $Re < 5 \times 10^5$, turbulent for $Re > 5 \times 10^5$.
- **Essential Correlations (must memorize):**
  - Free convection, laminar, vertical plate: $Nu = 0.59 \cdot Ra^{1/4}$.
  - Free convection, turbulent, vertical plate: $Nu = 0.10 \cdot Ra^{1/3}$.
  - Forced convection, laminar, flat plate: $Nu_L = 0.664 \cdot Re_L^{1/2} \cdot Pr^{1/3}$.
  - Forced convection, turbulent, flat plate: $Nu_L = 0.037 \cdot Re_L^{4/5} \cdot Pr^{1/3}$.
- **Boussinesq Approximation:** density variations are important **only in the buoyancy term**; all other fluid properties evaluated at film temperature $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$.
- **Volumetric Thermal Expansion Coefficient:** $\beta = 1/T$ (ideal gas) in **absolute Kelvin** — never Celsius.
- **Heat Transfer Coefficient Ranges (must remember):**
  - Free convection air: 5–25 W/m²·K.
  - Forced convection air: 10–200 W/m²·K.
  - Forced convection water: 500–20,000 W/m²·K.
- **Typical Food Processing Applications:**
  - **Free:** bread cooling, jam setting, canned food cooling in still retort, fruit storage cooling.
  - **Forced:** plate heat exchangers (milk pasteurization), air blast freezers (fish/meat), spray dryers (milk powder), fluidized bed dryers (grains), scraped surface exchangers (ice cream).
- **Exam Strategy Checklist:**
  1. Identify whether the problem is **free** or **forced** convection first.
  2. Compute the governing dimensionless number with **correct units** (T in K for $\beta$).
  3. **Check flow regime** against critical transition value.
  4. **Select correlation** within its valid range.
  5. Compute $Nu$, then $h$, then $q$ with proper units.
  6. **State flow regime** explicitly in the answer script.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Module 2 questions on this topic often combine free convection over food-related geometries (storage tanks, baking trays, cooling racks) with forced convection through heat exchangers. Practice identifying the **type of convection** before substituting any formula — examiners award marks for correct problem classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer Coefficient and Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Heat Transfer Coefficient and Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Heat Transfer Coefficient (h) The **Heat Transfer Coefficient (h)** is a proportionality constant that quantifies the rate of convective heat transfer between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) at that surface. It is defined by **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $$q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ where: - $q$ = rate of heat transfer (**W**) - $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²K**) - $A$ = surface area through which heat flows (**m²**) - $\Delta T$ = temperature difference between surface and bulk fluid (**K** or **°C**) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The coefficient $h$ is *not* a thermodynamic property of the fluid — it is an **engineering correlation parameter** that depends on geometry, flow regime (laminar/turbulent), fluid properties, and the driving $\Delta T$. Typical magnitudes in food processing: $h \approx 5$–$25$ W/m²K (natural convection in air), $h \approx 500$–$10{,}000$ W/m²K (forced convection with water, condensation, boiling). ### 1.2 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** consolidates the *combined thermal resistances* of all the layers and convective films through which heat must pass between two bulk fluids. For a typical heat exchanger: $$q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{overall}$$ The units of $U$ are also **W/m²K**, but it is referenced to a *chosen area* (inner, outer, or mean). It is the single coefficient you plug into the LMTD method. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Resistor" Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy (Electrical–Thermal Analogy):** > Imagine electricity flowing through a set of **resistors in series** (a battery driving current through several resistor blocks). The **current (I)** is the analogue of **heat flow (q)**; the **voltage drop (ΔV)** is the analogue of **temperature drop (ΔT)**. > - A **single convective film** behaves like a *single resistor* $R_{conv} = \frac{1}{hA}$. > - A **composite wall** (e.g., stainless steel + food crust + fouling layer) is a *stack of resistors in series*. > - The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is the **effective conductance** of this entire stack — i.e., $\frac{1}{UA} = \sum R_i$. So **h is local (one film)**, and **U is global (entire wall + both films)**. In food processing, you rarely deal with $h$ alone in design — almost every exchanger design starts with **$U$**. ### 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters in Food Processing Food products are usually: - **Sensitive to overheating** (nutrients, color, texture degrade). - **Multiphase / viscous** (milk, juices, sauces — low $h$). - **Prone to fouling** (proteins denature, sugars caramelize on hot walls — *fouling resistance*). This is why **U** is the practical design parameter — it absorbs *all* the losses (wall, fouling, both fluid films) into a single number. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Resistances Involved in a Typical Heat Exchanger When heat passes from a *hot fluid* to a *cold fluid* through a solid wall, it encounters the following **resistances in series**: 1. **Convective resistance on the hot (inner) side:** $R_{h,i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i}$ 2. **Conduction resistance of the pipe/wall:** $R_{wall} = \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2 \pi k L}$ *(cylindrical)* or $R_{wall} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{kA}$ *(plane)* 3. **Convective resistance on the cold (outer) side:** $R_{h,o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ 4. **Fouling resistances (in food industry — critical!):** $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$ ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU Cheat Sheet — Memorize These Equations** | # | Geometry | Governing Equation | Reference Area | |---|----------|--------------------|----------------| | 1 | Plane Wall, single layer | $q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{\Delta x}$ | $A$ (constant) | | 2 | Plane Wall, composite | $q = \dfrac{A \Delta T_{tot}}{\sum (\Delta x_i/k_i)}$ | $A$ (constant) | | 3 | Cylinder wall (single) | $q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | — | | 4 | **U for plane wall** | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{\Delta x_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Any $A$ (same) | | 5 | **U for cylinder (inner-area basis)** | $\dfrac{1}{U_i A_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Inner area | | 6 | **U for cylinder (outer-area basis)** | $\dfrac{1}{U_o A_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Outer area | | 7 | **U for cylinder (mean-area basis)** | $\dfrac{1}{U_m A_m} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Mean area | | 8 | Fouling-modified U | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_{i,o}} + \dfrac{\Delta x}{k} + R_{f} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | — | | 9 | LMTD (linked to U) | $q = U A \, \Delta T_{LM}$ | — | | 10 | Critical insulation radius (cylinder) | $r_{cr} = \dfrac{k}{h_o}$ | — | > **Key Identity (often asked in KTU):** Numerically, $U_i \cdot A_i = U_o \cdot A_o = U_m \cdot A_m = \dfrac{1}{\sum R_i}$ — they all represent the **same overall conductance** $(\text{W/K})$, just referenced to a different area. ### 2.3 The Logic of the "Sum of Resistances" Let us follow the heat path for a *jacketed steam-heated food pasteurizer*: $$\text{Steam} \xrightarrow{\text{convection inside pipe}} \text{Steel wall} \xrightarrow{\text{conduction}} \text{Food crust (fouling)} \xrightarrow{\text{conduction}} \text{Bulk food (convection)}$$ By energy conservation, the **same $q$** flows through every layer, so: $$q = \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{1/(h_i A_i)} = \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta x_2/(k_2 A)} = \dfrac{\Delta T_3}{\Delta x_3/(k_3 A)} = \dfrac{\Delta T_4}{1/(h_o A_o)}$$ Adding all temperature drops (which must equal the total driving $\Delta T$): $$\Delta T_{total} = q \cdot \left[\frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\Delta x_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{\Delta x_3}{k_3 A} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}\right]$$ Comparing with $q = U A \Delta T_{total}$: $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{UA} = \sum_{j} R_j \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{U} = \sum_{j} \dfrac{1}{h_j} \text{ (per unit area)}}$$ ### 2.4 The Role of the Fouling Factor in Food Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fouling in food heat exchangers** is *non-negligible*. Milk proteins (β-lactoglobulin), fruit pectins, and starches deposit on hot walls during pasteurization/UHT, adding a thermal resistance $R_f$ (typical: $\mathbf{0.0001 \text{ to } 0.001 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}}$). KTU problems frequently include a $R_f$ term: > $$\dfrac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \dfrac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$ > The U-value **drops** as fouling accumulates — this is why CIP (Clean-In-Place) cycles are scheduled in dairies. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) for milk pasteurization** — $U \approx 3000$–$6000$ W/m²K (clean); with fouling, $U$ can halve. - **Scraped Surface Exchanger (SSHE)** for viscous chocolate/ice-cream mix — $U \approx 500$–$1500$ W/m²K. - **Steam-jacketed cooking kettle** — $U \approx 200$–$500$ W/m²K (low, due to natural convection inside). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for a Plane Composite Wall **Setup:** Hot fluid at $T_{\infty,h}$ (h coeff $h_i$) → layer 1 (thickness $\Delta x_1$, $k_1$) → layer 2 (thickness $\Delta x_2$, $k_2$) → cold fluid at $T_{\infty,c}$ (h coeff $h_o$). All layers have the same area $A$. **Step 1 — Write $q$ for each resistance:** The same heat flux $q$ passes through all five resistances. For a plane wall (constant $A$): $$q = h_i A (T_{\infty,h} - T_{s,i}) \quad (1)$$ $$q = \frac{k_1 A}{\Delta x_1}(T_{s,i} - T_{s,1}) \quad (2)$$ $$q = \frac{k_2 A}{\Delta x_2}(T_{s,1} - T_{s,o}) \quad (3)$$ $$q = h_o A (T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,c}) \quad (4)$$ **Step 2 — Solve each for the temperature drop:** $$T_{\infty,h} - T_{s,i} = \frac{q}{h_i A}$$ $$T_{s,i} - T_{s,1} = \frac{q \Delta x_1}{k_1 A}$$ $$T_{s,1} - T_{s,o} = \frac{q \Delta x_2}{k_2 A}$$ $$T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,c} = \frac{q}{h_o A}$$ **Step 3 — Add all four equations (Telescoping cancellation):** All intermediate surface temperatures cancel because they are summed in a chain: $$\begin{aligned} (T_{\infty,h} - T_{s,i}) + (T_{s,i} - T_{s,1}) + (T_{s,1} - T_{s,o}) + (T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,c}) &= \frac{q}{A}\left[\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\Delta x_1}{k_1} + \frac{\Delta x_2}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_o}\right] \end{aligned}$$ The left-hand side collapses to: $$T_{\infty,h} - T_{\infty,c} = \Delta T_{total}$$ **Step 4 — Compare with the definition $q = U A \Delta T_{total}$:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_{total} &= \frac{q}{A}\left[\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\Delta x_1}{k_1} + \frac{\Delta x_2}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_o}\right] \\[4pt] \Rightarrow \quad \frac{q}{A} &= \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{\left[\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\Delta x_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{\Delta x_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}\right]} \end{aligned}$$ Comparing with $\dfrac{q}{A} = U \cdot \Delta T_{total}$: $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\Delta x_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{\Delta x_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$ This is the **plane-wall U-equation**, and the logic generalizes to *N* layers. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: U for a Cylindrical Pipe (Inner, Outer, and Mean Area Bases) **Setup:** Hot fluid inside a pipe of inner radius $r_i$ and outer radius $r_o$ (wall conductivity $k$); outer convection $h_o$ to surrounding. Length $L$. **Step 1 — Heat flow for the three resistances (each is the *same* q):** Inner convection: $\quad q = h_i (2\pi r_i L)(T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i})$ Wall conduction (cylindrical): $\quad q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_{s,i} - T_{s,o})}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ Outer convection: $\quad q = h_o (2\pi r_o L)(T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o})$ **Step 2 — Solve each for the temperature drop:** $$T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i} = \frac{q}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)}$$ $$T_{s,i} - T_{s,o} = \frac{q \ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$ $$T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o} = \frac{q}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)}$$ **Step 3 — Add and cancel intermediate $T_s$'s:** $$\begin{aligned} T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = q \left[\frac{1}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)}\right] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Define the overall coefficient $U_i$ (based on inner area $A_i = 2\pi r_i L$):** $$q = U_i (2\pi r_i L)(T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o})$$ Equating the two expressions for $q$: $$U_i (2\pi r_i L) = \left[\frac{1}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)}\right]^{-1}$$ Taking the reciprocal: $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{U_i A_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}}$$ Similarly, for **outer-area basis** ($A_o = 2\pi r_o L$): $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{U_o A_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}}$$ And **mean-area basis** ($A_m = 2\pi r_m L$ with $r_m = (r_o - r_i)/\ln(r_o/r_i)$): $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{U_m A_m} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall Note:** Students often write $U_i = U_o = U_m$. This is **WRONG**. Only the product $U \cdot A$ (the overall conductance) is invariant. The coefficients themselves differ because the area changes. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylindrical) Adding insulation (k_ins, outer radius $r$) on a bare pipe of radius $r_i$ actually *decreases* heat loss for $r < r_{cr}$ and *increases* it for $r > r_{cr}$. Let us find $r_{cr}$. **Step 1 — Total resistance per unit length (outer area basis, $A = 2\pi r L$):** $$\frac{1}{U_o A_o} = \frac{1}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)} + \frac{\ln(r/r_i)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r L)}$$ The first term does not depend on $r$. The other two do. **Step 2 — Heat loss per unit length:** $$q' = \frac{T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}}{\dfrac{1}{h_i (2\pi r_i)} + \dfrac{\ln(r/r_i)}{2\pi k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o (2\pi r)}}$$ **Step 3 — To find the extremum of $q'$ (i.e., minimum total resistance), differentiate the resistance $R'(r)$ w.r.t. $r$ and set to zero:** $$R'(r) = \frac{\ln(r/r_i)}{2\pi k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r)}$$ $$\frac{dR'}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\pi k_{ins} r} - \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r^2)} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2\pi k_{ins} r} &= \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r^2)} \\[4pt] h_o \, r &= k_{ins} \\[4pt] \boxed{r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation:** - If $r < r_{cr}$: adding insulation **increases** $q'$ (the added surface area dominates, the outer resistance falls faster than the conductive resistance rises). - If $r > r_{cr}$: adding insulation **decreases** $q'$ (insulation behaves normally). - If $r = r_{cr}$: heat loss is **maximum** at this critical thickness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Food Engineering Relevance:** Insulating a *small* copper/steel tube (e.g., refrigerant line of an ice-cream freezer) with $r < r_{cr}$ will *worsen* heat loss. Always check the critical radius before specifying insulation thickness. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table for KTU Problems | Condition | $\frac{1}{U}$ in simplest form | Comments | |-----------|--------------------------------|----------| | Plane wall, both films | $\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\Delta x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Same $A$ for all terms | | Cylinder, $A_i$ basis | $\dfrac{1}{U_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{r_i \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{r_i}{r_o h_o}$ | Multiply through by $1/A_i$ to get full form | | Thin wall ($r_o \approx r_i$) | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\Delta x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Reduces to plane-wall form | | With fouling | $\dfrac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \dfrac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Direct addition (food industry) | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Resistance Network in a Food Heat Exchanger The following Mermaid block diagram visualizes the **series-resistance path** of heat from the hot fluid (e.g., steam) to the cold fluid (e.g., milk): ```mermaid flowchart LR HF["HOT FLUID
T_infinity_h"] --> R1["R_i = 1 / h_i A_i
INNER CONVECTION"] R1 --> S1["SURFACE T_si"] S1 --> R2["R_wall = ln r_o/r_i / 2 pi k L
WALL CONDUCTION"] R2 --> S2["INTERFACE T_s1"] S2 --> R3["R_fouling = R_f / A
FOULING DEPOSIT"] R3 --> S3["SURFACE T_so"] S3 --> R4["R_o = 1 / h_o A_o

T_infinity_c"] style HF fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A65A00,color:#000 style R1 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#800000,color:#000 style S1 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R2 fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style S2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R3 fill:#D9B3FF,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style S3 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000 style R4 fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style CF fill:#80D0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The heat flux $q$ is the same through *every* block. Each block is a "resistor" with a known value. The sum of all four resistances gives the inverse of $U \cdot A$. ### 4.2 Process Flow — Algorithm to Compute U for a KTU Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START
Given: geometry, fluids, T's, h's, k, dimensions"] --> B{"Geometry?"} B -->|Plane wall| C["Use 1/U = 1/h_i + sum Delta x_i / k_i + 1/h_o"] B -->|Cylinder| D["Use 1/U_i A_i = 1/h_i A_i + ln r_o/r_i / 2 pi k L + 1/h_o A_o"] B -->|Sphere| E["Use 1/U_i A_i = 1/h_i A_i + 1/4 pi k (1/r_i - 1/r_o) + 1/h_o A_o"] C --> F{"Fouling given?"} D --> F E --> F F -->|Yes| G["Add R_f,i and R_f,o to sum of resistances"] F -->|No| H["Proceed without fouling"] G --> I["Invert to get 1/U = sum R_i"] H --> I I --> J["Solve: U A = 1 / sum R_i"] J --> K["Compute q = U A Delta T_total"] K --> L["END"] style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A65A00,color:#000 style B fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style E fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style F fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G fill:#D9B3FF,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style H fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style I fill:#FFFFB3,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style J fill:#FFFFB3,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style K fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#800000,color:#000 style L fill:#80D0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Concept Map — How h, U, LMTD, and Area Interlock ```mermaid flowchart LR h1["h_i
