Sizing"] Q2 --> DESIGN style h1 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#800000,color:#000 style h2 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#800000,color:#000 style kW fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style Rf fill:#D9B3FF,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style SUM fill:#FFFFB3,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style U fill:#80D0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style Aref fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,color:#000 style Q1 fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style DESIGN fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A65A00,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`** **Q1. Define the heat transfer coefficient and the overall heat transfer coefficient. How are they related? [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The **heat transfer coefficient (h)** is defined by Newton's Law of Cooling, $q = hA\Delta T$, where $h$ is the convective proportionality constant (**W/m²K**) between a surface and a single fluid. The **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** is the combined coefficient that accounts for the **convective resistance on both fluid sides plus the conductive resistance of the wall and any fouling layers**, in series. For a plane wall, $U$ is given by: $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{\Delta x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ **Relation:** $U$ is the *overall* version of $h$. For a single clean surface with one fluid, $U = h$. For a composite wall with both fluids, $U$ is always **less than the smallest individual $h$** because the sum of resistances in series exceeds any single resistance. --- > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`** **Q2. What is a fouling factor? Why is it significant in food heat exchangers? [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** A **fouling factor (R_f)** is an additional thermal resistance (units: **m²K/W**) added to the denominator of $U$ to account for the insulating deposit (baked-on food residue, scale, protein film, etc.) that forms on heat transfer surfaces during operation. It is significant in food heat exchangers because: 1. **Food products (milk, juice, starch) are deposit-prone** — proteins denature, sugars caramelize on hot walls. 2. $R_f$ **reduces $U$** over time, increasing the heating medium temperature required and reducing product throughput. 3. It dictates the **CIP (Clean-In-Place) cycle frequency** in dairy/food plants. 4. KTU design problems **must include $R_f$** for realistic sizing; neglecting it causes under-design of area by 30–50%. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Plane Composite Wall with Fouling* > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]`** A **plate heat exchanger (PHE)** used in milk pasteurization has a stainless-steel plate of thickness **3 mm** and thermal conductivity $k_{ss} = 16$ **W/mK**. Hot water flows on one side ($h_i = 2500$ W/m²K) and milk on the other side ($h_o = 1800$ W/m²K). A milk-protein deposit forms a fouling layer of thickness **0.5 mm** with $k_f = 0.2$ **W/mK** on the milk side. The hot water is at 90 °C, and the bulk milk is at 20 °C. The effective heat transfer area of one plate is **0.04 m²**. **(a)** Derive the expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient for a plane composite wall, and compute the **overall U** for the system including fouling. [7 Marks] **(b)** Compute the steady-state **rate of heat transfer** and the **temperature of the milk-side surface** of the steel plate. [7 Marks] --- ##### **Solution to Q-A (a):** **Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1. The general plane-wall result is: $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\Delta x_{ss}}{k_{ss}} + \frac{\Delta x_f}{k_f} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ **Computation (4 marks):** Substituting values: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{h_i} &= \frac{1}{2500} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W} \\[4pt] \frac{\Delta x_{ss}}{k_{ss}} &= \frac{0.003}{16} = 1.875 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W} \\[4pt] \frac{\Delta x_f}{k_f} &= \frac{0.0005}{0.2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W} \\[4pt] \frac{1}{h_o} &= \frac{1}{1800} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W} \\[4pt] \sum R &= 4.0\times10^{-4} + 1.875\times10^{-4} + 2.5\times10^{-3} + 5.556\times10^{-4} \\[4pt] \sum R &= 3.643 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{3.643 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 274.5 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}}$$ **Observation:** Notice that the **fouling layer** contributes the largest single resistance ($\approx 69\%$ of total). This is the realistic scenario in a dairy PHE. --- ##### **Solution to Q-A (b):** **Step 1 — Rate of heat transfer:** (3 marks) $$q = U A \Delta T_{total} = 274.5 \times 0.04 \times (90 - 20)$$ $$q = 274.5 \times 0.04 \times 70 = 768.6 \;\text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Temperature of the milk-side surface** (the steel–fouling interface): (4 marks) The heat $q$ first passes from hot water through $h_i$ to the steel's hot side, then through the steel, and reaches the cold side of the steel. We work from the hot water inward: Temperature drop across inner convection film: $$\Delta T_{h,i} = \frac{q}{h_i A} = \frac{768.6}{2500 \times 0.04} = 7.69 \;°\text{C}$$ Temperature drop across the steel wall: $$\Delta T_{ss} = \frac{q \, \Delta x_{ss}}{k_{ss} A} = \frac{768.6 \times 0.003}{16 \times 0.04} = 3.60 \;°\text{C}$$ So the temperature at the **cold side of the steel** (i.e., the steel–fouling interface, which is the milk-side surface of the steel): $$\begin{aligned} T_{steel,\text{cold}} &= T_{water} - \Delta T_{h,i} - \Delta T_{ss} \\[4pt] &= 90 - 7.69 - 3.60 = 78.71 \;°\text{C} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{T_{s,\text{steel,cold}} \approx 78.7 \;°\text{C}, \quad q \approx 768.6 \;\text{W}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Cylindrical Heat Exchanger with Critical Radius Discussion* > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`** A **double-pipe heat exchanger** is used to preheat orange juice. The inner stainless-steel pipe has $r_i = 12$ mm, $r_o = 14$ mm, $k_{ss} = 16$ W/mK, and length $L = 2$ m. Hot water flows inside with $h_i = 3000$ W/m²K, and juice flows in the annulus with $h_o = 1200$ W/m²K. Bulk hot water temperature is 85 °C, bulk juice temperature is 25 °C. **(a)** Derive the overall heat transfer coefficient for a cylindrical geometry (inner-area basis), and compute $U_i$ for the system. Also calculate the **total heat duty** $q$. [7 Marks] **(b)** It is proposed to insulate the outer pipe with 30 mm of mineral wool ($k_{ins} = 0.04$ W/mK). Determine the **critical radius of insulation** and state with reasons whether the insulation will **increase or decrease** the heat transfer rate. Calculate the new $q$ with insulation. [7 Marks] --- ##### **Solution to Q-B (a):** **Derivation (3 marks):** From Section 3.2, the cylindrical result is: $$\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k_{ss} L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$ **Computation (4 marks):** Compute the three resistances: $$\begin{aligned} A_i &= 2\pi r_i L = 2\pi (0.012)(2) = 0.1508 \;\text{m}^2 \\[4pt] A_o &= 2\pi r_o L = 2\pi (0.014)(2) = 0.1759 \;\text{m}^2 \\[4pt] R_{conv,i} &= \frac{1}{h_i A_i} = \frac{1}{3000 \times 0.1508} = 2.211 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K/W} \\[4pt] R_{wall} &= \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k_{ss} L} = \frac{\ln(14/12)}{2\pi (16)(2)} = \frac{0.1542}{201.06} = 7.669 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K/W} \\[4pt] R_{conv,o} &= \frac{1}{h_o A_o} = \frac{1}{1200 \times 0.1759} = 4.738 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K/W} \\[4pt] \sum R &= 2.211\times10^{-3} + 7.669\times10^{-4} + 4.738\times10^{-3} = 7.716 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K/W} \end{aligned}$$ So the overall conductance is: $$U_i A_i = \frac{1}{7.716 \times 10^{-3}} = 129.6 \;\text{W/K}$$ $$\boxed{U_i = \frac{129.6}{0.1508} \approx 859.3 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}}$$ **Heat duty (full 7 marks include this):** $$q = U_i A_i \, \Delta T = 129.6 \times (85 - 25) = 129.6 \times 60$$ $$\boxed{q = 7775 \;\text{W} \approx 7.78 \;\text{kW}}$$ --- ##### **Solution to Q-B (b):** **Step 1 — Critical radius of insulation:** (2 marks) $$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o} = \frac{0.04}{1200} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} = 0.0333 \;\text{mm}$$ **Step 2 — Comparison with outer pipe radius:** (2 marks) The insulation outer radius is $r = r_o + 0.030 = 0.014 + 0.030 = 0.044$ m $= 44$ mm. Since: $$r = 44 \;\text{mm} \gg r_{cr} = 0.0333 \;\text{mm}$$ we are far **above** the critical radius. Therefore, the insulation will **decrease** the heat transfer rate (i.e., reduce $q$), as expected for a "thick" insulation layer. **Step 3 — New heat duty with insulation:** (3 marks) New outer radius (insulated): $r_{new} = 0.044$ m. New $A_{o,new} = 2\pi r_{new} L = 2\pi (0.044)(2) = 0.5529$ m². Add the insulation resistance: $$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_{new}/r_o)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(0.044/0.014)}{2\pi (0.04)(2)} = \frac{1.1441}{0.5027} = 2.276 \;\text{K/W}$$ Wait — we must use **outer-area basis** $U_o$ now because the area reference is at the **outer surface of insulation**. Let's keep the conductance $UA$ invariant approach: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U_o A_o} &= R_{conv,i} + R_{wall} + R_{ins} + R_{conv,o,new} \\[4pt] R_{conv,o,new} &= \frac{1}{h_o A_{o,new}} = \frac{1}{1200 \times 0.5529} = 1.507 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K/W} \\[4pt] \sum R_{new} &= 2.211\times10^{-3} + 7.669\times10^{-4} + 2.276 + 1.507\times10^{-3} \;\text{K/W} \\[4pt] \sum R_{new} &\approx 2.281 \;\text{K/W} \end{aligned}$$ Wait — $R_{ins}$ is huge (2.276 K/W) because mineral wool is a great insulator. That's the point! Let me recompute carefully: $$\frac{1}{U_o A_o} = 0.002211 + 0.000767 + 2.276 + 0.001507 \approx 2.281 \;\text{K/W}$$ $$U_o A_o = \frac{1}{2.281} = 0.4384 \;\text{W/K}$$ $$q_{new} = 0.4384 \times 60 = 26.3 \;\text{W}$$ $$\boxed{q_{new} \approx 26.3 \;\text{W (vs. 7775 W uninsulated)}}$$ **Conclusion:** The 30 mm mineral wool insulation reduces heat loss from 7775 W to 26.3 W — a **296× reduction**. This is by design; insulation is meant to *suppress* heat loss. The critical-radius analysis confirms the insulation is operating in the "thick" regime where the conventional expectation holds. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency:** $h$ is in **W/m²K**, $k$ in **W/mK**, $\Delta x$ in **m**, so $\Delta x/k$ is in **m²K/W** (a *resistance* per unit area). Mixing units is the #1 reason students lose marks. > 2. **Area basis confusion:** For cylinders, you must be **consistent** — either use $U_i, A_i$ throughout *or* $U_o, A_o$ throughout. Don't mix. > 3. **Forgetting the $\ln$ in cylindrical walls:** A plane-wall formula applied to a pipe is wrong unless the pipe wall is "thin" ($r_o/r_i < 1.5$). > 4. **Fouling as a separate term:** Some students fold $R_f$ into the wall resistance by mistake. The fouling layer has its **own thickness and $k$**, treat it as a distinct layer. > 5. **Critical radius sign error:** $r_{cr} = k/h_o$, not $h_o/k$. The dimensions work out only with the correct form. > 6. **U is always less than the smallest h:** If you compute $U$ and get a number *larger* than $h_i$ or $h_o$, you have a sign error. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $q = hA\Delta T$ — the foundation of convective heat transfer. - **Overall U** consolidates *all* thermal resistances between two bulk fluids into a single coefficient. - **Plane wall composite:** $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \displaystyle\sum \dfrac{\Delta x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ - **Cylinder composite:** $\dfrac{1}{U_i A_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ - **$U \cdot A$ is invariant**, but $U$ itself depends on the chosen reference area. - **Fouling factor $R_f$ (m²K/W)** reduces $U$ over time — critical in food/dairy exchangers. - **Critical radius** $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$ — only relevant for *thin* insulation on *small* pipes. - **U is always less than the smallest $h$** in the system (resistances in series add). - **LMTD method** uses $q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ to size the exchanger area. - **Typical U values:** 5–25 (gas, natural conv.), 50–500 (liquid, natural), 500–2000 (liquid, forced), 3000–10000 W/m²K (condensing steam, boiling water). - **Dimension check:** $[h] = $ W/m²K; $[U] = $ W/m²K; $[k] = $ W/mK; $[R_f] = $ m²K/W. - **Food-processing significance:** U determines heating uniformity, residence time, nutritional retention, and plant throughput.

topics.title: Factors Affecting Heat Transfer Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Heat Transfer Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Transfer Coefficient (h):** The proportionality constant that relates the rate of heat transfer between a surface and a fluid to the temperature difference between them, expressed in **W/(m²·K)**. It encapsulates the combined effects of conduction and convection at a fluid–solid interface.

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** integrates the individual convective coefficients on both sides of a wall, the conductive resistance of the wall material, and any fouling resistances encountered in food processing equipment such as plate heat exchangers, evaporators, and sterilizers.

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In food process engineering, the heat transfer coefficient is **not a property of a fluid alone** — it is a system-level parameter that depends on geometry, flow regime, fluid properties, and surface characteristics. This is the central idea of Module 2.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pouring hot tea into a **ceramic mug** versus a **thin-walled steel cup**. The steel cup burns your hand almost immediately, while the ceramic mug feels warm. Why?

- The **steel** has high thermal conductivity, so heat moves quickly from the tea to your hand → high **h**.
- The **ceramic** has low thermal conductivity, slowing the heat flow → lower **h**.

Now imagine the same steel cup sitting in **still air** versus in front of a **fan**. The fan dramatically increases the rate at which your hand feels the heat, even though the cup is identical. The **velocity of the surrounding fluid (air)** has changed the heat transfer coefficient!

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-home intuition:** The heat transfer coefficient tells you "how efficiently" heat crosses a boundary. A large **h** means the boundary is a poor insulator; a small **h** means the boundary strongly resists heat flow.

## 1.3 Classification of Heat Transfer Coefficients

| Type | Symbol | Typical Range | Common Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forced Convection (gas) | $h$ | **5 – 250 W/(m²·K)** | Air cooling in cold storage |
| Forced Convection (liquid) | $h$ | **100 – 20,000 W/(m²·K)** | Milk pasteurization |
| Natural Convection | $h$ | **2 – 1,000 W/(m²·K)** | Ambient air heating of food vats |
| Condensing Steam | $h$ | **5,000 – 100,000 W/(m²·K)** | Steam-heated jacketed kettles |
| Boiling Water | $h$ | **2,500 – 100,000 W/(m²·K)** | Jacketed cooking vessels |
| Fouled Surfaces (food) | $h$ | Reduced by **20 – 80 %** | Heat exchangers in dairy industry |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test whether students can recall the **order of magnitude** of **h** for different processes. Always remember: **gases < liquids < condensing/boiling fluids**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Major Factors

The heat transfer coefficient at a fluid–solid interface is influenced by the following **eight systematic factors**. Understanding each is essential for Module 2 evaluation.

### Factor 1 — Fluid Velocity (v)

The velocity of the moving fluid directly governs the **thermal boundary layer thickness**. Higher velocity → thinner boundary layer → higher **h**.

$$h \propto v^{n} \quad \text{where } 0.4 \leq n \leq 0.8$$

- **Laminar flow:** $n \approx 0.5$
- **Turbulent flow:** $n \approx 0.8$

### Factor 2 — Fluid Physical Properties

The four key thermophysical properties are:

- **Viscosity (μ)** — affects momentum diffusion; high μ → thicker boundary layer → lower h
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** — directly proportional to h
- **Specific Heat (Cp)** — energy carrying capacity of the fluid
- **Density (ρ)** — affects Reynolds number and buoyancy-driven flow

These combine into **two dimensionless numbers**:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} \quad \text{(inertia / viscous forces)}$$

$$\text{Pr} = \frac{C_p \,\mu}{k} \quad \text{(momentum / thermal diffusivity)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Prandtl number** is a *fluid property only*. For food liquids (oils, syrups), Pr is large. For gases, Pr ≈ 0.7. For liquid metals, Pr << 1.

### Factor 3 — Geometry of the System

The **shape and characteristic length** (D for tubes, L for plates, R for spheres) directly affect the boundary layer development. A long tube allows the boundary layer to grow, eventually becoming fully developed, which changes the local **h**.

### Factor 4 — Nature of Fluid Flow (Laminar vs. Turbulent)

- **Laminar (Re < 2300):** Heat transfer primarily by conduction across the boundary layer. **h** is small.
- **Transitional (2300 < Re < 10,000):** Unsteady, difficult to predict.
- **Turbulent (Re > 10,000):** Vigorous mixing by eddies, very high **h**. Heat transfer increases as $\text{Re}^{0.8}$.

### Factor 5 — Temperature Difference (ΔT)

- In **natural convection**: higher ΔT → stronger buoyancy → higher h.
- In **boiling**: increasing ΔT changes the regime (natural convection → nucleate boiling → film boiling), each with very different h.
- In **condensation**: increasing ΔT reduces the condensate film thickness, slightly increasing h.

### Factor 6 — Phase Change (Condensation / Boiling)

Phase change introduces **latent heat transport**, which is enormously more efficient than sensible heat transport. This is why steam-side coefficients are 10–100× higher than hot-water-side coefficients.

### Factor 7 — Surface Conditions (Roughness, Wettability, Fouling)

- **Rough surfaces** in turbulent flow enhance mixing → higher h.
- **Non-wettable surfaces** promote dropwise condensation, which has h up to **10× higher** than filmwise condensation.
- **Fouling** (burn-on of milk proteins, caramelization of sugars, scale formation) **drastically reduces h** and is a critical concern in food processing.

### Factor 8 — Orientation and Buoyancy Effects

The orientation of the surface (horizontal vs. vertical) affects natural convection. For example, a horizontal plate facing upward loses heat faster than one facing downward.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All the following dimensionless correlations are **Nusselt number (Nu)**-based. The Nusselt number is the **dimensionless heat transfer coefficient**: $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{h D}{k}$.

| Correlation Name | Equation | Applicable Regime |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dittus–Boelter** | $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{n}$ | Turbulent, $0.7 \le \text{Pr} \le 160$, $n = 0.4$ (cooling), $n = 0.3$ (heating) |
| **Sieder–Tate** | $\text{Nu} = 0.027 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \left( \dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.14}$ | Turbulent, accounts for viscosity variation |
| **Laminar in tubes (Sieder–Tate)** | $\text{Nu} = 1.86 \, \text{Re}^{1/3} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \left( \dfrac{D}{L} \right)^{1/3} \left( \dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.14}$ | $\text{Re} < 2100$ |
| **Nusselt Condensation** | $\text{Nu} = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho g (\rho - \rho_v) h_{fg} \, k^{3}}{\mu \, L \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$ | Laminar film condensation on vertical plate |
| **Rohsenow Boiling** | $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k_l} = \dfrac{J}{C_{sf}} \left[ \dfrac{g (\rho_l - \rho_v) h_{fg}}{n \, \mu_l \, c_{pl} \, T_{sat}} \right]^{0.33}$ | Nucleate pool boiling |
| **Natural Convection (Vertical Plate)** | $\text{Nu} = 0.59 \, \text{Gr}^{1/4}$ | Laminar natural convection |
| **Natural Convection (Vertical Plate, Turbulent)** | $\text{Nu} = 0.10 \, \text{Gr}^{1/3}$ | Turbulent natural convection |
| **Overall U** | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Composite wall with fouling |

Where:
- $\text{Gr} = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$ is the **Grashof number**
- $R_f$ is the **fouling resistance** (m²·K/W)
- $J$ and $C_{sf}$ are fluid–surface combinations in Rohsenow

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in food processing?**
> - In **plate heat exchangers for milk pasteurization**, scale deposition from milk proteins (fouling) reduces h by 40–60 % within hours. The Sieder–Tate correlation predicts this drop through increased wall viscosity.
> - In **steam-jacketed cooking kettles**, condensing steam provides an extremely high h ≈ 10,000 W/(m²·K), enabling rapid cooking of viscous foods like sugar syrups.
> - In **freeze-drying (lyophilization)**, the dominant resistance is the **external mass/heat transfer coefficient** of the sublimed vapor leaving the food surface — controlling this h controls the entire drying rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)

Consider a composite wall separating a hot fluid (inside) from a cold fluid (outside). Heat flows through **five resistances in series**:

1. Inside convective resistance: $R_i = \dfrac{1}{h_i \, A_i}$
2. Inside fouling resistance: $R_{f,i} = \dfrac{R_{f,i}^{\prime}}{A_i}$
3. Wall conductive resistance: $R_w = \dfrac{x}{k \, A_m}$
4. Outside fouling resistance: $R_{f,o} = \dfrac{R_{f,o}^{\prime}}{A_o}$
5. Outside convective resistance: $R_o = \dfrac{1}{h_o \, A_o}$

For a **plane wall of equal area** $A$, by conservation of energy (steady state):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}}{R_{\text{total}}} \\[6pt]
q &= \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i}^{\prime} + \dfrac{x}{k} + R_{f,o}^{\prime} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining the **overall heat transfer coefficient U** as:

$$q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{\text{overall}}$$

Equating and solving for U:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i}^{\prime} + \dfrac{x}{k} + R_{f,o}^{\prime} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The **dominant resistance** controls the system. If $h_i$ is small (e.g., a viscous food), increasing $h_o$ (e.g., adding more steam) will *not* significantly improve $U$. The student must always identify the **controlling resistance** first.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Dittus–Boelter Correlation (Turbulent Forced Convection)

The Dittus–Boelter equation is empirical, but its functional form can be derived from **dimensional analysis** combined with the Reynolds analogy between heat and momentum transfer.

### Step 1 — Identify Governing Variables

For turbulent flow in a smooth tube, the local Nusselt number depends on:

$$\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Pr})$$

### Step 2 — Apply Buckingham π-Theorem

The two dimensionless groups form the basis of the correlation. Empirical fitting of thousands of data points gave the form:

$$\text{Nu} = a \cdot \text{Re}^{b} \cdot \text{Pr}^{c}$$

### Step 3 — Dittus & Boelter (1930) Experimental Fit

For the heating case (fluid colder than wall, $n = 0.4$):

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{0.4}\;}
$$

For the cooling case (fluid hotter than wall, $n = 0.3$):

$$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{0.3}$$

**Valid range:**
- $\text{Re} > 10{,}000$
- $0.7 \le \text{Pr} \le 160$
- $L/D > 10$ (fully developed flow)

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the exponent **0.8 on Re**: this is why turbulent heat transfer is so sensitive to velocity. Doubling velocity in turbulent flow multiplies **h** by $2^{0.8} \approx 1.74$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Nusselt's Condensation Correlation

Consider a vertical plate of height L at temperature $T_w$ in contact with saturated vapor at $T_{\text{sat}}$. A laminar film of condensate of thickness $\delta(y)$ flows downward under gravity.

### Step 1 — Force Balance on Film Element

A momentum balance on a differential element of the falling film yields the velocity profile across the film thickness.

### Step 2 — Apply Continuity and the Energy Equation

Heat is transferred by conduction across the film:

$$q_y = k \cdot \frac{dT}{dy}\bigg|_{\text{wall}} = \frac{k \, \Delta T}{\delta(y)}$$

### Step 3 — Combine Momentum and Energy Solutions

Substituting the velocity profile from the Navier–Stokes solution and integrating along the plate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \left[ \frac{\rho \, g \, (\rho - \rho_v) \, h_{fg} \, k^{3}}{4 \, \mu \, \Delta T \, L} \right]^{1/4} \\[6pt]
\text{Nu} &= \frac{h \, L}{k} = 0.725 \left[ \frac{\rho \, g \, (\rho - \rho_v) \, h_{fg} \, L^{3}}{\mu \, k \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight:** The exponent **1/4** (not 1/3) is characteristic of condensation, and is **less sensitive to ΔT** than natural convection (where the exponent is 1/3).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Demonstrating Factor Influence

**Problem:** Water flows through a 5 cm ID smooth tube at 1.5 m/s. Bulk temperature is 40 °C, wall temperature is 80 °C. Estimate the heat transfer coefficient using Dittus–Boelter.

**Step 1 — Properties of water at mean temperature $T_m = (40+80)/2 = 60$ °C:**

- $\rho = 983.2 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 4.71 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Pa·s}$
- $k = 0.654 \; \text{W/(m·K)}$
- $C_p = 4183 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}$
- $\text{Pr} = 3.02$

**Step 2 — Compute Reynolds number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Re} &= \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{983.2 \times 1.5 \times 0.05}{4.71 \times 10^{-4}} \\[4pt]
\text{Re} &\approx 1.566 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **highly turbulent**.

**Step 3 — Select exponent (cooling case, fluid colder than wall):**

For heating, use $n = 0.4$. The wall is hotter, so the fluid is being **heated**: $n = 0.4$.

**Step 4 — Apply Dittus–Boelter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nu} &= 0.023 \times (1.566 \times 10^{5})^{0.8} \times (3.02)^{0.4} \\[4pt]
&= 0.023 \times 8338.6 \times 1.541 \\[4pt]
\text{Nu} &\approx 295.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for h:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{\text{Nu} \cdot k}{D} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{295.5 \times 0.654}{0.05} \\[4pt]
h &\approx 3864 \; \text{W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{K)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (~3,864 W/(m²·K)) is consistent with **forced liquid convection** and confirms that water at moderate velocity is a very effective heat transfer medium in food processing lines such as HTST pasteurizers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Factor | Effect on **h** | Quantitative Effect | Food Engineering Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Velocity | ↑ Increases | $h \propto v^{0.8}$ (turbulent) | Pump throughput in a PHE |
| ↑ Viscosity (μ) | ↓ Decreases | $h \propto \mu^{-0.4}$ to $\mu^{-0.8}$ | Honey vs. water heating |
| ↑ Thermal Conductivity (k) | ↑ Increases | $h \propto k^{0.6}$ | Oil vs. water in kettle |
| ↑ ΔT (boiling) | Regime dependent | Peak h in nucleate boiling | Steam-jacketed rice cookers |
| ↑ Surface Roughness | Slightly ↑ | +10–20 % in turbulent flow | Corroded vs. new tubes |
| ↑ Fouling | ↓ Decreases | Can reduce h by 50–80 % | Milk scale in dairy PHE |
| Phase change | ↑↑ Drastically | +100× over sensible heat | Steam condensation in retort |
| Vertical vs. Horizontal | Geometric shift | 1.3× factor for horizontal | Plate orientation in evaporator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Factors Affecting Heat Transfer Coefficient

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Heat Transfer Coefficient h]:::core

    A --> B[Fluid Properties]:::branch
    A --> C[Flow Conditions]:::branch
    A --> D[System Geometry]:::branch
    A --> E[Thermal Conditions]:::branch
    A --> F[Surface Properties]:::branch
    A --> G[Phase Change Effects]:::branch

    B --> B1[Viscosity mu]
    B --> B2[Thermal Conductivity k]
    B --> B3[Density rho]
    B --> B4[Specific Heat Cp]

    C --> C1[Velocity v]
    C --> C2[Reynolds Number Re]
    C --> C3[Prandtl Number Pr]

    D --> D1[Characteristic Length L or D]
    D --> D2[Shape: tube plate sphere]
    D --> D3[Orientation]

    E --> E1[Delta T]
    E --> E2[Bulk Temperature]
    E --> E3[Wall Temperature]

    F --> F1[Roughness]
    F --> F2[Wettability]
    F --> F3[Fouling Resistance Rf]

    G --> G1[Condensation h_cond]
    G --> G2[Boiling h_boil]
    G --> G3[Sublimation h_sub]

    B1 --> H[Dimensionless Groups]
    B2 --> H
    C2 --> H
    C3 --> H

    H --> H1[Nusselt Number Nu]
    H1 --> I[Calculate h from Nu times k divided by L]
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I

    I --> J[Overall Coefficient U with fouling]:::core

    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef branch fill:#d9e2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Resistance Network Model (Overall U Derivation Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hot Side
        H1[Fluid Hot T_hi]:::fluid
        H2[Convective h_i]:::conv
        H3[Fouling R_fi]:::fouling
    end

    subgraph Wall
        W1[Conduction x over k]:::wall
    end

    subgraph Cold Side
        C1[Fouling R_fo]:::fouling
        C2[Convective h_o]:::conv
        C3[Fluid Cold T_co]:::fluid
    end

    H1 -->|q| H2 --> H3 --> W1 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3

    H2 -.- H2N[1 divided by h_i]:::note
    H3 -.- H3N[R_fi prime]:::note
    W1 -.- W1N[x divided by k]:::note
    C1 -.- C1N[R_fo prime]:::note
    C2 -.- C2N[1 divided by h_o]:::note

    classDef fluid fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000
    classDef fouling fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000
    classDef wall fill:#d9e2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#ffffff,stroke:#7f7f7f,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 4 3
```

## 4.3 Boiling Curve & Regimes — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Increasing Wall Superheat Delta T]:::axis

    A --> R1[Region I: Natural Convection]:::regime
    A --> R2[Region II: Nucleate Boiling]:::regime
    A --> R3[Region III: Transition Boiling]:::regime
    A --> R4[Region IV: Film Boiling]:::regime

    R1 --> D1[Low h, heat by conduction and convection]:::desc
    R2 --> D2[Peak h, bubbles detach and mix vigorously]:::desc
    R3 --> D3[Unstable, h decreases]:::desc
    R4 --> D4[Stable vapor film, h is low but radiation dominates]:::desc

    R2 --> P[Critical Heat Flux CHF]:::peak
    R3 --> P

    P --> P1[Leidenfrost Point: maximum heat flux]:::peakdesc

    classDef axis fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef regime fill:#d9e2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef desc fill:#ffffff,stroke:#7f7f7f,color:#000
    classDef peak fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef peakdesc fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Trace the influence path from any single factor (e.g., "viscosity") → dimensionless number (e.g., Re, Pr) → Nusselt number → **h**. This is the **canonical KTU derivation path** for convective problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define the overall heat transfer coefficient. Write its expression for a plane wall with fouling on both sides.

**Model Answer:**

> The overall heat transfer coefficient **U** is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit overall temperature difference across a composite system. It accounts for **convective** resistances on both sides, **conduction** through the wall, and **fouling** resistances. *(1 mark)*

The expression for a plane wall of thickness **x** and thermal conductivity **k** is:

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i}^{\prime} + \dfrac{x}{k} + R_{f,o}^{\prime} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$$

where $h_i, h_o$ are the inside and outside convective coefficients, and $R_f^{\prime}$ are the fouling factors in m²·K/W. *(2 marks)*

---

### Q2. State any **six** factors affecting the convective heat transfer coefficient.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Fluid velocity** — higher velocity increases **h** by thinning the boundary layer.
2. **Fluid physical properties** — viscosity, thermal conductivity, density, and specific heat.
3. **Geometry and characteristic length** of the heat transfer surface.
4. **Nature of flow** — laminar vs. turbulent (Re).
5. **Temperature difference** between wall and bulk fluid.
6. **Phase change** — boiling/condensation produce much higher **h** than sensible heating.
7. **Surface conditions** — roughness, wettability, and fouling.

*(Any six factors with one-line justification: 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Module 2 Style: Internal Choice Provided]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Discuss in detail the factors affecting the convective heat transfer coefficient. Explain the role of dimensionless numbers Re, Pr, and Nu. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The convective heat transfer coefficient depends on **eight systematic factors**:

**1. Fluid velocity (v):** Higher velocity thins the boundary layer. In turbulent flow, $h \propto v^{0.8}$. *(0.5 mark)*

**2. Fluid properties:** Viscosity, thermal conductivity, density, and specific heat influence both momentum and thermal boundary layers. *(0.5 mark)*

**3. Geometry:** Characteristic length D (tubes), L (plates), and shape (sphere, cylinder) affect boundary layer growth. *(0.5 mark)*

**4. Flow regime:** Laminar, transitional, or turbulent — defined by Re. Turbulent flow gives much higher h. *(0.5 mark)*

**5. Temperature difference (ΔT):** Affects buoyancy in natural convection and regimes in boiling. *(0.5 mark)*

**6. Phase change:** Boiling and condensation involve latent heat, giving 10–100× higher h. *(0.5 mark)*

**7. Surface conditions:** Roughness, wettability (dropwise vs. film condensation), and fouling layers. *(0.5 mark)*

**8. Orientation:** Vertical/horizontal placement affects natural convection currents. *(0.5 mark)*

**Role of Dimensionless Numbers:**

- **Reynolds number** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ represents the ratio of inertial to viscous forces. It classifies the flow regime (laminar < 2300, turbulent > 10,000). *(1 mark)*

- **Prandtl number** $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{C_p \mu}{k}$ represents the ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity. It is a fluid property only. Gases ≈ 0.7, water ≈ 7 at 20 °C, oils ≈ 100–1000. *(1 mark)*

- **Nusselt number** $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{h D}{k}$ is the dimensionless heat transfer coefficient, representing the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer. It is the **goal** of all dimensionless correlations. *(1 mark)*

#### (b) A stainless-steel plate heat exchanger has hot water at 90 °C flowing on one side and cold milk at 10 °C on the other. The plate thickness is 1.2 mm, $k_{ss} = 16$ W/(m·K), $h_{\text{water}} = 2500$ W/(m²·K), $h_{\text{milk}} = 800$ W/(m²·K). If the milk-side fouling factor is $0.0006$ m²·K/W, calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the resistance equation** *(1 mark)*:

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_{\text{water}}} + \dfrac{x}{k_{ss}} + R_{f,\text{milk}} + \dfrac{1}{h_{\text{milk}}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term** *(3 marks)*:

- $\dfrac{1}{h_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{1}{2500} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W
- $\dfrac{x}{k_{ss}} = \dfrac{0.0012}{16} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²·K/W
- $R_{f,\text{milk}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W
- $\dfrac{1}{h_{\text{milk}}} = \dfrac{1}{800} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$ m²·K/W

**Step 3 — Sum the resistances** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{U} &= 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 7.5 \times 10^{-5} + 6.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
&= 2.325 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate U** *(1 mark)*:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2.325 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 430 \; \text{W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{K)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **dominant resistance** is the milk-side convective resistance ($1.25 \times 10^{-3}$), which is **~54 %** of the total. Improving the milk-side flow (higher h) would yield the biggest gain in U.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### (a) Derive the expression for the Nusselt number for laminar film condensation on a vertical plate, starting from the energy balance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup** *(1 mark)*: Consider a vertical plate at $T_w$ in contact with saturated vapor at $T_{\text{sat}}$. A thin condensate film of thickness $\delta(y)$ flows downward.

**Step 2 — Velocity profile from momentum balance** *(2 marks)*: Applying the Navier–Stokes equation in the film with no inertia (thin film, low Re) yields a parabolic velocity profile:

$$u(y) = \frac{\rho \, g}{\mu} \left( \delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate at position y** *(1 mark)*:

$$\dot{m} = \int_0^{\delta} \rho \, u \, dy = \frac{\rho^{2} g \delta^{3}}{3 \mu}$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance** *(1 mark)*: Heat conducted across the film at the wall equals the latent heat carried by the condensate:

$$h_y \, \Delta T = \rho \, u_{\text{avg}} \, \frac{d}{dy} \left( \frac{h_{fg} \, \delta^{3}}{3 \mu} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Integrate to find $\delta(y)$** *(1 mark)*:

$$\delta(y) = \left[ \frac{4 \, \mu \, k \, \Delta T \, y}{g \, \rho^{2} \, h_{fg}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 6 — Average heat transfer coefficient** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\boxed{\;h = 0.725 \left[ \frac{\rho \, g \, (\rho - \rho_v) \, h_{fg} \, k^{3}}{\mu \, L \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}\;}
$$

#### (b) Steam at 1 atm condenses on a vertical tube of length 1.5 m. The surface temperature is 95 °C. Using the Nusselt correlation, estimate the average heat transfer coefficient. Given: $\rho_l = 960$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.6$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.82 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.68$ W/(m·K), $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $\Delta T = 5$ K. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute values** *(2 marks)*:

$$
h = 0.725 \left[ \frac{960 \times 9.81 \times (960 - 0.6) \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times (0.68)^{3}}{2.82 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 \times 5} \right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator computation** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= 960 \times 9.81 \times 959.4 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 0.3144 \\[2pt]
&\approx 6.40 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator computation** *(1 mark)*:

$$\text{Den} = 2.82 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 \times 5 = 2.115 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio** *(1 mark)*:

$$\dfrac{\text{Num}}{\text{Den}} \approx 3.025 \times 10^{15}$$

**Step 5 — Take the 1/4 power and multiply by 0.725** *(1 mark)*:

$$
h = 0.725 \times (3.025 \times 10^{15})^{0.25} \approx 0.725 \times 7.43 \times 10^{3} \approx 5{,}390 \; \text{W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{K)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is consistent with the **typical range of 5,000 – 10,000 W/(m²·K)** for steam condensation — confirming that steam-side coefficients dominate overall **U** in jacketed food-processing equipment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Units of fouling factor:** Students often write $R_f$ without the prime (per unit area) symbol. Always use **m²·K/W**.
> 2. **Choosing the correct exponent in Dittus–Boelter:** Heating uses $n = 0.4$, cooling uses $n = 0.3$. Mixing these up costs **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring the validity range:** Applying Dittus–Boelter at $\text{Re} < 10{,}000$ is a common error — the correlation is **only valid for fully developed turbulent flow**.
> 4. **Forgetting area consistency:** When tube areas differ on inside and outside, use $A_i, A_m, A_o$ correctly with log-mean area. For plane walls, the **same A** applies on both sides.
> 5. **Phase-change correlations:** Do not apply the Dittus–Boelter correlation to boiling or condensation — the physics is entirely different.
> 6. **Property selection:** Always use fluid properties at the **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{\text{wall}} + T_{\text{bulk}})/2$ unless otherwise stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Heat transfer coefficient (h)** is the convective proportionality constant; **U** combines all resistances in series.
- **Eight key factors:** velocity, fluid properties, geometry, flow regime, ΔT, phase change, surface conditions, orientation.
- **Dittus–Boelter** (heating, turbulent): $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{0.4}$.
- **Sieder–Tate** (viscosity-corrected, turbulent): $\text{Nu} = 0.027 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$.
- **Laminar tube flow:** $\text{Nu} = 1.86 \, \text{Re}^{1/3} \text{Pr}^{1/3} (D/L)^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$.
- **Natural convection (vertical plate, laminar):** $\text{Nu} = 0.59 \, \text{Gr}^{1/4}$.
- **Natural convection (vertical plate, turbulent):** $\text{Nu} = 0.10 \, \text{Gr}^{1/3}$.
- **Nusselt condensation (vertical plate):** $h = 0.725 \left[ \rho g (\rho - \rho_v) h_{fg} k^{3} / (\mu L \Delta T) \right]^{1/4}$.
- **Boiling regimes:** Natural Convection → Nucleate (peak h) → Transition → Film (low h, high radiation).
- **Overall U** for a fouled plane wall: $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i}^{\prime} + \dfrac{x}{k} + R_{f,o}^{\prime} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$.
- **Order of magnitude of h:** gases 5–250 < natural conv. < forced liquid 100–20,000 < condensing/boiling 5,000–100,000 W/(m²·K).
- **Velocity exponent:** $h \propto v^{0.5}$ (laminar) to $v^{0.8}$ (turbulent).
- **Fouling** is the **silent killer** of heat transfer in food processing — always check $R_f$ for dairy, sugar, and protein-rich streams.
- **Dominant resistance** controls system performance — identify it before optimizing.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Memory Hook:** Always remember the *dimensionless ladder* — properties give Pr → flow gives Re → correlation gives Nu → solve for h. This is the path every KTU problem follows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Boiling and Condensation, Importance of Boilers and Condensers in the Food Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Boiling and Condensation, Importance of Boilers and Condensers in the Food Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Boiling — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Boiling** is the process of heat transfer in which a liquid undergoes a phase change to its vapor state at the **saturation temperature** corresponding to the prevailing pressure, accompanied by the formation of **vapor bubbles** at a heated surface. The process is a *convective* mode of heat transfer that incorporates both **latent heat transport** and **bulk fluid motion**.

In food processing, boiling is often coupled with **concentration** (as in open-pan boiling of milk, syrup, or jam) or used as a heat source for **sterilization** (autoclaves, retorts) and **blanching** operations.

## 1.2 Condensation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Condensation** is the reverse process in which a vapor (saturated or superheated) is brought into contact with a surface at a temperature **below the saturation temperature**, leading to a phase change into liquid with the release of **latent heat of vaporization**. The released heat must be continuously removed by the cooler surface.

In the food industry, condensation is the workhorse in **multi-effect evaporators**, **steam-heated jacketed kettles**, **distillation columns** for ethanol and essential oils, and **refrigeration cycles** used in cold storage and freeze-drying.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

| Phenomenon | Everyday Analogy | Industrial Food Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Boiling** | Watching bubbles rise in a kettle of water on a gas stove | Steam generation in the boiler of a dairy plant to heat milk |
| **Condensation** | Water droplets forming on the outside of a cold glass of juice on a hot day | Vapor from an evaporator condensing on the tubes of a calandria in a sugar refinery |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Food Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Almost **75% of the energy** consumed in a typical food processing plant (dairy, fruit juice, brewing, sugar) is transferred across a **boiling or condensation boundary**. The efficiency of these phase-change processes directly determines **product quality** (Maillard browning, vitamin retention, aroma retention) and **operating cost** (steam economy, fuel cost).

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants Recap

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water, $h_{fg} \approx 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$** at **100 °C, 1 atm**
- **Standard gravity**, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Surface tension of water**, $\sigma \approx 0.0589 \text{ N/m}$ at 100 °C
- **Density of water**, $\rho_l \approx 957.9 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 100 °C
- **Density of saturated steam**, $\rho_v \approx 0.5956 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 100 °C

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Boiling

Boiling phenomena are classified along **two primary axes** that the KTU examiner loves to test:

### (A) By Bulk Fluid Motion

1. **Pool Boiling** — The heated surface is submerged in a *quiescent* (stagnant or gently moving) bulk liquid. The fluid motion is driven **only by buoyancy and bubble-induced agitation**.
2. **Flow Boiling (Convective Boiling)** — The bulk fluid is **forced** over the heated surface by an external pump or pressure difference, *in addition to* the bubble dynamics.

### (B) By Saturation State of the Liquid

1. **Saturated Boiling** — The bulk liquid is at its **saturation temperature** ($T_{sat}$). The excess temperature $\Delta T_e = T_s - T_{sat}$ is the only driving force.
2. **Subcooled Boiling** — The bulk liquid is at a temperature **below** $T_{sat}$. Bubbles form on the surface but **condense in the subcooled bulk**.

## 2.2 The Boiling Curve — *The Most Important Diagram in This Module*

The **boiling curve** plots the wall **heat flux** ($q''$) on the y-axis against the **excess temperature** ($\Delta T_e$) on the x-axis. It contains **four distinct regimes** separated by two critical points:

```
         q''
          │           Stable Film Boiling (D → E)
          │          ╱
          │         ╱  ← D = LEIDENFROST POINT (minimum heat flux)
          │        ╱
   CHF ───┤ ─ ─ ─╱  ← C = CRITICAL HEAT FLUX (BURN-OUT)
          │     ╱
          │    ╱ Transition Boiling (unstable)
          │   ╱
          │  ╱
          │ ╱ Nucleate Boiling (B → C)
          │╱
          │ Natural Convection (A → B)
          └──────────────────────────── ΔT_e
          0  5  10  20  30  50   100  200 °C
```

### Regime-by-Regime Breakdown

**Regime A → B: Natural Convection (Single-Phase Liquid Convection)**
- No bubbles. Heat transfer is by classical free convection.
- Governing correlation: $Nu = C (Gr \cdot Pr)^n$
- Corresponds to $\Delta T_e < 4 \text{ °C}$ for water at 1 atm.

**Regime B → C: Nucleate Boiling**
- Bubbles nucleate at **active cavities** on the heated surface.
- Two sub-categories:
  - **B → B′ : Isolated Bubble Regime** — bubbles are small and sparse.
  - **B′ → C : Slug/Slush Regime** — bubbles coalesce into columns.
- This is the **designer’s operating regime** — high $q''$ at relatively low $\Delta T_e$.
- Governed by the **Rohsenow Correlation** (see formula sheet).

**Point C: Critical Heat Flux (CHF) / Burn-Out Point**
- $q''_{max}$ represents the **upper limit** of useful nucleate boiling.
- Beyond this point, the surface is blanketed by a vapor film; the surface temperature must **jump sharply** to sustain the same heat flux — often leading to **catastrophic heater failure** in food industry boilers if not properly designed.

**Regime C → D: Transition Boiling (Unstable)**
- A combination of nucleate and film boiling; small unstable patches of liquid contact the surface.
- Operationally avoided in food equipment.

**Point D: Leidenfrost Point (Minimum Heat Flux)**
- The heat flux reaches a *minimum* as the surface is fully covered by a stable vapor layer.
- Below D, the surface is "superheated" but the film conducts heat poorly.

**Regime D → E: Stable Film Boiling**
- A continuous, *laminar or turbulent*, vapor film blankets the surface.
- Heat is transferred by **conduction through the vapor** plus radiation at higher $\Delta T_e$.
- Governed by **Bromley’s equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always label the boiling curve with all five points (A, B, C, D, E) and identify the *CHF (burn-out)* and *Leidenfrost* points. Examiners award 2–3 marks purely for correctly sketching and labeling the curve.

## 2.3 The Two Operating Limits — Equations

**Kutateladze–Zuber CHF Correlation** (most widely used):

$$q''_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Berenson’s Minimum Heat Flux (Leidenfrost Point):**

$$q''_{min} = 0.09 \, \rho_v \, h_{fg} \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

## 2.4 Classification of Condensation

| Type | Description | Heat Transfer Coefficient (h) | Application in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Film Condensation** | Liquid wets the surface and forms a continuous film that drains by gravity | Lower (~$10^3$ W/m²K) | All industrial steam condensers, evaporator calandrias |
| **Dropwise Condensation** | Liquid forms discrete droplets that grow, coalesce, and roll off | Higher (~$10^4$–$10^5$ W/m²K, 5–10× film) | Rare in industry (surface treatment required) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why film condensation dominates in food industry:** Most process fluids (water, milk, fruit juice vapors) **wet metal surfaces** (stainless steel) — a thermodynamic requirement for film formation. Promoting dropwise condensation requires exotic coatings (e.g., PTFE, oleophobic films) that are **not food-grade compliant**.

### Two Geometries for Film Condensation Analysis

1. **Vertical plate / vertical tube** — analyzed by **Nusselt (1916)**
2. **Horizontal tube (single or bank of N tubes)** — modified Nusselt analysis

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nucleate boiling** (Rohsenow) | $\dfrac{q''}{\mu_l h_{fg}}\left[\dfrac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2} = \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l}\Delta T_e}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_l^n}\right]^3$ | $n = 1.0$ for water, $n = 1.7$ for most other fluids |
| 2 | **CHF** (Kutateladze–Zuber) | $q''_{max} = 0.149\,h_{fg}\,\rho_v\!\left[\dfrac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | W/m² |
| 3 | **Min heat flux** (Berenson) | $q''_{min} = 0.09\,\rho_v h_{fg}\!\left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | W/m² |
| 4 | **Film boiling** (Bromley, vertical plate) | $\bar{h} = 0.943\!\left[\dfrac{k_v^3 \rho_v(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h''_{fg}}{L\,\mu_v\,(T_s - T_{sat})}\right]^{1/4}$ | $h''_{fg} = h_{fg} + 0.68\,c_{p,v}(T_s - T_{sat})$ |
| 5 | **Film condensation on vertical plate** (Nusselt) | $\bar{h} = 0.943\!\left[\dfrac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}\,k_l^3}{\mu_l\,L\,(T_{sat} - T_s)}\right]^{1/4}$ | Laminar wavy-laminar film, $\text{Re}_\delta < 30$ |
| 6 | **Nusselt No. for vertical plate** | $\bar{Nu}_L = \dfrac{\bar{h}\,L}{k_l} = 0.943\left[\dfrac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}\,L^3}{\mu_l\,k_l\,\Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Film condensation on horizontal tube** | $\bar{h} = 0.729\!\left[\dfrac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}\,k_l^3}{\mu_l\,(T_{sat} - T_s)\,D}\right]^{1/4}$ | Single tube |
| 8 | **N horizontal tubes in a vertical tier** | $\bar{h}_N = \bar{h}_1 \, N^{-1/4}$ | Bank correction |
| 9 | **Reynolds number of condensate film** | $Re_\delta = \dfrac{4\,\dot{m}}{\mu_l\,P} = \dfrac{4\,\Gamma}{\mu_l}$ | $\Gamma$ = mass flow rate per unit width |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nusselt’s Theory of Laminar Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU University Examinations for this module. A 7-mark question is regularly asked. **Practice writing it cold.**

### Problem Statement

Saturated vapor at temperature $T_{sat}$ condenses on a cold vertical plate of height $L$ whose surface temperature is $T_s < T_{sat}$. The condensate drains down as a thin laminar film. Derive an expression for the **average heat transfer coefficient** $\bar{h}$.

### Schematic and Coordinate System

Consider a vertical plate of width $W$ and height $L$. The $x$-axis is measured **downward** from the leading edge. The film thickness at position $x$ is $\delta(x)$.

```
  T_sat ─►─────────────────► Vapor (stagnant)
          \  T(x)  /
           \─────/  δ(x)  ← condensate film
            \   /         (laminar, gravity-driven)
             \ /
              ↓  x (downward)
              ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  Heated plate at T_s
```

### Assumptions (KTU examiners expect these listed)

1. Steady-state, **laminar** flow of the condensate.
2. The vapor is **quiescent** and at $T_{sat}$ (no shear at liquid–vapor interface).
3. Constant fluid properties evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$.
4. The liquid is **incompressible**; body force is gravity in the $+x$ direction.
5. The film is **thin** so that the momentum boundary layer approximation applies ($\partial p / \partial x = 0$).
6. **Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction** (since $v_x$ and $v_y$ components are small, convection in the $y$-direction is negligible).
7. Negligible subcooling of the liquid — sensible cooling of the condensate is ignored.

### Governing Equations

**Momentum equation** (boundary layer, $y$-direction):

$$\rho_l \left( v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} \right) = \mu_l \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \rho_l g$$

**Continuity:**

$$\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Energy equation** (boundary layer, conduction-dominated across film):

$$\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

### Step 1: Solve the Energy Equation

Integrating $\partial^2 T / \partial y^2 = 0$ twice with respect to $y$:

$$T(y) = C_1 y + C_2$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- At $y = 0$ (plate): $T = T_s$
- At $y = \delta(x)$ (interface): $T = T_{sat}$

Solving:

$$T(y) = T_s + (T_{sat} - T_s) \cdot \frac{y}{\delta(x)}$$

The **local heat flux** at the wall by Fourier’s law:

$$q''(x) = k_l \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta(x)}$$

So the local heat transfer coefficient is:

$$h_x = \frac{q''(x)}{T_{sat} - T_s} = \frac{k_l}{\delta(x)}$$

### Step 2: Mass Flow Rate of Condensate

The mass flow rate of condensate accumulating between $0$ and $x$ equals the integrated heat removed:

$$\dot{m}(x) = \frac{1}{h_{fg}} \int_0^x q''(x) \, W \, dx = \frac{W}{h_{fg}} \int_0^x \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta(x)} \, dx$$

But the local condensate mass flow rate per unit width is also expressible as:

$$\Gamma(x) = \rho_l \int_0^{\delta(x)} v_x \, dy$$

Equating (mass balance) and noting that the *average* velocity is the integral of $v_x$ over $\delta$:

$$\frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{q''(x)}{h_{fg}} = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{h_{fg}\,\delta(x)}$$

### Step 3: Solve the Momentum Equation

For a thin film, the **inertia terms** $v_x \partial v_x / \partial x$ and $v_y \partial v_x / \partial y$ are small compared to gravity and viscosity. So the momentum equation **reduces to**:

$$\mu_l \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} = -\rho_l g$$

Integrating **twice** with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} = -\frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l} y + C_3$$

$$v_x(y) = -\frac{\rho_l g}{2\mu_l} y^2 + C_3 y + C_4$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- No-slip at the wall: $v_x(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_4 = 0$
- Negligible vapor shear at $y = \delta$: $\partial v_x / \partial y \big|_{y=\delta} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_3 = \dfrac{\rho_l g \delta}{\mu_l}$

Therefore:

$$v_x(y) = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l} \left( \delta y - \frac{y^2}{2} \right)$$

### Step 4: Local Mass Flow Rate per Unit Width

$$\Gamma(x) = \rho_l \int_0^{\delta} v_x \, dy = \rho_l \int_0^{\delta} \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy$$

$$\Gamma(x) = \frac{\rho_l^2 g}{\mu_l} \left[ \frac{\delta y^2}{2} - \frac{y^3}{6} \right]_0^\delta = \frac{\rho_l^2 g}{\mu_l}\left(\frac{\delta^3}{2} - \frac{\delta^3}{6}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\Gamma(x) = \frac{\rho_l^2 g \, \delta^3(x)}{3 \mu_l}\;}$$

### Step 5: Combine Mass and Energy Balances

From the differential mass balance:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\rho_l^2 g \, \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}\right) = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{h_{fg}\,\delta}$$

Differentiate the LHS with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\rho_l^2 g \, \delta^2}{\mu_l} \frac{d\delta}{dx} = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{h_{fg}\,\delta}$$

Separate variables:

$$\delta^3 \, d\delta = \frac{k_l \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\rho_l^2 g \, h_{fg}} \, dx$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $\delta = 0$) to $x = x$:

$$\frac{\delta^4(x)}{4} = \frac{k_l \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\rho_l^2 g \, h_{fg}} \, x$$

$$\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 k_l \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, x}{\rho_l^2 g \, h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

### Step 6: Local Heat Transfer Coefficient

$$h_x = \frac{k_l}{\delta(x)} = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k_l^3}{4 \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, x}\right]^{1/4}$$

### Step 7: Average Heat Transfer Coefficient over the Plate

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L h_x \, dx = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \frac{k_l}{\delta(x)} dx$$

Substituting $\delta(x)$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g\, h_{fg}\, k_l^3}{4\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}\right]^{1/4} x^{-1/4}\, dx$$

The integral $\int_0^L x^{-1/4}\, dx = \frac{4}{3} L^{3/4}$, so:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \cdot \frac{4}{3} L^{3/4} \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g\, h_{fg}\, k_l^3}{4\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\bar{h} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{4^{1/4}} \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g\, h_{fg}\, k_l^3}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) L}\right]^{1/4}$$

Since $\frac{4}{3 \cdot 4^{1/4}} = \frac{4^{3/4}}{3} = \frac{2^{3/2}}{3} \approx 0.943$:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\, g\, h_{fg}\, k_l^3}{\mu_l\, L\, (T_{sat} - T_s)}\right]^{1/4}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Nusselt number form is obtained by multiplying both sides by $L/k_l$:
>
> $$\bar{Nu}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\, g\, h_{fg}\, L^3}{\mu_l\, k_l\, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-style 7-mark problem)

> **Problem (KTU 2019 Scheme, Modified):** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure ($T_{sat} = 100$ °C) condenses on a vertical stainless-steel plate of height $L = 0.4$ m, maintained at $T_s = 80$ °C. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient and the total rate of heat transfer. Use $k_l = 0.679 \text{ W/m·K}$, $\mu_l = 279 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·s/m}^2$, $\rho_l = 961.9 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_v = 0.5956 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Nusselt formula:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{(961.9)(961.9 - 0.5956)(9.81)(2257 \times 10^3)(0.679)^3}{(279 \times 10^{-6})(0.4)(20)}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator term-by-term:**

$$\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v) = 961.9 \times 961.3 = 9.247 \times 10^5 \text{ kg}^2/\text{m}^6$$

$$\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g = 9.247 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 = 9.071 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}^2/\text{m}^5\text{·s}^2$$

$$h_{fg}\,k_l^3 = 2257 \times 10^3 \times (0.679)^3 = 2257 \times 10^3 \times 0.3131 = 7.067 \times 10^5 \text{ W}^3\text{·s/kg·m·K}^3$$

**Numerator:**

$$9.071 \times 10^6 \times 7.067 \times 10^5 = 6.411 \times 10^{12}$$

**Denominator:**

$$\mu_l L \Delta T = 279 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.4 \times 20 = 2.232 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{6.411 \times 10^{12}}{2.232 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.873 \times 10^{15}$$

**Step 4 — Take the fourth root and multiply by 0.943:**

$$(2.873 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = 2317.4 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \times 2317.4 = 2185 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Total rate of heat transfer** (for $W = 1$ m width):

$$Q = \bar{h}\,A\,\Delta T = 2185 \times (0.4 \times 1) \times 20 = 17{,}480 \text{ W} \approx 17.48 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\;\bar{h} \approx 2185 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}, \quad Q \approx 17.48 \text{ kW per metre width}\;}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define pool boiling. With a neat sketch, identify the four regimes of pool boiling and the two critical points on the boiling curve.

**Model Answer:**

**Pool boiling** is a mode of convective heat transfer in which a stationary or gently moving body of liquid is in contact with a heated surface whose temperature is above the saturation temperature, leading to bubble formation, growth, and detachment from the surface.

The **four regimes** are:

1. **Natural Convection (A–B):** No bubbles; heat transfer by free convection. $\Delta T_e < 4$ °C for water at 1 atm.
2. **Nucleate Boiling (B–C):** Bubbles nucleate at active cavities; high heat flux at moderate $\Delta T_e$. Governed by Rohsenow.
3. **Transition Boiling (C–D):** Unstable; combination of nucleate and film; **avoided operationally**.
4. **Stable Film Boiling (D–E):** Continuous vapor film blankets the surface; heat transfer by conduction through vapor and radiation. Governed by Bromley.

**Two critical points:**
- **Point C: Critical Heat Flux (CHF) / Burn-out** — upper limit of nucleate boiling.
- **Point D: Leidenfrost Point (Minimum Heat Flux)** — stable film boiling begins.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Compare film condensation and dropwise condensation. Why is film condensation more commonly encountered in food processing?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Film Condensation | Dropwise Condensation |
|---|---|---|
| Wetting | Complete wetting of surface | Non-wetting |
| Liquid morphology | Continuous film drains by gravity | Discrete droplets coalesce and roll off |
| Heat transfer coefficient $\bar{h}$ | Lower ($10^2$–$10^3$ W/m²K) | Much higher ($10^4$–$10^5$ W/m²K) |
| Thermal resistance | High (entire film) | Low (only thin droplet–surface interface) |
| Surface requirement | Plain metal | Requires non-wettable coating (e.g., PTFE, oleic acid) |
| Occurrence in food industry | Common | Rare |

**Reason film condensation dominates in food industry:**
1. Most food-grade process fluids (water, milk vapor, fruit juice vapor) **wet stainless steel** due to favorable surface energy.
2. Promoting dropwise condensation requires **non-food-grade coatings** (PTFE, silicones, long-chain fatty acids) that are **not compliant** with FDA/CFSSI/EU food-contact regulations.
3. Dropwise coatings **degrade quickly** under repeated thermal cycling, mechanical cleaning (CIP), and exposure to food acids.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### CHOICE QUESTION

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Saturated steam at **1 atm** condenses on a vertical plate of height $L = 0.5$ m whose surface temperature is $T_s = 75$ °C. Estimate the **average heat transfer coefficient** using Nusselt’s analysis. Properties of water at film temperature $T_f = 87.5$ °C: $k_l = 0.668$ W/m·K, $\mu_l = 329 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s/m², $\rho_l = 966.6$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.6382$ kg/m³, $h_{fg} = 2294$ kJ/kg.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the **Nusselt correlation** for film condensation on a vertical plate, clearly stating all assumptions.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat labeled sketch, explain the **pool boiling curve**. Define CHF and the Leidenfrost point. State the **Kutateladze–Zuber** equation for CHF.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of condensers** used in food industry. Discuss the importance of condensers in **(i)** multi-effect evaporators and **(ii)** aroma recovery in fruit juice concentration.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### Part (a) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $T_{sat} = 100$ °C, $T_s = 75$ °C, $L = 0.5$ m
- $\Delta T = 25$ °C
- $k_l = 0.668$ W/m·K, $\mu_l = 329 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s/m²
- $\rho_l = 966.6$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.6382$ kg/m³
- $h_{fg} = 2294 \times 10^3$ J/kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Formula:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}\,k_l^3}{\mu_l L \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator components:**

$$\rho_l - \rho_v = 966.6 - 0.6382 = 965.96 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v) = 966.6 \times 965.96 = 9.336 \times 10^5$$

$$\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v) \cdot g = 9.336 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 = 9.159 \times 10^6$$

$$h_{fg} \cdot k_l^3 = 2294 \times 10^3 \times (0.668)^3 = 2294 \times 10^3 \times 0.2980 = 6.836 \times 10^5$$

**Numerator:** $9.159 \times 10^6 \times 6.836 \times 10^5 = 6.261 \times 10^{12}$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$\mu_l L \Delta T = 329 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 \times 25 = 4.113 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{6.261 \times 10^{12}}{4.113 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.522 \times 10^{15}$$

**Step 4 — Fourth root and final answer:**

$$(1.522 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = 1972 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \times 1972 = 1860 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{\;\bar{h} \approx 1860 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}\;}$$

#### Part (b) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Statement:** Derive the Nusselt correlation for **laminar film condensation** on a cold vertical plate of height $L$, surface temperature $T_s$, exposed to saturated vapor at $T_{sat}$.

**Assumptions (write each in the exam):**
1. Steady-state, laminar condensate flow.
2. Quiescent vapor; negligible interfacial shear.
3. Constant properties at film temperature $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$.
4. Thin film — momentum BL approximation valid.
5. Heat transfer across film is **pure conduction** (convection across $y$ negligible).
6. Subcooling of condensate ignored.
7. Negligible vapor momentum effects.

**Step 1 — Energy equation across film:** With $v_y \approx 0$ and no viscous dissipation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; T(y) = T_s + (T_{sat} - T_s)\frac{y}{\delta(x)}$$

Local heat flux: $q''(x) = k_l(T_{sat} - T_s)/\delta(x)$

**Step 2 — Mass balance** (condensate accumulating as film descends):

$$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\int_0^\delta \rho_l v_x\, dy\right) = \frac{q''(x)}{h_{fg}}$$

**Step 3 — Momentum equation** (inertia terms neglected for thin film):

$$\mu_l \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} = -\rho_l g$$

With $v_x(0) = 0$ and $\partial v_x/\partial y \big|_\delta = 0$:

$$v_x(y) = \frac{\rho_l g}{\mu_l}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Local mass flow per unit width:**

$$\Gamma(x) = \rho_l \int_0^\delta v_x\, dy = \frac{\rho_l^2 g \delta^3}{3\mu_l}$$

**Step 5 — Combine with mass balance:**

$$\frac{\rho_l^2 g \delta^2}{\mu_l} \frac{d\delta}{dx} = \frac{k_l(T_{sat} - T_s)}{h_{fg}\,\delta}$$

Integrate with $\delta(0) = 0$:

$$\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 k_l \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, x}{\rho_l^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 6 — Local and average h:**

$$h_x = \frac{k_l}{\delta(x)} = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g\, h_{fg}\, k_l^3}{4\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, x}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L h_x\, dx = \frac{4}{3 \cdot 4^{1/4}}\left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g\, h_{fg}\, k_l^3}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)\, L}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\boxed{\;\bar{h} = 0.943\left[\frac{\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v)\,g\,h_{fg}\,k_l^3}{\mu_l\,(T_{sat} - T_s)\,L}\right]^{1/4}\;}$$

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### Part (a) — Pool Boiling Curve (7 Marks)

**Sketch the boiling curve** (axes: $\Delta T_e$ horizontal, $q''$ vertical):

- **A → B:** Natural convection; no bubbles; correlation $q'' \sim (\Delta T_e)^{5/4}$
- **B → C:** Nucleate boiling; bubbles form and detach; $q'' \sim (\Delta T_e)^3$ (Rohsenow)
- **C:** **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** — maximum heat flux nucleate boiling can sustain. Above this, the surface overheats rapidly. In food industry boilers, CHF must never be exceeded or heater tubes will burn out.
- **C → D:** Transition boiling; unstable; mixture of nucleate and film.
- **D:** **Leidenfrost Point** — minimum heat flux point; vapor film fully blankets the surface.
- **D → E:** Stable film boiling; governed by Bromley.

**Kutateladze–Zuber CHF equation:**

$$q''_{max} = 0.149\,h_{fg}\,\rho_v\left[\frac{\sigma\,g\,(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

#### Part (b) — Classification of Condensers & Food Industry Importance (7 Marks)

**Classification of condensers used in food industry:**

1. **Surface (shell-and-tube) condensers** — cooling water or refrigerant separated from process vapor by a tube wall; common in multi-effect evaporators.
2. **Contact condensers** — direct mixing of coolant and vapor; used for vacuum systems (e.g., barometric condensers in sugar vacuum pans).
3. **Jet condensers** — vapor condensed by direct contact with a counter-current spray of cold liquid.
4. **Refrigerant condensers** — air-cooled or water-cooled, used in chillers, cold storage, freeze-dryers.
5. **Cryogenic (cold-finger) condensers** — used for **aroma recovery**; condense volatile flavor compounds at very low temperatures.

**Importance in food industry:**

**(i) Multi-effect evaporators:**
- The vapor generated in one effect is the **heating medium** for the next effect.
- Condensers at the **last effect** maintain vacuum and condense the secondary vapor, enabling reuse of latent heat and **steam economy** of 0.4–0.7 kg water evaporated per kg steam.
- For milk concentration (from 12% to 48% TS) and orange juice (from 11% to 65% TSS), condensers are critical for product quality and energy efficiency.

**(ii) Aroma recovery in fruit juice:**
- During juice concentration, volatile aroma compounds (esters, aldehydes, terpenes) are stripped with water vapor.
- A **cryogenic condenser** at –30 to –80 °C condenses these volatiles **separately** from water, allowing them to be **added back** to the concentrate before packaging.
- This preserves the characteristic flavor of the fresh fruit — critical for orange, apple, and grape juice concentrates.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in Boiling & Condensation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include $(\rho_l - \rho_v)$** in the Nusselt formula — examiners deduct **1 mark** for this. Many students write only $\rho_l$.
> 2. **Using $\rho_v$ instead of $\rho_l$** in the CHF formula — the Kutateladze–Zuber equation has $\rho_v$ *outside* and $(\rho_l - \rho_v)$ inside the bracket. Swapping these is a common error.
> 3. **Wrong sign in the temperature difference** — write $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s$ (positive value) consistently in film condensation. Some students write $T_s - T_{sat}$ and then take the absolute value, leading to confusion in the units.
> 4. **Confusing "boiling" with "evaporation"** — evaporation occurs at the *free surface* (vapor pressure driven), boiling occurs at a *heated surface* (nucleation driven). This distinction is worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in the Nusselt derivation — KTU examiners allocate **2–3 marks** specifically for the assumption list. Do not skip it.
> 6. **Mixing up CHF and Leidenfrost** — CHF (point C) is the *maximum* heat flux in nucleate boiling; Leidenfrost (point D) is the *minimum* heat flux in film boiling. Confusing them loses **2 marks**.
> 7. **Unit mismatch** in the energy balance — always use **SI units** (W, J, m, s, kg) consistently. Mixing kcal and kJ is a classic error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Boiling & Condensation in Food Processing**

### Quick-Fire Definitions
- **Boiling:** Phase change from liquid to vapor at a heated surface, with bubble formation.
- **Condensation:** Phase change from vapor to liquid at a cooled surface, releasing latent heat.
- **Pool boiling:** Stagnant bulk liquid over a heated surface.
- **Flow boiling:** Forced convection of liquid over a heated surface.
- **Film condensation:** Liquid forms a continuous draining film (most common in food industry).
- **Dropwise condensation:** Discrete droplets (rare in food industry due to coating issues).

### Boiling Curve — Remember the 5 Points
- **A → B:** Natural convection
- **B → C:** Nucleate boiling *(safe operating regime)*
- **C:** **CHF = Burn-out** (maximum)
- **C → D:** Transition *(unstable, avoid)*
- **D:** **Leidenfrost = minimum heat flux**
- **D → E:** Stable film boiling

### Three Equations You Must Memorize
1. **Rohsenow (Nucleate Boiling):**
   $q''/(\mu_l h_{fg}) \cdot [\sigma/(g(\rho_l - \rho_v))]^{1/2} = [c_{p,l} \Delta T_e/(C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n)]^3$
2. **Kutateladze–Zuber (CHF):**
   $q''_{max} = 0.149 h_{fg} \rho_v [\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)/\rho_v^2]^{1/4}$
3. **Nusselt (Film Condensation, Vertical Plate):**
   $\bar{h} = 0.943 [\rho_l(\rho_l - \rho_v) g h_{fg} k_l^3 / (\mu_l L \Delta T)]^{1/4}$

### Food Industry Applications — Must Know
- **Boilers:** Steam generation for sterilization, pasteurization, blanching, evaporation, jacketed cooking.
- **Condensers:** Vapor recovery in multi-effect evaporators; aroma recovery in juice concentration; refrigerant condensation in chillers and freeze-dryers; vacuum maintenance in sugar vacuum pans.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Strategy
1. Identify whether the problem is **nucleate boiling**, **film boiling**, or **film condensation** from context.
2. **List all given properties** with units.
3. **Compute** the temperature difference $\Delta T$ first.
4. **Substitute** carefully into the right equation; do not mix formulas.
5. **Check orders of magnitude**: For steam condensation, $\bar{h} \sim 10^3$ W/m²K; for film boiling, $\bar{h} \sim 10^2$ W/m²K.
6. **Report** with proper units and to **3–4 significant figures**.

### Quick-Reference — Property Table
| Property | Water at 100 °C | Steam at 100 °C |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | 957.9 kg/m³ | 0.5956 kg/m³ |
| $h_{fg}$ | 2257 kJ/kg | — |
| $k$ | 0.679 W/m·K | 0.025 W/m·K |
| $\mu$ | 279 × 10⁻⁶ N·s/m² | 12.0 × 10⁻⁶ N·s/m² |
| $c_p$ | 4216 J/kg·K | 2030 J/kg·K |
| $\sigma$ | 0.0589 N/m | — |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionless Numbers Used in Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dimensionless Numbers Used in Heat Transfer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **dimensionless number** (also called a **dimensionless group**, **dimensionless parameter**, or **pi term**) is a pure, unit-less quantity derived from a mathematical combination of physical variables (such as velocity, length, viscosity, thermal conductivity, etc.) whose individual dimensional components cancel out completely. In heat transfer, these numbers represent the **ratios of competing physical phenomena** that govern the transport of thermal energy, momentum, or mass across a system boundary. In the rigorous framework of the **Buckingham Pi Theorem**, any well-posed physical problem involving $n$ variables and $k$ fundamental dimensions can be reduced to $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups. For convective heat transfer in food processing equipment (plate heat exchangers, scraped-surface heat exchangers, agitated vessels, ovens, and retorts), these groups are the backbone of empirical correlations, scale-up procedures, and dimensionless similarity analysis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCFTT402 / Module 2):** Students must be able to (i) identify and write the mathematical form of at least **eight core dimensionless numbers** used in heat transfer, (ii) explain their **physical significance**, and (iii) apply them in **similarity analysis and correlation-based problem solving** for food process engineering. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Race" Intuition Imagine you are baking a tray of cookies in an oven. Three things are happening at the same time: 1. **Hot air rushes over the cookies** (forced convection — momentum carrying heat). 2. **The metal tray conducts heat from its surface to the cookie center** (conduction through solid). 3. **Hot air naturally rises above the oven floor** because it is lighter (natural/free convection). Now ask: *which effect is winning the race at this instant?* - If hot air flow is dominant → the **Reynolds Number** is high. - If conduction inside the cookie body is dominant → the **Biot Number** is large. - If natural buoyancy dominates → the **Grashof Number** is high. A **dimensionless number is simply the scoreboard of that race**. It tells you, with a single pure number, which physical mechanism is dominant, which can be neglected, and how you can scale up a small laboratory experiment to predict the behaviour of a 10,000-litre industrial pasteurizer without ever running it. > [!NOTE] > **Why "dimensionless"?** Because when you divide *force A* by *force B*, the units (kg, m, s) cancel. The resulting number is "pure" — it has the same meaning whether the experiment was done in Kerala, Canada, or on Mars. This **universality** is what allows one laboratory correlation to predict full-scale industrial performance. ## 1.3 Classification of Heat-Transfer Dimensionless Numbers For KTU 2024, dimensionless numbers in heat transfer are classified under four physical contexts: | Category | Purpose | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Momentum / Flow characterisation** | Distinguish laminar, transitional, turbulent flow | Reynolds Number (Re) | | **Thermal property characterisation** | Compare thermal vs. momentum diffusion | Prandtl Number (Pr), Peclet Number (Pe) | | **Convection regime characterisation** | Forced vs. natural convection dominance | Nusselt (Nu), Grashof (Gr), Rayleigh (Ra) | | **Transient / Internal resistance** | Internal conduction vs. external convection | Biot (Bi), Fourier (Fo) | | **Heat transfer coefficient correlation** | Efficiency of convective transfer | Stanton (St), Colburn (jH) | | **Coupled heat & mass transfer** | Simultaneous drying, evaporation | Lewis (Le), Schmidt (Sc), Sherwood (Sh) | > [!TIP] > **Memorisation Trick (KTU Board favourite):** Remember the chain *R-P-G-N-B-F-S* — Reynolds, Prandtl, Grashof, Nusselt, Biot, Fourier, Stanton.
