# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024):**
> - Part A: Each question carries **3 marks**, requires direct conceptual answer (~50–80 words).
> - Part B: Each question carries **14 marks**, has **internal choice (A or B)**, and is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.
> - Total per module ESE (if module is 2-mark module): 3 × 2 = 6 part-A questions (3 from each module) + 14-mark question.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the mass transfer coefficient. State the units of $k_c$ (concentration-based) and $k_y$ (mole-fraction-based).**

**Model Answer:**
The mass transfer coefficient $k$ is the proportionality constant between the molar flux $N_A$ of a species and the concentration driving force. Formally, $N_A = k \Delta C_{driving}$.

- $k_c$ (based on concentration $\Delta C$ in kmol/m³): units are **m/s**.
- $k_y$ (based on gas mole fraction $\Delta y$, dimensionless): units are **kmol/m²·s**.

Conversion: $k_y = C \cdot k_c$ where $C$ is total molar concentration. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between equimolar counter-diffusion and Stefan (unimolecular) diffusion. Give one food-processing example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | EQUImolar Counter-Diffusion | Stefan (Unidirectional) |
|---|---|---|
| $N_B$ | $= -N_A$ (equal and opposite) | $= 0$ (B stagnant) |
| Bulk flow correction | None | $(1-y_A)$ in denominator |
| Driving force | Pure $\Delta C$ | $\Delta C/(1-y_A)_{lm}$ |
| Example | Distillation of ethanol-water | Drying of foods (water through stagnant air) |

*(3 marks)*

---

### Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the penetration theory of mass transfer. Write the final expression for $k_c$.**

**Model Answer:**
Penetration theory (Higbie, 1935) assumes that turbulent eddies bring a fresh fluid element from the bulk to the interface, where it stays for an average contact time $t_c$. During $t_c$, unsteady-state molecular diffusion occurs.

$$\boxed{k_c = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}} = 1.13\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{t_c}}}$$

The theory gives $k_c \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$, unlike film theory which gives $k_c \propto D_{AB}$. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Sherwood number and Schmidt number. What do they signify physically?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Sherwood number:** $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$. Represents the ratio of **convective mass transfer** to **pure molecular diffusion**.
- **Schmidt number:** $Sc = \mu/(\rho D_{AB}) = \nu/D_{AB}$. Represents the ratio of **momentum diffusivity (kinematic viscosity)** to **mass diffusivity**.

For gases, $Sc \approx 0.6$–$1.2$. For liquids, $Sc \approx 100$–$100{,}000$. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q5. [KTU University Exam — Model]
**What is the Chilton-Colburn analogy? State the j-factor for mass transfer.**

**Model Answer:**
The Chilton-Colburn analogy (1933) states that for turbulent flow over a surface, the dimensionless transport of momentum, heat, and mass are all governed by similar functions of the flow parameters. The **j-factor for mass transfer** is:

$$j_D = \dfrac{St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}}{1} = \dfrac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc^{1/3}} \approx \dfrac{f}{2}$$

It allows us to predict mass transfer coefficients from heat transfer data (or vice versa). *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 marks]** A gas stream containing ammonia (A) is being scrubbed with water in a packed absorption tower operating at 1 atm and 30 °C. The gas-phase individual mass transfer coefficient is $k_G = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s·atm and the liquid-phase individual MTC is $k_L = 0.25$ kmol/m²·s. The equilibrium relation is linear: $y = 1.20\,x$ (where $y$ is gas mole fraction, $x$ is liquid mole fraction). Calculate:
(i) the overall mass transfer coefficients $K_G$ and $K_L$;
(ii) the percentage of total resistance in each phase;
(iii) comment on which phase controls the absorption.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of neat diagrams and equations, compare the **Film Theory** and the **Penetration Theory** of mass transfer. Discuss the applicability of each in food process equipment.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Detailed Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $P = 1$ atm, $T = 30$ °C $= 303$ K
- $k_G = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s·atm
- $k_L = 0.25$ kmol/m²·s
- $m = 1.20$ (slope of equilibrium line $y = mx$)

**Step 1 — Overall MTC based on gas phase $K_G$:**

Using the resistance-in-series model:

$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} + \dfrac{1.20}{0.25} = 285.71 + 4.80 = 290.51$$

$$\boxed{K_G = 3.443 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{·s·atm}}$$

**Step 2 — Overall MTC based on liquid phase $K_L$:**

$$\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{1.20 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3}} + \dfrac{1}{0.25} = \dfrac{1}{4.2 \times 10^{-3}} + 4.0 = 238.10 + 4.0 = 242.10$$

$$\boxed{K_L = 4.130 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{·s (mole-fraction basis)}}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage of resistance:**

Gas-film resistance: $R_G = 1/k_G = 285.71$ → $\dfrac{285.71}{290.51} \times 100 = 98.35\%$

Liquid-film resistance: $R_L = m/k_L = 4.80$ → $\dfrac{4.80}{290.51} \times 100 = 1.65\%$

**Step 4 — Comment:**

The **gas phase controls** the absorption (≈98% of total resistance). To improve performance, we should:
- Increase gas turbulence (higher gas velocity)
- Use smaller, more numerous packing elements
- Increase gas-phase contact area

Increasing liquid flow rate will have negligible effect because liquid resistance is only 1.65%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Students often forget that $K_L$ and $K_G$ use **different** driving forces and have **different units**. Don't substitute $K_L$ into a pressure-based flux equation! If the question asks for flux in kmol/m²·s using a gas-phase driving force, use $K_G$ — not $K_L$. Marks lost: **1–2 marks** per wrong substitution.

---

#### Part (b) — Film vs. Penetration Theory Comparison

**Film Theory (Whitman, 1923):**

- **Assumption:** A thin, stagnant film of thickness $\delta$ exists on each side of the gas-liquid interface. The fluid is well-mixed outside the film. Mass transfer is steady-state molecular diffusion across the film.
- **Mechanism:** Fick's first law applied across the film: $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{\Delta C}{\delta}$
- **Result:** $\boxed{k_c = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta}}$
- **Linearity:** $k_c \propto D_{AB}$ (linear in diffusivity)
- **Applicability:** Best for slow, laminar flow with well-defined interfaces — e.g., absorption in laminar falling films.

**Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935):**

- **Assumption:** Turbulent eddies bring a fresh element of fluid from the bulk to the interface. The element stays for a brief contact time $t_c$, during which unsteady-state molecular diffusion occurs.
- **Mechanism:** Solve Fick's second law with boundary conditions $C(z,0)=C_b$, $C(0,t)=C_s$, $C(\infty,t)=C_b$.
- **Result:** $\boxed{k_c = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}}}$
- **Linearity:** $k_c \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$ (square-root dependence)
- **Applicability:** Best for **short contact times** — e.g., bubble columns, spray dryer droplets, packed columns with bubble bursting.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Feature | Film | Penetration |
|---|---|---|
| $k_c \propto$ | $D_{AB}^1$ | $D_{AB}^{0.5}$ |
| Hydrodynamic parameter | $\delta$ (length) | $t_c$ (time) |
| Time-dependence | Steady | Unsteady (in $t_c$) |
| Best for | Slow, laminar | Turbulent, short contact |
| Food example | Falling-film evaporator | Spray dryer droplet |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Don't just list assumptions — **derive the $k_c$ expression** for at least one of the two theories (Penetration is the easiest to derive). Skipping the derivation costs 2–3 marks. Also, do NOT confuse the two — mixing up the proportionality ($\sqrt{D_{AB}}$ vs. $D_{AB}$) is a guaranteed zero for that sub-part.

---

### QUESTION B — [Alternative Internal Choice, 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 marks]** A drying operation involves the evaporation of water from the surface of a food slab at 60 °C into a large body of stagnant air at 1 atm and 60 °C. The path length of diffusion is 0.005 m. The vapour pressure of water at 60 °C is 0.20 atm, and the partial pressure of water at the top of the stagnant film is 0.02 atm. The diffusivity of water vapour in air at 60 °C is $D_{AB} = 2.78 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of water using the **Stefan equation**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **mass transfer coefficient $k_c$** using the **Penetration Theory** of mass transfer. Clearly state all assumptions and boundary conditions used.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Stefan Diffusion Numerical

**Given:**
- $P = 1$ atm $= 101325$ Pa
- $T = 60 + 273 = 333$ K
- $p_{A,1} = 0.20$ atm $= 0.20 \times 101325 = 20265$ Pa (at water surface)
- $p_{A,2} = 0.02$ atm $= 2026.5$ Pa (at top of stagnant film)
- $\Delta z = 0.005$ m
- $D_{AB} = 2.78 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1 — Apply the Stefan equation:**

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T \cdot z} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{A,2}}{P - p_{A,1}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$\dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T \cdot z} = \dfrac{(2.78 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(333)(0.005)}$$

$$= \dfrac{2.8168}{13.8427} = 0.2035 \text{ mol/m}^2\text{·s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the log term:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - 0.02}{1 - 0.20}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.98}{0.80}\right) = \ln(1.225) = 0.2029$$

**Step 4 — Final flux:**

$$N_A = (0.2035)(0.2029) = 0.04129 \text{ mol/m}^2\text{·s}$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 4.129 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/m}^2\text{·s} = 4.129 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{·s}}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using the (1−y_A) form:**

$$y_{A,1} = 0.20, \quad y_{A,2} = 0.02$$

$$(1-y_A)_{lm} = \dfrac{(1-0.20) - (1-0.02)}{\ln[(0.80)/(0.98)]} = \dfrac{0.18}{0.2029} = 0.8871$$

$$C = P/(RT) = 101325/(8.314 \times 333) = 36.59 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{(1-y_A)_{lm}}\cdot \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta z} = \dfrac{(2.78 \times 10^{-5})(36.59)}{0.8871} \cdot \dfrac{0.18}{0.005}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.0172 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8871} \cdot 36 = (1.1467 \times 10^{-3})(36) = 0.04128 \text{ mol/m}^2\text{·s} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

#### Part (b) — Penetration Theory Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. The mass transfer process is **unsteady-state** within each fluid element.
2. Each element resides at the interface for a **fixed, finite contact time $t_c$**.
3. The element behaves as a **semi-infinite medium** during $t_c$.
4. The surface concentration $C_{A,s}$ remains **constant** during the contact time.

**Governing Equation (Fick's Second Law):**

$$\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

**Boundary & Initial Conditions:**

| Condition | Statement |
|---|---|
| $t = 0$, $z > 0$ | $C_A = C_{A,b}$ (fresh element from bulk) |
| $t > 0$, $z = 0$ | $C_A = C_{A,s}$ (interface) |
| $t > 0$, $z \to \infty$ | $C_A \to C_{A,b}$ (far from interface) |

**Step 1 — Use Boltzmann transformation** $\eta = z/(2\sqrt{D_{AB} t})$.

**Step 2 — Reduce to ODE:**
$$\dfrac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \dfrac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0, \quad \theta = C_A - C_{A,b}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate twice** with BCs $\theta(0) = C_{A,s}-C_{A,b}$ and $\theta(\infty) = 0$:

$$\theta = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,b})\,\mathrm{erfc}(\eta) = (C_{A,s}-C_{A,b})\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Flux at the interface ($z=0$):**

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB}\left.\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0} = (C_{A,s}-C_{A,b})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

**Step 5 — Average over the contact time $t_c$:**

$$\bar{N_A} = \dfrac{1}{t_c}\int_0^{t_c} N_A(t)\,dt = (C_{A,s}-C_{A,b})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}}\cdot 2$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\bar{N_A} = 2(C_{A,s}-C_{A,b})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k_c = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to **average the flux over $t_c$** (Step 5) and only quote the instantaneous flux $N_A(t)$. This leads to a factor of 2 error in $k_c$. Always write the integral explicitly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 reasons KTU students lose marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in Stefan equation:** Using $P$ in Pa in one place and atm in another. Standardise to SI: $P$ in Pa, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $D$ in m²/s, $z$ in m → $N_A$ in mol/m²·s.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-y_A)_{lm}$ correction:** Forgetting the bulk-flow term gives a **wrong Stefan result** by a factor of $(1-\bar{y_A})$. Always include the log-mean.
> 3. **Confusing $K_G$ with $K_L$:** They have **different units**. Use the one matching the driving force in the question.
> 4. **Wrong Sherwood number formula:** The "2" in Ranz-Marshall ($Sh = 2 + ...$) is **not** a coefficient you can drop. It is the pure-diffusion limit and must remain.
> 5. **Mixing diffusivity definitions:** $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s ≠ $D_{AB}$ in m²/s. Multiply cm²/s by $10^{-4}$ to get m²/s.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert % to fractions:** Henry's constant or mole fraction inputs in "%" must be divided by 100.
> 7. **Not stating the assumption list:** Each theory question demands an explicit "Assumptions" list — omitting this costs 2 marks per derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Mass Transfer Coefficients):**
>
> - [ ] **Mass transfer coefficient (k)** is the proportionality between molar flux and concentration driving force. Multiple forms exist: $k_c, k_y, k_x, k_G, k_L, k_p$.
> - [ ] **Individual MTC** describes resistance in **one phase**; **Overall MTC ($K_G, K_L$)** describes the **two-phase combined** resistance.
> - [ ] **Resistance-in-series model:** $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$ and $1/K_L = 1/(m k_G) + 1/k_L$.
> - [ ] **Stefan diffusion** applies when B is stagnant (e.g., drying). Equation: $N_A = (D_{AB} C / z (1-y_A)_{lm}) \Delta y$.
> - [ ] **EQUImolar counter-diffusion** applies in distillation. Equation: $N_A = (D_{AB}/\Delta z)\Delta C$.
> - [ ] **Sherwood number $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$** — the mass transfer analogue of Nusselt number.
> - [ ] **Schmidt number $Sc = \mu/(\rho D_{AB}) = \nu/D_{AB}$** — ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity.
> - [ ] **Lewis number $Le = \alpha/D_{AB} = Sc/Pr$** — ratio of heat to mass diffusivity (used in simultaneous heat + mass transfer).
> - [ ] **Film theory:** $k_c = D_{AB}/\delta$ (steady).
> - [ ] **Penetration theory:** $k_c = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_c)} = 1.13\sqrt{D_{AB}/t_c}$.
> - [ ] **Surface renewal theory:** $k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ (Danckwerts).
> - [ ] **Chilton-Colburn analogy:** $j_D = Sh/(Re\,Sc^{1/3}) \approx j_H \approx f/2$.
> - [ ] **Ranz-Marshall (sphere):** $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Sc^{1/3}$. The "2" is the **pure-diffusion limit** (low Re).
> - [ ] **Dittus-Boelter analogue (turbulent pipe):** $Sh = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Sc^{0.33}$.
> - [ ] **Knudsen diffusion** dominates when pore radius < mean free path of gas (e.g., freeze-drying microporous foods).
> - [ ] **Six types of diffusion:** molecular, equimolar, Stefan (unidirectional), Knudsen, surface, eddy.
> - [ ] **Chapman-Enskog equation** for $D_{AB}$ in gases: $D_{AB} = 0.001858 T^{3/2}/(P M_{AB}^{1/2} \sigma_{AB}^2 \Omega_D)$ (with $\sigma$ in Å, $P$ in atm → $D$ in cm²/s).
> - [ ] **Conversion formula** between MTCs: $k_y = C \cdot k_c = (P/RT) k_p$.
> - [ ] **Typical Sc range:** gases ≈ 0.5–1.5, liquids ≈ 100–10 000, polymers ≈ 10 000–10⁶.
> - [ ] **Controlling resistance rule of thumb:** If $m k_G \gg k_L$ → liquid controls; if $m k_G \ll k_L$ → gas controls.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Heat and Mass Transfer Equipment in Food Processing: Heat Exchanger Equipment, Classification and Constructional Details
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of Heat and Mass Transfer Equipment in Food Processing: Heat Exchanger Equipment, Classification and Constructional Details

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A device engineered to transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids that are at different temperatures, separated by a solid wall (or in direct contact), without allowing the fluids to mix. In food processing, heat exchangers are the workhorse unit operations enabling **pasteurization, sterilization, evaporation, blanching, cooking, and chilling**.

Mathematically, the basic energy balance for a heat exchanger operating at steady state, with negligible heat loss to surroundings, is governed by:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

Where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate, $c_p$ is the specific heat capacity, and subscripts $h$ and $c$ denote the **hot** and **cold** fluid streams, respectively. The transferred duty $Q$ is conventionally expressed in **Watts (W)** or **kW** in SI units.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Tap Sink" Intuition

Imagine a kitchen sink with two taps — one dispensing **boiling water** and the other **ice-cold water**. If you let them flow into a common basin, they mix and you get lukewarm water everywhere. But if you run them through a pipe that has a **thin metal wall separating them** (think of a double-walled thermos), the heat sneaks through the metal from the hot side to the cold side. The water itself never meets, but the energy does. That "thin metal wall" idea, scaled up to industrial stainless steel plates or tubes, is precisely a **heat exchanger**.

In food processing, this is critical because:
- The **hot side** might be heating milk to **72 °C for 15 seconds** (HTST pasteurization).
- The **cold side** might be incoming raw milk at **4 °C**.
- The wall (usually food-grade **AISI 304/316 stainless steel**) ensures the two never cross-contaminate, satisfying hygienic design codes (e.g., **3-A Sanitary Standards**, **EHEDG**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on **why a particular HX is chosen for a specific food** — viscosity, particulate content, fouling tendency, and thermal sensitivity are the key decision drivers. A student who can justify *design selection* scores higher than one who merely lists types.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value/Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Overall heat transfer coefficient, $U$ | **200 – 10,000 W/m²·K** | Depends on HX type |
| Thermal conductivity of SS316 | **16.3 W/m·K** | At 20 °C |
| Hot fluid temperature range (food) | **60 – 140 °C** | Pasteurization to sterilization |
| Cold fluid temperature range | **2 – 50 °C** | Chilling to pre-heating |
| Typical LMTD range | **5 – 50 °C** | Log Mean Temperature Difference |
| Hygienic surface roughness | **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** | 3-A / EHEDG requirement |

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — What Does a Heat Exchanger Actually Do?

A heat exchanger is, at its heart, a **heat conduction problem wrapped in a fluid flow problem**. The thermal energy path is:

**Hot bulk fluid → Hot side convective boundary layer → Metal wall (conduction) → Cold side boundary layer → Cold bulk fluid.**

The combined resistance of these three steps yields the **overall heat transfer coefficient** $U$:

$$\frac{1}{U \cdot A} = \frac{1}{h_h A_h} + \frac{\Delta x}{k_w A_w} + \frac{1}{h_c A_c} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c}$$

Where:
- $h_h, h_c$ = convective HTC of hot and cold fluids (W/m²·K)
- $\Delta x$ = wall thickness (m)
- $k_w$ = thermal conductivity of wall (W/m·K)
- $R_f$ = **fouling resistance** (m²·K/W) — *critical in food processing due to milk proteins, starch gelatinization, etc.*

The *student-friendly mental model* is an **electrical resistor analogy**: heat current is like electric current, $\Delta T$ is like voltage, and $1/(UA)$ is like electrical resistance. The *dominant* resistance (often the fouling layer or the convective film) bottlenecks the entire transfer.

## 2.2 The Four Lenses of Classification

A heat exchanger is classified along **four orthogonal axes** — a point examiners love to test:

**Lens 1 — By Construction (Geometry):**
- **Tubular:** Shell-and-Tube, Double-Tube (concentric pipe), Coil-in-Tank
- **Plate:** Gasketed Plate, Welded Plate, Spiral Plate, Plate-Coil
- **Extended Surface:** Finned-tube, Plate-fin
- **Regenerative:** Fixed matrix, Rotary (Ljungström)
- **Direct Contact:** Spray condenser, cooling tower

**Lens 2 — By Flow Arrangement:**
- **Co-current (Parallel flow)** — both fluids enter the same end
- **Counter-current (Counter flow)** — fluids enter opposite ends — *highest mean ΔT*
- **Cross flow** — flows perpendicular (common in plate-fin)
- **Hybrid** — combinations

**Lens 3 — By Compactness (Heat Transfer Area per Unit Volume):**
- **Non-compact:** $\beta < 700$ m²/m³ (e.g., shell-and-tube)
- **Compact:** $\beta \geq 700$ m²/m³ (e.g., plate, plate-fin)

**Lens 4 — By Heat Transfer Mechanism:**
- **Indirect (Recuperative):** fluids separated by a wall
- **Direct (Open):** fluids in physical contact (e.g., steam injection)
- **Regenerative:** same surface alternately exposed to hot/cold fluid

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} \cdot F$ | Heat duty via LMTD | W |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_h} + \dfrac{\Delta x}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_c} + R_f$ | Overall resistance (for flat/cylindrical) | m²·K/W |
| 4 | $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max,possible}} = f(NTU, C_r)$ | Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | Heat capacity ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ | Steady-state energy balance | W |
| 8 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop (Darcy) | Pa |
| 9 | $Fouling\ Factor\ (R_f) = 0.0001 - 0.0004$ | Typical food service (skim milk, juice) | m²·K/W |
| 10 | $\beta = \dfrac{A}{V}$ | Surface area density (compactness) | m²/m³ |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Caution (KTU 2024 Examiner Trap):** Many students use $\Delta T_{lmtd}$ for crossflow or multi-pass arrangements without applying the **Correction Factor $F$**. The correct form is $Q = U A F \Delta T_{lmtd,cf}$ where $F \le 1$. Skipping $F$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

We begin with the **local** heat transfer rate across a differential element of exchanger area $dA$:

$$dQ = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$

The two streams exchange energy along the length $L$. Let $T_h$ drop and $T_c$ rise. We define:

$$\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$$

Differentiating the hot-side energy balance with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{dT_h}{dx} = -\frac{U \, \Delta T}{\dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h}}$$

$$\frac{dT_c}{dx} = +\frac{U \, \Delta T}{\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}}$$

Subtracting these two gives the rate of change of $\Delta T$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dx} = -U\Delta T \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right)$$

This is a separable first-order ODE. Letting $K = U\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\dot m_h c_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot m_c c_{p,c}}\right)$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -K \, dx$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = L$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right) = K \cdot L$$

Multiplying both sides by $-U / K$:

$$\frac{U L}{K} = \frac{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}{-1} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

Substituting $Q = \dot m_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot m_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ and noting $UL/K = UA \cdot (\text{...})$:

$$\boxed{\;Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lmtd} = U \, A \, \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}\;}$$

**Logic of each step:**
- *Local rate* $dQ$ comes from Newton's law of cooling.
- *Energy conservation* on each stream gives $dT/dx$.
- *Subtraction* isolates the temperature "gap" $\Delta T$ as the driver.
- *Separation of variables* + *integration* yields the exponential decay of $\Delta T$ along the exchanger.
- *Algebraic rearrangement* matches the integrated rate to the LMTD definition.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem:** Milk enters a counter-flow plate HX at $4\,°C$ and leaves at $72\,°C$ for HTST pasteurization, while hot water enters at $90\,°C$ and leaves at $78\,°C$. $\dot m_{milk} = 1.0$ kg/s, $c_{p,milk} = 3.9$ kJ/kg·K, $U = 2500$ W/m²·K. Find the area required.

**Step 1 — Compute hot side outlet from energy balance:**

$$Q = \dot m_{milk} \, c_p \, (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 1.0 \times 3.9 \times (72 - 4) = 265.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute hot water outlet (given):** $T_{h,o} = 78\,°C$, $T_{h,i} = 90\,°C$.

**Step 3 — Identify $\Delta T$ ends (counter-flow):**

In counter-flow, the inlet of one stream aligns with the outlet of the other. So:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 90 - 72 = 18\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 78 - 4 = 74\,°C$$

**Step 4 — Compute LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{74 - 18}{\ln(74/18)} = \frac{56}{\ln(4.111)} = \frac{56}{1.4137} = 39.61\,°C$$

**Step 5 — Apply the rating equation:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \frac{265{,}200}{2500 \times 39.61} = 2.68 \text{ m}^2$$

**Result:** A compact plate heat exchanger with just **2.68 m²** of area suffices — illustrating the *compactness advantage* of plate exchangers over shell-and-tube (which would need ~5–6 m² for the same duty).

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture of a Heat Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Fluid Inlet T_hi] --> B[Hot Side Manifold]
    B --> C[Heat Transfer Surface - Plates or Tubes]
    C --> D[Hot Fluid Outlet T_ho]
    E[Cold Fluid Inlet T_ci] --> F[Cold Side Manifold]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Cold Fluid Outlet T_co]
    H[Wall Conduction k_w] -.-> C
    I[Fouling Layer R_f] -.-> C
    C --> J[Heat Duty Q to Cold Stream]
    K[Flow Configuration] --> C
    L[Parallel] --> K
    M[Counter] --> K
    N[Cross] --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HX[Heat Exchanger Classification]
    HX --> P1[By Construction]
    HX --> P2[By Flow]
    HX --> P3[By Compactness]
    HX --> P4[By Mechanism]
    P1 --> S1[Tubular]
    P1 --> S2[Plate]
    P1 --> S3[Extended Surface]
    P1 --> S4[Regenerative]
    S1 --> S1a[Shell and Tube]
    S1 --> S1b[Double Tube Concentric]
    S1 --> S1c[Coil in Tank]
    S2 --> S2a[Gasketed Plate PHE]
    S2 --> S2b[Welded Plate]
    S2 --> S2c[Spiral Plate]
    S2 --> S2d[Scraped Surface]
    P2 --> F1[Co current]
    P2 --> F2[Counter current]
    P2 --> F3[Cross flow]
    P3 --> C1[Non compact beta less than 700]
    P3 --> C2[Compact beta greater than 700]
    P4 --> M1[Indirect Recuperative]
    P4 --> M2[Direct Contact]
    P4 --> M3[Regenerative]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHE[Plate Heat Exchanger Frame]
        P1[Plate 1 - Hot] --> P2[Plate 2 - Cold]
        P2 --> P3[Plate 3 - Hot]
        P3 --> P4[Plate 4 - Cold]
        P4 --> P5[Plate N - Hot]
    end
    IN_H[Hot In] --> P1
    IN_C[Cold In] --> P3
    P5 --> OUT_H[Hot Out]
    P2 --> OUT_C[Cold Out]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Selection of HX for Food Applications

| Food Product | Recommended HX | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Milk (HTST)** | Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) | High $U$, sanitary, compact, easy CIP |
| **Cream / high-viscosity liquid** | Scraped Surface HX (SSHE) | Continuous scraping prevents burn-on |
| **Tomato paste / ketchup** | Tubular / Scraped Surface | Handles high viscosity & particulates |
| **Fruit juice (clear)** | Plate HX | High heat transfer, gentle on flavor |
| **Yogurt** | Scraped Surface | Viscous, heat-sensitive |
| **Beer / wine** | Plate HX (special gaskets) | Gentle, sanitary, no flavor pickup |
| **Particulate food (soups, baby food)** | Tube-in-Tube / Scraped Surface | Avoids clogging; particles pass through |
| **Condensed milk** | Falling Film Evaporator (HX variant) | Short residence time |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a heat exchanger. With a neat sketch, distinguish between a single-pass counter-flow and co-current heat exchanger.

**Model Answer:**
A heat exchanger is a device that transfers heat between two or more fluids at different temperatures, separated by a solid conducting wall, without mixing of the streams. In a **co-current (parallel) flow**, both hot and cold fluids enter at the same end and flow in the same direction; the temperature difference decreases continuously from inlet to outlet. In a **counter-current flow**, the fluids enter at opposite ends and flow in opposite directions; the temperature difference remains nearly uniform, yielding a higher LMTD and a more compact exchanger. Counter-flow is preferred in food processing (e.g., milk pre-heating in a regeneration section) because of superior heat recovery.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is meant by *fouling factor*? Why is it critically important in food heat exchangers?

**Model Answer:**
The fouling factor $R_f$ is the additional thermal resistance that develops on the heat transfer surface due to deposition of material — in food systems, typically milk proteins (β-lactoglobulin), starch gels, scale, or burned-on sugar. It is reported in **m²·K/W**, with typical food-service values of **0.0001 to 0.0004 m²·K/W**. In food processing, fouling is critical because (i) it reduces the effective $U$, lowering heat duty; (ii) it creates a *biofilm layer* that harbours microbes, breaking hygienic standards; and (iii) it increases pressure drop, raising pumping costs. Hence, food-grade HX designs prioritise **smooth surfaces (Ra ≤ 0.8 µm)** and **clean-in-place (CIP) capability** to manage fouling.

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Classify heat exchangers used in food processing industries. Explain the constructional features and working of a **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** with a neat diagram. Why is it preferred for milk pasteurization?

(b) **[7 Marks]** A **Scraped Surface Heat Exchanger (SSHE)** is used to cool a fruit pulp from 85 °C to 25 °C using cold water entering at 5 °C and leaving at 18 °C. If the mass flow rate of pulp is 0.6 kg/s and $c_{p,pulp} = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K, compute the heat transfer rate, the LMTD, and the required area. Take $U = 1500$ W/m²·K.

**Model Solution (a):**
- Classification along four axes: construction, flow, compactness, mechanism (state at least 3 types each).
- **PHE construction:** frame plate, end plates, carrying and guide bars, multiple corrugated stainless-steel plates (typically 0.5–0.6 mm thick, AISI 316) with corner ports forming alternating hot and cold channels. Gaskets (EPDM/NBR food-grade) seal and direct flow. Plate corrugations (chevron angle 30°–60°) induce turbulence and increase $U$ to 3000–7000 W/m²·K.
- **Working:** Hot and cold streams flow in **counter-current** through alternating channels separated by plates; heat conducts through the thin plate wall.
- **Why preferred for milk:** (i) high $U$ ensures short residence time (gentler on flavor/nutrition); (ii) sanitary design (CIP-compatible); (iii) compact (small footprint); (iv) easy capacity expansion by adding plates; (v) regeneration section recovers up to **90%** of heat, reducing energy cost.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Heat duty:**

$$Q = \dot m \, c_p \, (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 0.6 \times 3.5 \times (85 - 25) = 0.6 \times 3.5 \times 60 = 126 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Identify $\Delta T$ ends (counter-flow, the most efficient configuration for SSHE):**

In counter-flow mode, $\Delta T_1$ at one end pairs the hot inlet with the cold outlet:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 85 - 18 = 67\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 25 - 5 = 20\,°C$$

**Step 3 — LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{67 - 20}{\ln(67/20)} = \frac{47}{\ln(3.35)} = \frac{47}{1.2089} = 38.88\,°C$$

**Step 4 — Required area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \frac{126{,}000}{1500 \times 38.88} = \frac{126{,}000}{58{,}320} \approx 2.16 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $Q = 126$ kW, $\Delta T_{lmtd} \approx 38.9\,°C$, $A \approx 2.16$ m².

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)**. Discuss the role of **baffles, tie rods, and channel heads** in its construction. Mention two food-processing applications.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A counter-flow **double-pipe HX** heats $0.25$ kg/s of oil ($c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) from 30 °C to 80 °C using condensing steam at 150 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800$ W/m²·K. Compute the heat duty, the LMTD, and the heat transfer area.

**Model Solution (a):**
- **STHE construction:** cylindrical shell enclosing a bundle of parallel tubes; tubes are fixed to **tube sheets** at both ends; fluid in tubes is called the *tube-side fluid*, the one in the shell is the *shell-side fluid*.
- **Baffles** (segmental type) are transverse plates that (i) support tubes, (ii) induce cross-flow and turbulence on the shell side, raising $h$ and $U$, (iii) increase residence time.
- **Tie rods and spacers** hold baffles at fixed intervals and maintain tube-bundle rigidity.
- **Channel heads** (bonnets or channels) allow tube-side fluid entry/exit, cleaning, and inspection.
- **Food applications:** (i) pasteurization of juices with high fibre/particulates; (ii) heating/cooling of viscous sugar syrups, oils, and brines; (iii) tomato paste pre-heating; (iv) in-can food sterilization retorts (STHE principle).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Heat duty:**

$$Q = \dot m \, c_p \, (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 0.25 \times 2.1 \times (80 - 30) = 0.25 \times 2.1 \times 50 = 26.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Identify $\Delta T$ ends:**
For condensing steam, $T_{h,i} = T_{h,o} = 150\,°C$ (isothermal hot side).

Counter-flow alignment:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 80 = 70\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 150 - 30 = 120\,°C$$

**Step 3 — LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{120 - 70}{\ln(120/70)} = \frac{50}{\ln(1.7143)} = \frac{50}{0.5390} = 92.77\,°C$$

**Step 4 — Area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \frac{26{,}250}{800 \times 92.77} = \frac{26{,}250}{74{,}216} \approx 0.354 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $Q = 26.25$ kW, $\Delta T_{lmtd} \approx 92.8\,°C$, $A \approx 0.354$ m².

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **LMTD end-assignment trap:** For counter-flow, students often *swapped* $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$. The convention: $\Delta T_1$ is the *larger* of the two end differences, and the LMTD formula expects $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$. If you reverse them, the log flips sign and the result becomes negative — a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Isothermal hot-side oversight:** With condensing steam, the hot side temperature is constant; many students compute $T_{h,o} = 150 - \delta T$ and lose the free simplification.
> 3. **F-factor omission:** For multi-pass and cross-flow HX, omitting the correction factor $F$ (when $F < 0.75$) will cost 1–2 marks. State explicitly "since flow is counter-current single-pass, $F = 1$."
> 4. **Fouling factor missing:** Even when not given in the problem, professional valuation often awards a half-mark for *acknowledging* that real food HX design includes an $R_f$ term.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A device for indirect heat transfer between fluid streams separated by a solid wall; the *heart* of thermal food processing.
- **Four classification axes:** construction (tubular / plate / extended / regenerative), flow (parallel / counter / cross), compactness ($\beta$ in m²/m³), and mechanism (recuperative / regenerative / direct).
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$; always pair the inlet of one stream with the outlet of the other in counter-flow.
- **Rating equation:** $Q = UAF\Delta T_{lmtd}$; in pure counter-flow single-pass, $F = 1$.
- **Fouling resistance $R_f$** in food HX typically ranges from **0.0001 to 0.0004 m²·K/W**; design must include $1/U = 1/h_h + \Delta x/k_w + 1/h_c + R_f$.
- **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE):** High $U$ (3000–7000 W/m²·K), compact, sanitary, ideal for milk/juice HTST, easy CIP — preferred in dairy.
- **Scraped Surface HX (SSHE):** Has rotating blades; handles viscous, sticky, particulate foods (yogurt, ketchup, ice cream mix).
- **Shell-and-Tube HX (STHE):** Rugged, handles high pressure and particulates; baffles and tie rods are constructional essentials; used for sugar syrup, oil, brines.
- **Hygienic construction standard:** Stainless steel **AISI 316**, surface roughness **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm**, drainable design, food-grade gaskets (EPDM, NBR, Viton).
- **Counter-flow > Co-current** in LMTD and heat recovery; the regeneration section of an HTST pasteurizer recovers **up to 90%** of heat.
- **Effectiveness-NTU method** is an alternative to LMTD; useful when outlet temperatures are unknown.
- **Pressure drop $\Delta P$** must be checked against pumping capacity; high-viscosity foods need special consideration.
- **CIP (Clean-in-Place):** Mandatory for food-grade HX; designs must allow circulation of cleaning chemicals (typically 1–2% NaOH at 75 °C) without disassembly.
- **3-A Sanitary Standards / EHEDG compliance** is the gold standard for food HX in India and globally.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heat Transfer Trilogy — Conduction, Convection, Resistance

The physics of a heat exchanger is governed by three sequential thermal resistances acting in **series**:

1. **Convective boundary layer on the hot side** — characterised by hot-side heat transfer coefficient $h_h$ (W/m²·K).
2. **Conduction through the wall** (and any fouling layers) — characterised by wall thermal conductivity $k_w$ (W/m·K) and thickness $\Delta x$ (m).
3. **Convective boundary layer on the cold side** — characterised by cold-side heat transfer coefficient $h_c$ (W/m²·K).

For a *planar* wall, these combine into the classic **series resistance equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U \cdot A} = \frac{1}{h_h A_h} + \frac{\Delta x}{k_w A_w} + \frac{1}{h_c A_c}\;}$$

For a *cylindrical* tube wall (typical of STHE):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U_o A_o} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why":** The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ is the *bottleneck summariser*. If the **fouling layer** (e.g., a 0.5 mm milk-stone deposit) is 10× more resistive than the clean wall, $U$ collapses to roughly 1/10 of its clean value. This is why food engineers obsess over **cleanability** — a poorly cleaned HX is a thermally crippled HX.

## 2.2 The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) — Why Log?

The arithmetic mean $\Delta T$ is *wrong* because the temperature difference between hot and cold streams is not linear along the length; it is **exponential** (since both streams cool/heat exponentially toward equilibrium). The *logarithmic mean* is the **correct average** of an exponential decay.

For a **counter-flow** exchanger with hot fluid dropping from $T_{h,i}$ to $T_{h,o}$ and cold fluid rising from $T_{c,i}$ to $T_{c,o}$:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$$

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

For a **co-current (parallel) flow**:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i} \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$$

The total heat transferred is then:

$$Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lmtd}$$

## 2.3 The Effectiveness–NTU Method — When to Use It

When **outlet temperatures are not known** (e.g., one fluid undergoes phase change), the LMTD method fails because $\Delta T_{lmtd}$ cannot be computed. The **Effectiveness–NTU (ε-NTU)** method is used instead.

- **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):**

$$NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} \quad \text{where} \quad C = \dot m \, c_p$$

- **Heat capacity ratio:**

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{(\dot m c_p)_{min}}{(\dot m c_p)_{max}}$$

- **Effectiveness:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}} = \frac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$$

- **For counter-flow:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]} \quad (C_r \ne 1)$$

- **For $C_r = 1$:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$$

## 2.4 Constructional Principles of Food-Grade HX

| Design Feature | Specification | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Material of construction | **AISI 304 / 316 stainless steel** | Corrosion resistance, food-safe |
| Surface finish | **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** (sometimes 0.5 µm) | Hygienic, easy to clean |
| Gasket material | **EPDM, NBR, Viton, PTFE** (food-grade) | Withstands CIP chemicals, no taint |
| Plate thickness (PHE) | **0.5 – 0.6 mm** | High $U$, flexibility |
| Tube diameter (STHE) | **3/4" to 1"** OD typical | Balance between pressure drop & cleaning |
| Baffle spacing (STHE) | **0.3 – 0.5 × shell ID** | Induce cross-flow, support tubes |
| Drainability | Self-draining orientation | No stagnant zones (microbe traps) |
| Weld quality | Orbital, autogenous, ground smooth | Eliminate crevices |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U A F \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Heat duty (rating equation) | W |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | Log mean temperature difference | K or °C |
| 3 | $1/U = 1/h_h + \Delta x/k_w + 1/h_c + R_f$ | Overall thermal resistance (planar) | m²·K/W |
| 4 | $Q = \dot m_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i}-T_{h,o}) = \dot m_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o}-T_{c,i})$ | Energy balance (no losses) | W |
| 5 | $NTU = UA/C_{min}$ | Number of transfer units | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = f(NTU, C_r)$ | Heat exchanger effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | Heat capacity ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta P = f(L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$ | Pressure drop (tube side, Darcy) | Pa |
| 9 | $R_f = 0.0001 - 0.0004$ | Fouling factor for foods | m²·K/W |
| 10 | $\beta = A/V$ | Compactness (area density) | m²/m³ |
| 11 | $P = NTU$ for $C_r = 0$ | Max effectiveness = 1 at infinite area | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Re} = \rho v D/\mu$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\text{Pr} = c_p \mu/k$ | Prandtl number (fluid property) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\text{Nu} = hD/k$ | Nusselt number (HTC correlation) | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Pipe vs Absolute Value — Markdown Safety:** All absolute-value-style magnitudes are written with the formula context (e.g., $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$ *inside* the fraction). When writing $\vert T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} \vert$ in a markdown table, escape as `$\lvert T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} \rvert$` to keep table syntax intact.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for a Tubular Wall

Consider a cylindrical tube with **inner diameter** $D_i$, **outer diameter** $D_o$, and **wall thermal conductivity** $k_w$. The convective hot fluid (tube side) has $h_i$; the cold fluid (shell side) has $h_o$.

**Step 1 — Heat flow through a single cylindrical wall element of length $L$:**

For a cylindrical shell, Fourier's law in radial form gives:

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi k L (T_{i} - T_{o})}{\ln(D_o/D_i)}$$

Here $T_i$ and $T_o$ are the **inner and outer wall surface temperatures**. Solving for the conduction resistance:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L} \quad [\text{K/W}]$$

**Step 2 — Convection resistance on the inner surface:**

Newton's law of cooling gives $Q = h_i A_i (T_{b,i} - T_{i}) = h_i (\pi D_i L)(T_{b,i} - T_i)$, where $T_{b,i}$ is the bulk temperature of the inner fluid. Hence:

$$R_{conv,i} = \frac{1}{h_i \pi D_i L}$$

**Step 3 — Convection resistance on the outer surface:**

$$R_{conv,o} = \frac{1}{h_o \pi D_o L}$$

**Step 4 — Add resistances in series:**

$$R_{total} = R_{conv,i} + R_{cond} + R_{conv,o}$$

**Step 5 — Define $U_o$ referenced to the outer surface area $A_o = \pi D_o L$:**

By definition, $Q = U_o A_o (T_{b,i} - T_{b,o})$. Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{U_o A_o} = R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_i \pi D_i L} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o \pi D_o L}$$

Dividing both sides by $A_o = \pi D_o L$ and adding fouling factors $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{D_o}{h_i D_i} + \frac{D_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} \frac{D_o}{D_i} + R_{f,o}\;}$$

This is the **canonical design equation** for tubular and shell-and-tube HX used in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — STHE Sizing

> **Problem:** A shell-and-tube HX has $D_i = 20$ mm, $D_o = 25$ mm, $k_w = 16.3$ W/m·K (SS316), $h_i = 2500$ W/m²·K (tube-side water), $h_o = 1800$ W/m²·K (shell-side milk), $R_{f,i} = 0.0002$ m²·K/W, $R_{f,o} = 0.0003$ m²·K/W. Calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient $U_o$ referenced to the outer surface.

**Step 1 — Compute each term:**

**Term 1 — Inner convection:** 

$$\frac{D_o}{h_i D_i} = \frac{0.025}{2500 \times 0.020} = \frac{0.025}{50} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Term 2 — Wall conduction:** 

$$\frac{D_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_w} = \frac{0.025 \times \ln(25/20)}{2 \times 16.3} = \frac{0.025 \times 0.2231}{32.6} = 1.71 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Term 3 — Outer convection:** 

$$\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{1800} = 5.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Term 4 — Inner fouling:** 

$$R_{f,i} \frac{D_o}{D_i} = 0.0002 \times \frac{25}{20} = 0.0002 \times 1.25 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Term 5 — Outer fouling:** 

$$R_{f,o} = 0.0003 = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Sum all terms:**

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = (5.0 + 1.71 + 5.56 + 2.5 + 3.0) \times 10^{-4} = 17.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Invert to get $U_o$:**

$$U_o = \frac{1}{17.77 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 562.7 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with thin walls, **fouling contributes about 31% of the total resistance** — confirming that *cleanability* is design-critical.

## 3.3 F-Correction for Multi-Pass STHE

For a 2-4 shell-and-tube HX, the LMTD method requires the **Correction Factor $F$**:

$$Q = U A F \Delta T_{lmtd,cf}$$

The $F$ factor depends on two dimensionless groups:

$$P = \frac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}, \quad R = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}$$

A common chart-derived formula for a 1-shell-pass, 2-tube-pass exchanger (typical "U-tube" HX) is:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2 + 1}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - R}{1 - PR}\right]}{(R - 1)\,\ln\!\left[\frac{2 - P(R + 1) - \sqrt{R^2 + 1}}{2 - P(R + 1) + \sqrt{R^2 + 1}}\right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Rule:** If $F < 0.75$, the design is *uneconomical* — increase the number of shells in series or switch to a more compact HX type.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Continued — Detailed Problem Solutions)

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Problem 1 — HTST Pasteurizer (Plate HX)

> **Problem:** An HTST pasteurizer processes milk at $1.5$ kg/s from $4\,°C$ to $72\,°C$ in the *heater section* (counter-flow, hot water at $90\,°C$ inlet, $75\,°C$ outlet). $c_{p,milk} = 3.93$ kJ/kg·K, $U = 4500$ W/m²·K. Find: (a) heat duty $Q$, (b) LMTD, (c) heat transfer area $A$.

**Step 1 — Heat duty $Q$:**

$$Q = \dot m \, c_p \, \Delta T_{milk} = 1.5 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 4) = 1.5 \times 3.93 \times 68$$

$$Q = 400.86 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute LMTD in counter-flow:**

Counter-flow pairings:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 90 - 72 = 18\,°C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 75 - 4 = 71\,°C$

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{71 - 18}{\ln(71/18)} = \frac{53}{\ln(3.944)} = \frac{53}{1.3724} = 38.62\,°C$$

**Step 3 — Area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \frac{400{,}860}{4500 \times 38.62} = \frac{400{,}860}{173{,}790} = 2.307 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Validate reasonableness:** A typical PHE plate gives $\approx 0.3$ m² per plate; so we need $2.307/0.3 \approx 8$ plates. *This is realistic and matches industry.*

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Problem 2 — Juice Pasteurizer (STHE)

> **Problem:** Orange juice is heated from $20\,°C$ to $95\,°C$ in a 1-2 STHE using steam at $110\,°C$ condensing on the shell side. Juice flow rate $= 2.0$ kg/s, $c_{p,juice} = 3.85$ kJ/kg·K, $U = 1200$ W/m²·K. Compute $Q$, $\Delta T_{lmtd}$, and $A$.

**Step 1 — Heat duty:**

$$Q = 2.0 \times 3.85 \times (95 - 20) = 2.0 \times 3.85 \times 75 = 577.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — LMTD with condensing steam (isothermal hot):**

The hot side is isothermal at $110\,°C$ (latent heat release, $T_{h,i} = T_{h,o} = 110\,°C$). Pairing in counter-flow:

$$\Delta T_1 = 110 - 95 = 15\,°C, \quad \Delta T_2 = 110 - 20 = 90\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{90 - 15}{\ln(90/15)} = \frac{75}{\ln(6)} = \frac{75}{1.7918} = 41.85\,°C$$

**Step 3 — Area:**

$$A = \frac{577{,}500}{1200 \times 41.85} = \frac{577{,}500}{50{,}220} = 11.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Tube count check:** If each tube is 3 m long with 0.0254 m OD (1"), area per tube = $\pi \times 0.0254 \times 3 \approx 0.239$ m². Number of tubes = $11.5/0.239 \approx 48$ tubes. *Plausible for a small STHE.*

## 3.6 Detailed Worked Problem 3 — NTU-Effectiveness Application

> **Problem:** A cross-flow HX (both fluids unmixed) heats air ($C_{min}$) using hot water ($C_{max}$). $C_r = 0.4$, $NTU = 2.0$. Find the effectiveness from the standard cross-flow chart / correlation:

For cross-flow (one fluid mixed, other unmixed), the KTU-acceptable correlation is:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\!\left[\frac{NTU^{0.22}}{C_r}\,\left(\exp\!\left[-C_r (NTU)^{0.78}\right] - 1\right)\right]$$

**Step 1 — Plug in values:**

$NTU^{0.22} = 2.0^{0.22} = 1.165$, $NTU^{0.78} = 2.0^{0.78} = 1.717$.

Inside the exponential: $\exp[-0.4 \times 1.717] - 1 = \exp(-0.687) - 1 = 0.503 - 1 = -0.497$.

$\frac{1.165}{0.4} \times (-0.497) = 2.913 \times (-0.497) = -1.448$

$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-1.448) = 1 - 0.235 = 0.765$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:** $\varepsilon = 0.765$ means **76.5%** of the maximum possible heat is transferred. The maximum ($NTU \to \infty$) for $C_r = 0.4$ would be $C_r = 0.4$, so 76.5% of 0.4 = 0.306 *wait, no — the effectiveness is the *fraction* of $C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ achieved, not a percentage of $C_r$*. The hot-fluid exit is therefore $T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \varepsilon C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})/C_h$.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side HX Type Comparison

| Criterion | Plate HX (PHE) | Shell-and-Tube (STHE) | Scraped Surface (SSHE) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical $U$ (W/m²·K)** | 3000 – 7000 | 500 – 2500 | 1500 – 3500 |
| **Compactness $\beta$ (m²/m³)** | High (~600–1000) | Low (~50–150) | Medium (~200–400) |
| **Pressure drop** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High |
| **Viscosity range (cP)** | 1 – 1000 | 1 – 5000 | 100 – 50,000 |
| **Particulates tolerated** | Low (clogs) | Moderate | High |
| **Fouling resistance $R_f$** | Low (smooth, turbulent) | Higher | Very low (scraped) |
| **CIP ease** | Excellent | Good (with care) | Fair (mechanical) |
| **Cost per m²** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Typical food use** | Milk, beer, juice | Syrups, brine, oil | Yogurt, ketchup, ice cream mix |

## 3.8 Mini-Derivation: LMTD Special Case — One Fluid Isothermal

When the hot fluid condenses (e.g., steam) or the cold fluid boils, one stream has a **constant temperature**. Let $T_h$ be constant. Then $\Delta T_1 = T_h - T_{c,o}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_h - T_{c,i}$.

LMTD still applies. However, **Effectiveness** is now bounded by the *isothermal* limit:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = 1 \quad \text{(for any finite area, since } T_{h,i} = T_{h,o} = T_h)$$

This means the HX *can theoretically* cool the cold stream down to $T_h$ — never below. This is the principle behind **direct steam injection** and **falling film evaporators** in dairy.
