# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
What is a *Scraped Surface Heat Exchanger (SSHE)*? State two food applications.

**Model Answer:**
A Scraped Surface Heat Exchanger (SSHE) is a jacketed cylindrical vessel fitted with a **rotating shaft carrying scraper blades** that continuously scrape the inner heat transfer surface. This prevents the build-up of fouling material and ensures good heat transfer even with viscous, sticky, or particulate foods. The heating/cooling medium circulates in the outer jacket. *Food applications:* (i) **continuous cooking and cooling of ketchup, jam, and chocolate**; (ii) **yogurt and ice cream mix pasteurization/cooling**; (iii) processing of fruit pulp and baby foods.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between *regenerative* and *recuperative* heat exchangers. Give one example of each used in food processing.

**Model Answer:**
A **recuperative** (or indirect) heat exchanger keeps the two fluids **physically separated by a solid wall**; heat passes through the wall by conduction. Example: a **plate heat exchanger** heating milk with hot water — milk and water never mix. A **regenerative** heat exchanger uses a **thermal storage matrix** that is alternately heated by the hot fluid and then used to heat the cold fluid; the same surface is exposed to both streams at different times. Example: a **rotary regenerator** pre-heating combustion air in a food dehydration oven. In food industry, recuperative HX dominate because they preserve hygienic isolation of streams; regenerative HX are mostly used in air pre-heating of dryers and furnaces.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the constructional features of a **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** with the help of a labelled diagram. Discuss why a PHE is preferred for **HTST pasteurization of milk**.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A counter-flow PHE is used to heat milk from $5\,°C$ to $72\,°C$. The hot water enters at $90\,°C$ and leaves at $75\,°C$. Milk flow rate = $0.8$ kg/s, $c_{p,milk} = 3.9$ kJ/kg·K, $U = 4200$ W/m²·K. Calculate: (i) Heat transfer rate, (ii) LMTD, (iii) Heat transfer area, (iv) The number of plates required if each plate has an effective area of 0.25 m².

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Constructional features:** (i) Frame consisting of fixed plate, pressure plate, carrying bar, guide bar, support column; (ii) Corrugated stainless steel plates (AISI 316, 0.5–0.6 mm thick) hung between the bars; (iii) Food-grade gaskets (EPDM/NBR) around ports and edges forming seals; (iv) Hot and cold streams enter through corner ports and flow in alternating channels in counter-flow; (v) Chevron-corrugations (30°–60°) induce turbulence and enhance $U$; (vi) Tightening bolts compress the plate pack.
- **Why preferred for HTST milk:** (1) Very high $U$ (3000–7000 W/m²·K) gives short residence time and minimal flavour/nutritional damage; (2) Sanitary design with CIP capability; (3) Small footprint; (4) The **regeneration section** recovers up to **90%** of heat from pasteurized milk to preheat incoming cold milk; (5) Easy to add/remove plates to scale capacity.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \dot m \, c_p \, \Delta T_{milk} = 0.8 \times 3.9 \times (72 - 5) = 0.8 \times 3.9 \times 67 = 209.04 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) LMTD (counter-flow):**

$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 90 - 72 = 18\,°C$
$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 75 - 5 = 70\,°C$

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{70 - 18}{\ln(70/18)} = \frac{52}{\ln(3.889)} = \frac{52}{1.358} = 38.29\,°C$$

**(iii) Heat transfer area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \frac{209{,}040}{4200 \times 38.29} = \frac{209{,}040}{160{,}818} = 1.30 \text{ m}^2$$

**(iv) Number of plates:**

$$N = \frac{A}{A_{plate}} = \frac{1.30}{0.25} = 5.2 \approx 6 \text{ plates (round up)}$$

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Describe the **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)**. With a neat sketch, label the following parts: shell, tube bundle, tube sheets, baffles, channel head, tie rods, and impingement plate. Explain the function of each.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A **condensing steam** at $120\,°C$ heats oil in a 1-2 STHE from $40\,°C$ to $100\,°C$. Oil flow rate = $1.0$ kg/s, $c_{p,oil} = 2.2$ kJ/kg·K, $U = 950$ W/m²·K. Find the heat duty, LMTD, area required, and the log-mean-temperature-difference correction factor $F$ (assume $P = 0.45$, $R = 1.0$ for chart use; give an interpretation).

**Model Solution (a):**

**Description:** An STHE consists of a cylindrical shell enclosing a tube bundle. Tubes are held between two **tube sheets** at the front and rear. The shell-side fluid enters one nozzle, flows across the tube bundle (induced by **baffles**), and exits another nozzle. The tube-side fluid enters the front **channel head**, flows through the tubes, and exits the rear channel head.

**Functions of parts:**
- **Shell** — pressure vessel containing the shell-side fluid.
- **Tube bundle** — multiple parallel tubes carrying tube-side fluid; provides the heat transfer area.
- **Tube sheets** — flat plates that hold tubes in position; separate shell and channel.
- **Baffles (segmental)** — transverse plates that direct shell-side flow across the tubes, increasing $h_o$ and supporting tubes against vibration.
- **Channel head (front bonnet)** — directs tube-side fluid in/out; allows inspection.
- **Tie rods and spacers** — hold baffles at fixed intervals; maintain tube-bundle rigidity.
- **Impingement plate** — protects tube inlets from erosion by high-velocity shell-side fluid.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Heat duty:**

$$Q = \dot m \, c_p \, (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 1.0 \times 2.2 \times (100 - 40) = 1.0 \times 2.2 \times 60 = 132 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) LMTD with condensing steam (isothermal):**

$T_h = 120\,°C$ (constant), $T_{c,i} = 40\,°C$, $T_{c,o} = 100\,°C$.

$\Delta T_1 = T_h - T_{c,o} = 120 - 100 = 20\,°C$
$\Delta T_2 = T_h - T_{c,i} = 120 - 40 = 80\,°C$

$$\Delta T_{lmtd,cf} = \frac{80 - 20}{\ln(80/20)} = \frac{60}{\ln(4)} = \frac{60}{1.3863} = 43.28\,°C$$

**(iii) Area (before $F$):**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd,cf}} = \frac{132{,}000}{950 \times 43.28} = \frac{132{,}000}{41{,}116} = 3.21 \text{ m}^2$$

**(iv) $F$ factor and adjusted area:** From the $F$-chart at $P = 0.45$, $R = 1.0$, $F \approx 0.88$ (typical for 1-2 HX). The actual area needed:

$$A_{actual} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd,cf}} = \frac{3.21}{0.88} = 3.65 \text{ m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** Since $F \ge 0.75$, the 1-2 configuration is acceptable. If $F$ had been $< 0.75$, two shells in series (2-4 arrangement) would have been required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **LMTD Sign Error:** Some students compute $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ in the wrong order. The convention is: $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ inside the formula. The LMTD must always be **positive**.
> 2. **Forgetting $F$ in multi-pass:** Even though the problem may not ask for $F$, mentioning it in your solution earns bonus marks. In real KTU board exams, the $F$ factor is *commonly* tested in STHE problems.
> 3. **Unit mixing in $Q$:** $\dot m$ in kg/s, $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K, $\Delta T$ in K → $Q$ in kW. If $c_p$ is mistakenly used in J/kg·K, $Q$ will be 1000× too large.
> 4. **Plate count rounding:** Always **round up** the number of plates (cannot have a fraction of a plate).
> 5. **Steam-side $c_p$:** When a problem says "condensing steam," the hot-side $c_p$ is irrelevant — use only the latent heat or treat it as isothermal.
> 6. **Fouling factor absence:** Even if not given, a sophisticated answer includes a sentence on $R_f$ for food service.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A device that transfers heat between two (or more) fluids separated by a solid wall (indirect) or in direct contact (direct).
- **Main Classification axes:** (1) Construction — tubular, plate, extended surface, regenerative; (2) Flow — co-current, counter-current, cross-flow; (3) Compactness — compact ($\beta \ge 700$ m²/m³) or non-compact; (4) Mechanism — recuperative, regenerative, direct contact.
- **LMTD:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$; always pair inlet-of-one with outlet-of-other in counter-flow.
- **Rating equation:** $Q = UAF\Delta T_{lmtd}$; $F = 1$ for pure counter-current single-pass; $F < 1$ for multi-pass and cross-flow.
- **Resistance equation:** $1/U = 1/h_h + \Delta x/k_w + 1/h_c + R_f$; fouling $R_f$ for food = 0.0001–0.0004 m²·K/W.
- **NTU method:** $\varepsilon = f(NTU, C_r)$; use when outlet temperatures are unknown or one fluid is isothermal.
- **PHE:** Compact, high $U$ (3000–7000), corrugated plates (0.5–0.6 mm AISI 316), gaskets (EPDM/NBR), counter-flow, ideal for milk/juice pasteurization.
- **STHE:** Robust, handles high pressure & particulates, baffles induce cross-flow, used for syrups/brines/oils; 1-2 or 2-4 standard.
- **SSHE:** Rotating scraper blades, handles viscous & particulate foods (yogurt, ketchup, ice cream), high cost.
- **Counter-flow > Co-current** in $\Delta T_{lmtd}$ and heat-recovery efficiency.
- **Fouling and CIP:** Food HX must be designed for clean-in-place; surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm; drainable.
- **3-A Sanitary Standards / EHEDG:** Mandatory hygiene benchmarks for food HX.
- **Energy recovery:** The regeneration section of an HTST pasteurizer can recover up to 90% of thermal energy.
- **Pressure drop check:** $\Delta P = f(L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$; must be within pump capacity.
- **Selection criteria for food HX:** Viscosity, particulate content, thermal sensitivity, fouling tendency, and CIP compatibility.
- **Material:** AISI 304/316 stainless steel; gaskets — EPDM (≤ 95 °C), NBR (≤ 110 °C), Viton (≤ 200 °C), PTFE (≤ 260 °C).
- **Compactness benchmark:** $\beta = A/V$ — PHE has the highest, STHE the lowest.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Absorption: Packed Tower and Plate Tower, Types and Properties of Packing, Choice of Solvent, Solubility of a Gas in the Liquid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gas Absorption: Packed Tower and Plate Tower, Types and Properties of Packing, Choice of Solvent, Solubility of a Gas in the Liquid # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Gas Absorption — Formal Definition **Gas Absorption** is a diffusional mass-transfer operation in which one or more soluble components (solute gases) of a gaseous mixture are selectively dissolved into a **non-volatile liquid solvent**. The insoluble components of the gas phase leave the equipment essentially unchanged, while the solute is transferred across the gas–liquid interface into the bulk of the liquid. Mathematically, the driving force is the departure from thermodynamic equilibrium, expressed as: $$\Delta y_A = y_A - y_A^* \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta x_A = x_A^* - x_A$$ where $y_A^*$ and $x_A^*$ are the gas- and liquid-phase mole fractions in equilibrium with the actual bulk compositions. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Gas absorption is treated as the *counter-current analogue of gas stripping*. In absorption, the lean gas enters the bottom and the solvent enters the top; the rich solvent leaves the bottom and the lean gas leaves the top. This is the standard configuration assumed in every KTU numerical. ## 1.2 Packed Tower — Definition A **Packed Tower (Packed Column)** is a vertical, cylindrical pressure vessel filled with inert solid elements (the *packing*) that provide a large interfacial area for gas–liquid contact. The **liquid** is distributed over the top and flows downward as a thin film over the packing surface, while the **gas** flows upward through the void spaces in a **continuous, differential contact** mode (no discrete stages). ## 1.3 Plate Tower — Definition A **Plate Tower (Tray Tower / Plate Column)** is a vertical vessel fitted with a series of horizontal trays (sieve, valve, bubble-cap) at fixed vertical intervals. Gas and liquid contact occurs in **discrete, stage-wise equilibrium steps**, with liquid cascading from one tray to the next and gas bubbling through the liquid pool on each tray. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Maker vs. The Sieve Stack" | Real World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent | |---|---| | Pour-over coffee maker: water drips slowly through a bed of coffee grounds | **Packed Tower** — continuous, film-type contact | | Sieve stack: water passes through one sieve, then the next, then the next | **Plate Tower** — discrete, stage-wise contact | | Sweetened water in a covered glass: CO₂ keeps dissolving until flat | **Henry's Law equilibrium** — solubility limit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** "**P**acked = **P**orous, **C**ontinuous; **P**late = **P**ool of liquid, **S**tages" — remember this for distinguishing contact mode in two-mark questions. ## 1.5 Standard Industrial Metrics (Bold Constants) - Operating pressure: typically **1 atm to 30 atm** for most food-industry absorbers (CO₂, SO₂, NH₃ removal) - Typical gas superficial velocity: **0.3 to 1.0 m/s** (below flooding) - Common solvents in food processing: **water, vegetable oils, sugar solutions, ethanol–water mixtures** - Henry's law constant for **CO₂ in water at 25 °C ≈ 29.4 atm·(mol/mol)⁻¹** (≈ 3.4 × 10⁻² mol/L·atm) - Henry's law constant for **O₂ in water at 25 °C ≈ 4.01 × 10⁴ atm** - Henry's law constant for **NH₃ in water at 25 °C ≈ 0.086 atm** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Packed Tower — Operational Principles (Step-by-Step) 1. **Gas entry** — Lean (solute-poor) gas enters the *bottom* of the tower and rises through the voidage of the packing. 2. **Liquid distribution** — Lean solvent is sprayed at the *top* through a distributor that creates uniform wetting. 3. **Film formation** — Liquid wets the packing and flows down as a thin, turbulent film (≈ 0.1–0.3 mm thick). 4. **Mass transfer zone** — Solute diffuses from bulk gas → across the gas film → through the interface → across the liquid film → into bulk liquid. The **two-film theory of Whitman (1923)** governs the rate. 5. **Pressure drop** — Gas experiences a continuous pressure drop, ΔP ≈ 10–50 mm H₂O per meter of packing. 6. **Exit streams** — Rich solvent leaves the bottom; treated gas leaves the top. > [!TIP] > **"Why packed towers in food?"** — Low hold-up, no mechanical moving parts, ideal for heat-sensitive liquids (fruit juices, dairy) and corrosive gases (Cl₂, SO₂). ## 2.2 Plate Tower — Operational Principles (Step-by-Step) 1. **Liquid flows horizontally** across each tray and cascades via a downcomer to the tray below. 2. **Gas rises** through perforations/valves and bubbles through the liquid pool. 3. Each tray acts as an **equilibrium stage**; the Murphree efficiency $\eta_M$ quantifies departure from equilibrium. 4. **Pressure drop per tray** is discrete and higher than in packed towers. 5. Used when **high turndown ratio** or **stage-wise design** (McCabe–Thiele) is needed. ## 2.3 Types of Packing ### A. Random (Dumped) Packing | Type | Material | Size (mm) | Surface Area (m²/m³) | Voidage | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Raschig Rings** | Ceramic / SS | 15–50 | 190–380 | 0.62–0.74 | | **Pall Rings** | PP / Metal | 16–90 | 200–380 | 0.74–0.95 | | **Berl Saddles** | Ceramic | 13–50 | 250–480 | 0.62–0.68 | | **Intalox Saddles** | PP / Ceramic | 25–75 | 200–330 | 0.78–0.91 | | **Hiflow / Super-Ring** | PP | 25–90 | 200–350 | 0.93 | ### B. Structured (Regular) Packing - Corrugated metal/plastic sheets stacked in cross-fluted arrangement - **Mellapak 250Y** ≈ 250 m²/m³, voidage ≈ 0.97 - **Gempak, Sulzer, Flexipac** — commercial brands ## 2.4 Desirable Properties of Packing (KTU 2024 — High-Yield) 1. **Large specific surface area** $a$ (m²/m³) — promotes gas–liquid contact 2. **High voidage** $\varepsilon$ (> 0.70) — reduces pressure drop and flooding risk 3. **Chemical inertness** to both gas and liquid 4. **Mechanical strength** to support its own weight plus liquid hold-up 5. **Low cost, low weight, easy installation** 6. **Promote uniform liquid distribution** and avoid channeling 7. **Resistance to fouling and corrosion** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Compare Raschig rings and Pall rings with respect to surface area, voidage, and pressure drop."* — Pall rings win on all three counts (≈ 25 % higher capacity, lower ΔP). ## 2.5 Choice of Solvent — Selection Criteria A good absorbent should satisfy the following: 1. **High solubility** for the solute gas (low Henry's constant $H$, high $m_{slope} \to 0$) 2. **High selectivity** — must not absorb valuable carrier-gas components 3. **Low volatility** — minimal solvent loss in the exit gas 4. **Chemical stability** — no degradation under operating T and P 5. **Low viscosity** — enhances mass-transfer coefficient 6. **Non-corrosive, non-toxic, non-flammable, inexpensive** 7. **Easy regeneration** (low heat of desorption for stripping step) 8. **No chemical reaction with solute** (physical absorption preferred for easy reversibility) > [!EXAMPLE] > In the **DEA (diethanolamine) removal of CO₂**, water would dissolve CO₂ but is too volatile; DEA is selective, regenerable, and gives much higher capacity — hence chosen industrially. ## 2.6 Solubility of a Gas in a Liquid The thermodynamic equilibrium between a gas and a dissolved solute is governed by **Henry's Law (1803)**: $$p_A = H \cdot x_A \quad \text{(true Henry form)}$$ or in mass-transfer notation: $$y_A = m \cdot x_A \quad \text{with} \quad m = \frac{H}{P_{total}}$$ For **dilute systems**, $H$ is the Henry's constant (units of atm or bar). **Larger $H$ ⇒ lower solubility** (O₂ in water is poorly soluble; NH₃ is highly soluble). > [!NOTE] > **Temperature effect:** Solubility of most gases *decreases* with increasing temperature. This is why boiling water expels dissolved air. The van't Hoff relation: > $$\ln H = -\frac{\Delta H_{soln}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) + C$$ ## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet | # | Equation | Symbols / Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $y = mx + b$ (eq. line) | $m$ = slope, $b$ = intercept | McCabe–Thiele construction | | 2 | $H_A = p_A / x_A$ | atm or bar | Henry's law (true) | | 3 | $m = H/P$ | dimensionless | Dilute-solution form | | 4 | $\Delta y = y - y^*$ | mole frac. | Driving force (gas side) | | 5 | $L(\Delta x) = G(\Delta y)$ | mol/s | Material balance | | 6 | $L = V\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | mol/s | Operating line slope $L/G$ | | 7 | $N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$ | dimensionless | Packed-tower transfer units | | 8 | $H_{OG} = Z / N_{OG}$ | m | Height of overall gas transfer unit | | 9 | $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | m | Packed-tower height | | 10 | $Kremser: \frac{y_{N+1}-y^*}{y_1-y^*} = \frac{A^{N+1}-A}{A^{N+1}-1}$ | $A = L/mV$ | Plate-tower analytical solution | | 11 | $K_y \cdot a = \frac{G}{H_{OG}}$ | kmol/m³·s | Overall mass-transfer coefficient | | 12 | $\Delta P_{tower} = \rho_L g h_L + \dots$ | Pa | Hydraulic design check | | 13 | $\eta_M = \frac{y_{n-1} - y_n}{y_{n-1} - y_n^*}$ | fraction | Murphree tray efficiency | | 14 | $F_{LV} = \frac{L}{V}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$ | dim.-less | Flooding correlation parameter | | 15 | $\varepsilon \cdot d_p \gtrsim 0.05$ m | m | Min. packing size for wall effect | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Henry's Law from Thermodynamics At the gas–liquid interface, equality of the chemical potential of the solute in both phases gives: $$\mu_A^{gas}(T,p_A) = \mu_A^{liq}(T,x_A)$$ Expanding around a standard reference state at infinite dilution ($x_A \to 0$): $$\mu_A^{gas,0} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{p_A}{p^0}\right) = \mu_A^{liq,0} + RT \ln x_A$$ Rearranging to isolate $p_A$: $$RT \ln\!\left(\frac{p_A}{p^0}\right) - RT \ln x_A = \mu_A^{liq,0} - \mu_A^{gas,0}$$ $$RT \ln\!\left(\frac{p_A}{p^0 x_A}\right) = \Delta \mu_A^0$$ Since $\Delta \mu_A^0$ is a function of $T$ only, the group $p_A / x_A$ must be a function of $T$ only — **defined as Henry's constant**: $$\boxed{\,H_A(T) \;=\; \frac{p_A}{x_A} \;=\; \text{const. at fixed } T\,}$$ ## 3.2 Operating Line of an Absorber (Packed Tower, Counter-Current) A differential mass balance on the solute over a height $dZ$ of the tower: $$G\,dy = -L\,dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad G\,dy = L(-dx)$$ Integrating between the top (state 1) and any point (state $i$): $$G(y_1 - y) = L(x_1 - x)$$ Solving for $y$: $$\boxed{\,y = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)x \;+\; \left(y_1 - \frac{L}{G}x_1\right)\,}$$ This is a straight line on $y$ vs $x$ coordinates — the **operating line**, with slope $L/G$ and intercept $y_1 - (L/G)x_1$. > [!NOTE] > The operating line passes through $(x_1, y_1)$ — *top* of the tower — and $(x_2, y_2)$ — *bottom*. In KTU graphs the top is always the dilute end. ## 3.3 Number of Transfer Units — $N_{OG}$ Derivation Starting from the two-film model, for the gas phase: $$N_A = K_y\,(y - y^*)\,dA \quad \text{with} \quad dA = a\,S\,dZ$$ Combining with the material balance $G\,S\,dy = N_A\,dA$: $$G\,S\,dy = K_y\,(y - y^*)\,a\,S\,dZ$$ $$\frac{K_y\,a}{G}\,dZ = \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$ Integrating from $y_2$ (bottom) to $y_1$ (top): $$\boxed{\,Z = \underbrace{\frac{G}{K_y a}}_{H_{OG}} \cdot \underbrace{\int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}}_{N_{OG}}\,}$$ This separates the problem into (a) a *kinetic* part $H_{OG}$ (m) and (b) a *thermodynamic* part $N_{OG}$ (dimensionless). ## 3.4 Kremser Equation for a Plate Tower For $N$ ideal equilibrium stages with constant $A = L/(mV)$ and constant $y^* = mx$ (linear equilibrium): $$\frac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ **Step 1:** Substitute $y^* = m x = (L/V) x$, and note that $y_{N+1} = y_{in}$ (gas inlet): $$\frac{y_{in} - m x_{out}}{y_{out} - m x_{in}} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ **Step 2:** Using the overall material balance $L(x_{in} - x_{out}) = V(y_{in} - y_{out})$, eliminate one unknown. **Step 3:** Solve for $N$ (number of stages) numerically or by trial. > [!TIP] > For KTU problems, the **minimum number of stages** is obtained by setting $A \to \infty$ (i.e., infinite solvent rate): the equation collapses to a graphical McCabe–Thiele stepping from the operating line to the equilibrium curve $y = mx$. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Packed-Tower Height **Problem:** A packed tower absorbs NH₃ from air using water. $G = 0.020$ kmol/s, $L = 0.030$ kmol/s. Inlet gas: $y_1 = 0.020$ (top exit actually), $y_2 = 0.0010$. Inlet water: $x_2 = 0$. $H_{OG} = 0.30$ m. Find (a) operating line, (b) $x_1$, (c) tower height. **Solution:** **(a)** Slope: $L/G = 0.030 / 0.020 = 1.5$ Top exit point: $(x_1, y_1)$. Bottom inlet point: $(x_2, y_2) = (0, 0.0010)$. Operating line: $$y = 1.5\,x + 0.0010$$ **(b)** Substitute $y_1 = 0.020$: $$0.020 = 1.5\,x_1 + 0.0010 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = \frac{0.019}{1.5} = 0.01267$$ **(c)** Assume $m = 0$ (very soluble gas — straight horizontal equilibrium line). Then $y - y^* = y$, and: $$N_{OG} = \int_{0.0010}^{0.020} \frac{dy}{y} = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.020}{0.0010}\right) = \ln(20) = 2.996$$ Tower height: $$Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = 0.30 \times 2.996 = \mathbf{0.899\ m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Using the *wrong* integration limits. The exit gas is $y_1 = 0.020$ (top), and the inlet gas is $y_2 = 0.0010$ (bottom). Always integrate from the *gas inlet* to the *gas exit* — i.e., $y_2 \to y_1$. Reversing the limits changes the sign of $N_{OG}$. ## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — Packed vs. Plate | Criterion | Packed Tower | Plate Tower | |---|---|---| | Contact mode | Differential (continuous) | Stage-wise (discrete) | | Pressure drop | Lower (10–50 mm H₂O/m) | Higher (50–100 mm H₂O/tray) | | Liquid hold-up | Low | Moderate–High | | Foaming liquids | Better suited | Poor | | Corrosive service | Better (no moving parts) | More seals, more risk | | Turndown ratio | Poor (channeling) | Good (1 : 3 typical) | | Scale-up | Empirical ($H_{OG}$) | Analytical (Kremser) | | Typical use | Vacuum, heat-sensitive | High-pressure, large diameter | | Min. diameter | $\approx 0.3$ m (wall effect) | $\approx 0.5$ m | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Packed Tower — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Lean Gas Inlet
Bottom] --> B[Gas Distributor
Plenum Chamber] B --> C[Packing Bed
Ceramic Pall Rings] C --> D[Liquid Redistributor
Mid Section] C --> E[Counter-current
Contact Zone] E --> F[Top Mist Eliminator
Demister Pad] F --> G[Treated Gas Outlet
Top] H[Lean Solvent Inlet
Top] --> I[Liquid Distributor
Spray Nozzles] I --> C C --> J[Rich Solvent Collector
Sump] J --> K[Rich Solvent Outlet
Bottom to Stripper] L[Instrumentation
dP, T, y_monitoring] -.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Plate Tower — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Lean Gas Inlet
Bottom] --> B[Stage 1 Tray
Sieve Type] B --> C[Stage 2 Tray
Valve Type] C --> D[Stage 3 Tray
Sieve Type] D --> E[...] E --> F[Top Tray N
Final Contact] F --> G[Treated Gas Outlet
Top with Demister] H[Lean Solvent Inlet
Top] --> F F --> I[Downcomer 1] I --> D D --> J[Downcomer 2] J --> C C --> K[Downcomer 3] K --> B B --> L[Bottom Sump
Rich Solvent] L --> M[Rich Solvent Outlet
To Regeneration] N[Weir and Downcomer
Hydraulic Control] -.-> B N -.-> C N -.-> D ``` ## 4.3 Packed-Tower Mass-Transfer Process Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Gas Bulk
y_A] -->|Convection| B[Gas Film
y_A to y_Ai] B -->|Molecular Diffusion| C[Interface
y_Ai = m x_Ai] C -->|Molecular Diffusion| D[Liquid Film
x_Ai to x_A] D -->|Convection| E[Liquid Bulk
x_A] F[Driving Force
Delta y or Delta x] -.-> B F -.-> D G[Equilibrium Line
y star = m x] -.-> C ``` ## 4.4 Choice-of-Solvent Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Solute Gas Identified] --> B{Solubility
Required High?} B -- Yes --> C{High Volatility
Solvent?} B -- No --> X[Reject: Use Alternative] C -- Yes --> Y[Reject: Use Less Volatile] C -- No --> D{Chemically Reactive
with Solute?} D -- Yes --> Z[Reject: Use Inert] D -- No --> E{Corrosive to
Equipment?} E -- Yes --> X1[Reject: Use Non-corrosive] E -- No --> F{Easily Regenerable
at Low Cost?} F -- No --> X2[Reject: Cost Prohibitive] F -- Yes --> G[Approved Solvent
e.g. Water for NH3] ``` ## 4.5 Packing Selection Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Packing] --> B{Service Type} B -- Corrosive --> C[Ceramic Berl Saddles
or PP Pall Rings] B -- High Capacity --> D[Metal Pall Rings
or Intalox Metal] B -- High Efficiency --> E[Structured Packing
Mellapak 250Y] C --> F{Cost Sensitive?} F -- Yes --> G[Raschig Rings
Ceramic] F -- No --> H[Pall Rings
PP] D --> I{Fouling Risk?} I -- Yes --> J[Pall Rings
Larger Size] I -- No --> K[Intalox Metal
IMTP] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List the desirable properties of a tower packing material. Why are Pall rings preferred over Raschig rings in modern absorbers?** **Model Answer (10 lines):** Desirable properties of packing: (i) large specific surface area $a$ (m²/m³), (ii) high fractional voidage $\varepsilon$ (≥ 0.70), (iii) chemical inertness, (iv) mechanical strength, (v) uniform liquid distribution (no channeling), (vi) low cost and low weight, (vii) resistance to fouling and corrosion. Pall rings vs. Raschig rings: - **Surface area:** Comparable (≈ 200 m²/m³ for 50 mm), but Pall rings have *open* side windows. - **Voidage:** Pall rings ≈ 0.94 vs. Raschig ≈ 0.74 — so lower pressure drop. - **Capacity:** Pall rings give ≈ 25 % higher flooding capacity. - **Efficiency:** Slightly higher $K_G a$ due to better wetting. Hence Pall rings are the modern default; Raschig rings are now used only in highly corrosive ceramic-duty service. > [3 marks: 1 mark for listing 4+ properties, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for concluding statement] --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **State Henry's law. Why is ammonia highly soluble in water while oxygen is poorly soluble?** **Model Answer (8 lines):** Henry's law (1803): *At constant temperature, the partial pressure of a dissolved gas in equilibrium with a liquid is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid.* $$p_A = H \cdot x_A \quad \text{or} \quad y_A P = H \cdot x_A$$ The Henry's constants at 25 °C are: - NH₃ in water: $H \approx 0.086$ atm → highly soluble - O₂ in water: $H \approx 4.01 \times 10^4$ atm → poorly soluble **Reason:** NH₃ reacts with water to form NH₄⁺ + OH⁻ (a *chemical absorption* enhancement), which effectively reduces the partial pressure required to maintain equilibrium. O₂ is only physically dissolved, with weak van der Waals interactions and no reaction, hence requires a very high partial pressure to dissolve appreciably. > [3 marks: 1 mark for stating law + equation, 1 mark for the two Henry constants, 1 mark for reaction argument] --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **packed absorption tower**. Discuss the role of (i) liquid distributor, (ii) packing support, (iii) demister pad, and (iv) redributor. — **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A gas mixture containing **8 mol % SO₂** in air is to be scrubbed with water in a packed tower. The gas flow rate is **1500 m³/h** at STP, and the water flow rate is **4500 kg/h**. The exit gas should contain **not more than 0.5 mol % SO₂**. The equilibrium relation is $y = 2.2\,x$ (over the operating range). Calculate: (i) the slope of the operating line, $L/G$; (ii) the mole fraction of SO₂ in the exit water, $x_1$; (iii) the minimum solvent rate $L_{min}$ for the same gas and exit conditions. — **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Q A **(a) Construction & Working of Packed Absorption Tower (7 marks)** A packed absorption tower is a vertical cylindrical shell, typically 0.3–3 m in diameter, constructed of carbon steel, stainless steel, fibreglass-reinforced plastic (FRP), or rubber-lined steel depending on corrosion. Internally it contains: 1. **Inlet gas sparger / plenum** at the bottom to distribute the rising gas uniformly. 2. **Packing support plate** (grid or screen) to hold the bed while allowing free liquid drainage and gas flow. 3. **Random or structured packing** (e.g., 50 mm Pall rings) packed to a height of 1–10 m in one or more beds. 4. **Liquid distributor** at the top — must give ≥ 40 drip points per m² for good wetting. 5. **Redistributors** between packed sections if the bed height exceeds 3× the column diameter (to cure wall-flow and channeling). 6. **Demister pad / mist eliminator** at the top — a knitted mesh pad removing entrained droplets (> 99 % at design velocity). 7. **Bottom sump** with residence time of 5–10 minutes for surge and pump suction. The lean gas enters at the bottom, rises through the voidage, and contacts the downward-flowing liquid film. The solute diffuses across the gas film, the interface, and the liquid film. The rich solvent is collected in the sump; the treated gas exits through the demister. *Component roles:* - **Liquid distributor:** uniform initial wetting; bad distribution = channeling = 30–50 % loss in efficiency. - **Packing support:** mechanical load bearing; must have ≥ 70 % open area to avoid flooding. - **Demister pad:** prevents solvent loss and downstream contamination. - **Redistributor:** repairs wall-flow maldistribution every 3 m of bed height. > [7 marks: 2 for sketch/description, 4 for component roles, 1 for working statement] **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $y_1 = 0.005$ (exit, top), $y_2 = 0.08$ (inlet, bottom) - $V = 1500$ m³/h at STP = $1500/22.4 = 66.96$ kmol/h - $L = 4500/18 = 250$ kmol/h - $y = 2.2 x$ → $m = 2.2$ - $x_2 = 0$ (fresh water inlet, top) **(i) Slope of operating line:** $$L/G = 250/66.96 = 3.733$$ Since this is on a mole-fraction basis, $L$ and $G$ are molar flows. **(ii) Exit liquid composition $x_1$:** Operating line equation: $$y = \frac{L}{G}(x - x_1) + y_1$$ Substituting the bottom point $(x_2, y_2) = (0, 0.08)$: $$0.08 = 3.733(0 - x_1) + 0.005$$ $$0.075 = -3.733 x_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{x_1 = 0.0201}$$ **(iii) Minimum solvent rate $L_{min}$:** At $L_{min}$, the operating line passes through the equilibrium curve at the gas inlet point $(x_2, y_2)$. With $x_2 = 0$, the line through $(0, 0.08)$ with the maximum slope $(L/G)_{min}$ touches the equilibrium curve $y = 2.2 x$ at the bottom. The pinch occurs at the inlet gas. If the pinch is at the inlet gas composition itself: at $y_2 = 0.08$, $x_2^* = 0.08/2.2 = 0.0364$. With the operating line passing through $(0.0364, 0.08)$ and $(0, 0.005)$: $$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{0.08 - 0.005}{0.0364 - 0} = \frac{0.075}{0.0364} = 2.061$$ $$L_{min} = 2.061 \times 66.96 = 137.97 \text{ kmol/h} = 137.97 \times 18 = 2483 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$L_{actual}/L_{min} = 4500/2483 = 1.81 \quad (\text{typical operating range} \approx 1.5 - 2.0)$$ > [7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2 distribution across sub-parts] --- ### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Compare **packed towers** and **plate towers** for gas absorption under the following heads: contact mode, pressure drop, foaming service, turndown ratio, scale-up method, and typical industrial application. — **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A plate tower is to absorb **acetone vapour** from air using water. The gas inlet contains **3 mol %** acetone and the exit should contain **0.1 mol %**. The water is acetone-free at the inlet. The gas flow is **30 kmol/h** and the water flow is **90 kmol/h**. The equilibrium relation is $y^* = 1.20\,x$ (linear). Determine the **number of ideal stages** using the Kremser equation. — **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Q B **(a) Packed vs. Plate Tower Comparison (7 marks)** | # | Head | Packed Tower | Plate Tower | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Contact mode | Differential (continuous) | Stage-wise (discrete) | | 2 | Pressure drop | Low (10–50 mm H₂O/m) | High (50–100 mm H₂O/tray) | | 3 | Foaming service | **Preferred** (low hold-up) | **Avoid** (foam = flooding) | | 4 | Turndown ratio | Poor (≈ 1.5) | Good (≈ 3.0) | | 5 | Scale-up | Empirical via $H_{OG}$, pilot data | Analytical via Kremser / McCabe–Thiele | | 6 | Typical application | Vacuum, heat-sensitive, corrosive gas | High-pressure, large-dia, wide load swings | > [7 marks: 1 mark per row × 6 + 1 mark for concluding sentence] **(b) Kremser Equation — Number of Ideal Stages (7 marks)** **Given:** $V = 30$ kmol/h (gas), $L = 90$ kmol/h (liquid) - $y_{N+1} = y_{in} = 0.030$, $y_1 = y_{out} = 0.001$ - $x_0 = x_{in} = 0$, $m = 1.20$ **Step 1 — Absorption factor $A$:** $$A = \frac{L}{mV} = \frac{90}{1.20 \times 30} = \frac{90}{36} = 2.5$$ **Step 2 — Kremser equation (from $y_1$ to $y_{N+1}$):** For a *pure* solute with $x_{in} = 0$: $$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ With $x_0 = 0$: $$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1}} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ $$\frac{0.030 - 0.001}{0.030} = \frac{2.5^{N+1} - 2.5}{2.5^{N+1} - 1}$$ $$0.9667 = \frac{2.5^{N+1} - 2.5}{2.5^{N+1} - 1}$$ Let $u = 2.5^{N+1}$: $$0.9667(u - 1) = u - 2.5$$ $$0.9667 u - 0.9667 = u - 2.5$$ $$1.5333 = 0.03333 u$$ $$u = 46$$ $$2.5^{N+1} = 46 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N+1 = \frac{\ln 46}{\ln 2.5} = \frac{3.829}{0.9163} = 4.18$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 3.18 \text{ ideal stages}}$$ Round up: **4 ideal stages** in practice. > [7 marks: 1 for absorption factor, 2 for Kremser setup, 3 for solving, 1 for rounding] > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing gas inlet/outlet assignment** — top of the tower is the *dilute* end (gas exit, solvent inlet). Mixing up $y_1$ and $y_{N+1}$ gives negative answers. Always mark your stream directions on the sketch. > 2. **Forgetting the $m x_0$ term** — when fresh solvent is used ($x_0 = 0$), it disappears, but in *stripping* or *reboiled absorber* problems it does not. The examiner docks 1 mark for this. > 3. **Packed vs. plate confusion** — packed towers use $H_{OG}$ and $N_{OG}$; plate towers use Kremser. Don't mix the two formulas. > 4. **Henry's law units** — use atm, not Pa, unless explicitly stated. The dimensionless $m$ form $y = m x$ requires $P_{total}$ to be the same in the datum. > 5. **Pall ring vs. Raschig ring** — many students list *surface area* as the only advantage. The 1-mark lost is for *forgetting voidage* and *flooding capacity*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Gas absorption** is a *diffusion-controlled* operation; mass transfer occurs across the **two films** at the gas–liquid interface. - **Packed towers** give *differential* contact → use $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$. - **Plate towers** give *stage-wise* contact → use **Kremser equation** or **McCabe–Thiele**. - Counter-current flow → **lean gas at bottom, lean solvent at top**. - **Henry's law:** $p_A = H x_A$ (lower $H$ → higher solubility). - Most gases *lose* solubility with **rising temperature** (Clausius-type behaviour). - **Packing properties to memorise:** surface area, voidage, wettability, cost, corrosion resistance. - **Pall rings > Raschig rings** in capacity, ΔP, and mechanical strength. - **Structured packing** (Mellapak) > random packing for vacuum service and high efficiency. - **Choice of solvent:** high solubility, low volatility, low viscosity, easy regeneration, non-corrosive, cheap. - **Chemical absorption** (e.g., NH₃ + H₂O) ≫ physical absorption in capacity. - **Operating line** slope is $L/G$; **equilibrium line** slope is $m$ (or $H/P$). - **Minimum solvent rate** $L_{min}$ occurs when the operating line touches the equilibrium curve at the *gas inlet* end. - **Flooding velocity** sets the upper gas throughput; design at 50–80 % of flooding. - For a packed tower, **always include a redributor** if bed height > 3× tower diameter. - **Demister pad** is mandatory at the top to recover solvent and protect downstream equipment. - A **Murphree efficiency** $\eta_M < 1$ means the actual number of trays is $N_{actual} = N_{ideal} / \eta_M$.

topics.title: Basic Principle and Operation of Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT402 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Principle and Operation of Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Distillation** is a physical separation process in which a liquid mixture is partially vaporized, and the resulting vapor is subsequently condensed to obtain a product enriched in the more volatile component. The process exploits the **differences in volatility** (i.e., differences in boiling points) of the constituent components under thermodynamic equilibrium conditions. It is classified as a **mass transfer unit operation** governed by **vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)** and is a cornerstone separation technique in food process engineering, biochemical engineering, and chemical engineering.

In the strict KTU 2024 scheme (PCFTT402) terminology, distillation is defined as a **diffusion-based separation operation** where the driving force is the **concentration gradient** between the liquid phase and the vapor phase at the gas–liquid interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 4:** Distillation is examined as a *combined heat and mass transfer* operation, where sensible + latent heat is supplied to the liquid (heat transfer) while volatile components are preferentially transported into the vapor phase (mass transfer). This duality is what distinguishes it from simple evaporation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine a pot of **water with a few drops of vanilla essence** simmering on a stove. Both water and vanilla evaporate, but vanilla (the more *volatile*, lower-boiling component) evaporates **faster and more readily** than water. If you hold a cold plate (a condenser) above the pot, the vapors condense back into liquid. That condensed liquid is now **richer in vanilla aroma** than the original mixture. 

That, in essence, is distillation:
- **Heat** the mixture → components compete to enter the vapor phase.
- The **more volatile** component "wins" and concentrates in the vapor.
- **Cool** the vapor → the enriched condensate is collected.

A more technical analogy is a **marathon race** between molecules on a hot track. The lighter, more agile (volatile) molecules reach the finish line (vapor phase) first, while the heavier, sluggish (less volatile) molecules lag behind in the liquid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Master:**
> - **Volatility** – The tendency of a component to vaporize.
> - **Relative Volatility (α)** – The ratio of vapor pressures (or K-values) of two components. Higher α ⇒ easier separation.
> - **Reflux** – Condensed vapor returned to the column to enhance separation.
> - **Condensate / Distillate** – The vapor that has been condensed to liquid.
> - **Residue / Bottoms** – The remaining liquid in the still, depleted in volatile component.
> - **Azeotrope** – A mixture that boils at constant composition; cannot be separated by simple distillation.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P$ | **101.325 kPa** = 1 atm | Reference condition for boiling points |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in Antoine / Clapeyron equations |
| Latent heat of vaporization (water) | $\lambda$ | **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C | Energy needed to vaporize 1 kg of water |
| Normal boiling point of water | $T_b$ | **373.15 K (100 °C)** | Reference volatile component in food systems |
| Normal boiling point of ethanol | $T_b$ | **351.45 K (78.37 °C)** | Common food-industry volatile |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Foundational Laws of Distillation

Distillation is mathematically grounded in two laws from classical thermodynamics:

### 2.1.1 Raoult's Law (Liquid Phase)
For an *ideal* solution at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, the partial pressure exerted by component $i$ in the vapor is:

$$p_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$

where:
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of $i$ in the **liquid** phase,
- $P_i^{sat}(T)$ = **saturation vapor pressure** of pure $i$ at temperature $T$.

### 2.1.2 Dalton's Law (Vapor Phase)
For an ideal gas mixture:

$$p_i = y_i \cdot P$$

where:
- $y_i$ = mole fraction of $i$ in the **vapor** phase,
- $P$ = total system pressure.

### 2.1.3 Combining Both Laws (VLE Equation)
Equating the two expressions and rearranging:

$$y_i = \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This is the single most-asked governing equation. If you cannot derive $y_i$ from $x_i$, you cannot solve any distillation numerical.

## 2.2 Relative Volatility – The "Ease of Separation" Index

For a binary mixture (i = more volatile, j = less volatile), relative volatility is defined as:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{K_i}{K_j} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_j^{sat}(T)} = \frac{y_i / x_i}{y_j / x_j}$$

Interpretation:
- $\alpha > 1$ ⇒ separation is feasible.
- $\alpha = 1$ ⇒ **azeotrope**, no separation possible by simple distillation.
- $\alpha \gg 1$ (typically $> 1.05$) ⇒ easy separation.
- For the **ethanol–water system at the azeotrope**, $\alpha = 1$ at ~95.6 % ethanol by mass.

## 2.3 The Rayleigh Equation – Heart of Batch Distillation

For a simple differential (Rayleigh) batch distillation at constant pressure, the integrated material balance yields:

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x}$$

For the special case of **constant relative volatility** ($\alpha =$ constant), the closed-form Rayleigh equation is:

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $L_0$ = initial moles of liquid in the still,
- $L$ = moles of liquid remaining,
- $x_0$ = initial mole fraction of the volatile component in the liquid,
- $x$ = final mole fraction of the volatile component in the liquid.

## 2.4 Flash Distillation (Equilibrium / Single-Stage Distillation)

A feed mixture is partially vaporized in a single equilibrium stage (a "flash drum"). Defining the **fraction vaporized** as $q_f$ (mass or mole basis), the material balance gives:

$$y = \frac{x_F}{\alpha + (1 - \alpha) \cdot x_F} \quad \text{(binary, constant } \alpha\text{)}$$

Alternative standard form using the fraction vaporized $f$:

$$y = \frac{x_F - (1 - f) \, x_B}{f}$$

with the equilibrium relation $y^* = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$ coupling the two.

## 2.5 McCabe–Thiele Method (Continuous Distillation – Conceptual Overview)

For a continuous distillation column, the **rectifying section operating line** is:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R + 1} \, x_n + \frac{x_D}{R + 1}$$

where:
- $R$ = reflux ratio ($L/D$),
- $x_D$ = distillate composition (mole fraction of volatile in top product),
- $x_n$, $y_{n+1}$ = liquid and vapor compositions on adjacent stages.

The **stripping section operating line** is:

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'} \, x_m - \frac{B \, x_B}{V'}$$

where $B$ is the bottoms product rate and $x_B$ its composition.

The **feed line (q-line)** for a feed with thermal quality $q$ (moles of liquid formed per mole of feed) is:

$$y = \frac{q}{q - 1} \, x - \frac{z_F}{q - 1}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raoult's Law | $p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Liquid phase VLE |
| 2 | Dalton's Law | $p_i = y_i P$ | Vapor phase VLE |
| 3 | VLE Relation | $y_i = \dfrac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | Equilibrium between phases |
| 4 | Relative Volatility | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}} = \dfrac{y_i/x_i}{y_j/x_j}$ | Separation feasibility |
| 5 | K-value | $K_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P} = \dfrac{y_i}{x_i}$ | Shortcut to equilibrium |
| 6 | Rayleigh (general) | $\ln(L_0/L) = \displaystyle\int_{x_0}^{x} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$ | Batch distillation |
| 7 | Rayleigh (constant α) | $\ln(L_0/L) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\ln(x_0/x) + \alpha\ln\dfrac{1-x}{1-x_0}\right]$ | Batch distillation, ideal |
| 8 | Flash (binary) | $y = \dfrac{x_F}{\alpha + (1-\alpha)x_F}$ | Single-stage flash |
| 9 | Rectifying line | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Continuous column |
| 10 | Stripping line | $y_{m+1} = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x_m - \dfrac{B x_B}{V'}$ | Continuous column |
| 11 | q-line | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Feed condition |
| 12 | Antoine equation | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | Saturation pressure |
| 13 | Mass balance (overall) | $F = D + B$ | Continuous column |
| 14 | Component balance | $F z_F = D x_D + B x_B$ | Continuous column |
| 15 | Reflux ratio | $R = L/D$ | Operating parameter |
| 16 | Minimum plates (Fenske) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\left[(x_D/x_B)(1-x_B)/(1-x_D)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{ij}}$ | Lower bound on stages |
| 17 | Steam distillation | $P_{total} = P_{water}^{sat} + P_{essential\,oil}^{sat}$ | Aroma extraction |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Production of rectified spirits (vodka, whisky), flavor and essence recovery, dealcoholization of wine/beer, aroma stripping from spices.
- **Dairy Industry:** Concentration of milk flavors, separation of milk fat volatiles.
- **Edible Oils:** Deodorization of vegetable oils (high-vacuum steam distillation of free fatty acids and odoriferous compounds).
- **Essential Oils:** Isolation of peppermint oil, citrus oils (steam/hydro-distillation).
- **Water Treatment:** Recovery of high-purity water from fermentation broths.
- **Biofuels:** Production of bioethanol from sugar/starch fermentation broths.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Equation (Batch Distillation)

**Statement:** A charge of $L_0$ moles of liquid mixture with mole fraction $x_0$ of the more volatile component is charged to a simple batch still. Heat is supplied; vapor is removed continuously and condensed. At any instant, $L$ moles of liquid remain in the still with composition $x$. Derive an integral relation between $L$, $L_0$, $x$, and $x_0$.

**Step 1 – Set up the differential material balance.**

A small quantity $dL$ of liquid is vaporized. The vapor produced is in equilibrium with the liquid and has composition $y^*$. The loss of the volatile component from the liquid equals the amount carried away in the vapor:

$$-d(L x) = y^* \, (-dL)$$

Here, the negative sign on $-dL$ indicates that $L$ is decreasing.

**Step 2 – Expand the differential product using the product rule.**

$$-x \, dL - L \, dx = y^* \, (-dL)$$

Multiplying out the right-hand side:

$$-x \, dL - L \, dx = -y^* \, dL$$

**Step 3 – Move all $dL$ terms to one side.**

$$L \, dx = (y^* - x) \, dL$$

**Step 4 – Separate variables.**

$$\frac{dL}{L} = \frac{dx}{y^* - x}$$

**Step 5 – Integrate from the initial state $(L_0, x_0)$ to the final state $(L, x)$.**

$$\int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y^* - x}$$

The left side integrates to $\ln(L) - \ln(L_0)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y^* - x}$$

By convention, we flip the sign and write:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y^* - x}\;}$$

This is the **Rayleigh equation in its most general form**.

**Step 6 – Specialize to constant relative volatility.**

For constant $\alpha$, the equilibrium relation for a binary system is:

$$y^* = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$$

Substituting into the integrand:

$$y^* - x = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x} - x = \frac{\alpha x - x[1 + (\alpha - 1) x]}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$\alpha x - x - (\alpha - 1) x^2 = (\alpha - 1) x - (\alpha - 1) x^2 = (\alpha - 1) x (1 - x)$$

Therefore:

$$y^* - x = \frac{(\alpha - 1) x (1 - x)}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$$

Taking the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{y^* - x} = \frac{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}{(\alpha - 1) x (1 - x)} = \frac{1}{(\alpha - 1)}\!\left[\frac{1}{x} + \frac{\alpha - 1}{1 - x}\right] = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\!\left[\frac{1}{x} + \frac{\alpha}{1 - x} - 1\right]$$

Integrating from $x_0$ to $x$:

$$\int_{x_0}^{x}\frac{dx}{y^*-x} = \frac{1}{\alpha-1}\!\left[\ln\!\frac{x}{x_0} + \alpha\ln\!\frac{1-x_0}{1-x}\right]$$

Equating to the Rayleigh result:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha-1}\!\left[\ln\!\frac{x}{x_0} + \alpha\ln\!\frac{1-x_0}{1-x}\right]$$

Re-arranging to the standard textbook sign convention (multiply by $-1$ and flip fractions):

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha-1}\!\left[\ln\!\frac{x_0}{x} + \alpha\ln\!\frac{1-x}{1-x_0}\right]\;}$$

This is the working form for all Rayleigh problems in KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A batch still initially contains $L_0 = 100$ kmol of an ethanol–water mixture with $x_0 = 0.20$ (mole fraction ethanol). The relative volatility of ethanol with respect to water may be taken as $\alpha = 2.45$ (constant). The distillation is continued until the liquid residue composition drops to $x = 0.05$. Calculate:
(a) The fraction of the original liquid remaining in the still.
(b) The total moles of distillate produced.
(c) The overall mole fraction of ethanol in the collected distillate.

**Solution – Part (a):**

Apply the constant-α Rayleigh equation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\frac{x_0}{x} + \alpha\ln\!\frac{1-x}{1-x_0}\right]$$

Plug in numerical values: $\alpha = 2.45$, $x_0 = 0.20$, $x = 0.05$.

**Compute the first logarithm:**

$$\ln\!\frac{x_0}{x} = \ln\!\frac{0.20}{0.05} = \ln(4) = 1.3863$$

**Compute the second logarithm:**

$$\ln\!\frac{1-x}{1-x_0} = \ln\!\frac{1-0.05}{1-0.20} = \ln\!\frac{0.95}{0.80} = \ln(1.1875) = 0.1719$$

**Compute the bracket:**

$$\ln\!\frac{x_0}{x} + \alpha \ln\!\frac{1-x}{1-x_0} = 1.3863 + 2.45 \times 0.1719 = 1.3863 + 0.4212 = 1.8075$$

**Compute the prefactor:**

$$\frac{1}{\alpha - 1} = \frac{1}{2.45 - 1} = \frac{1}{1.45} = 0.6897$$

**Compute the Rayleigh LHS:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = 0.6897 \times 1.8075 = 1.2463$$

**Solve for the ratio:**

$$\frac{L_0}{L} = e^{1.2463} = 3.477$$

**Invert to get the remaining fraction:**

$$\frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{1}{3.477} = 0.2876$$

Therefore, the liquid remaining is:

$$L = 0.2876 \times 100 = 28.76 \text{ kmol}$$

**Solution – Part (b):**

Total moles of distillate:

$$D = L_0 - L = 100 - 28.76 = 71.24 \text{ kmol}$$

**Solution – Part (c):**

Overall component balance on ethanol:

$$L_0 \, x_0 = L \, x + D \, \bar{x}_D$$

$$100 \times 0.20 = 28.76 \times 0.05 + 71.24 \times \bar{x}_D$$

$$20.0 = 1.438 + 71.24 \, \bar{x}_D$$

$$\bar{x}_D = \frac{20.0 - 1.438}{71.24} = \frac{18.562}{71.24} = 0.2606$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Note that the *instantaneous* vapor composition at the end is much higher in ethanol ($y^* \approx 0.116$ from the VLE relation $y = \alpha x / [1+(\alpha-1)x]$) than the *overall* distillate composition of $0.2606$. This is because the *first* portion of distillate is rich in ethanol, while the *last* portion is leaner. The average lies in between. This is the essence of why batch distillation is less efficient than continuous distillation for a given separation.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Types of Distillation

| Feature | Simple (Batch) | Flash (Equilibrium) | Continuous (Plate Column) | Steam | Vacuum |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of stages | 1 (theoretical) | 1 | Multiple (N plates) | 1 + stripping | 1 + stripping |
| Reflux used? | No | No | Yes | No | No |
| Operating mode | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Batch/Cont. | Batch/Cont. |
| Energy efficiency | Low | Moderate | High | Moderate | High |
| Heat-sensitive food? | Limited | Limited | Good (with vacuum) | Good | Excellent |
| Separation sharpness | Poor → Moderate | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate | Good |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Highest | Low | Moderate |
| Typical food use | Spirit flavoring | Brine concentration | Bioethanol, vodka | Essential oils | Fruit juice aroma |

## 3.4 Energy / Heat-Transfer Perspective (Module Theme)

The latent heat required to vaporize the feed is:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{vap} \cdot \lambda_{vap}$$

where $\lambda_{vap}$ is the **latent heat of vaporization** of the volatile component (e.g., for water $\lambda = 2257$ kJ/kg at 100 °C; for ethanol $\lambda \approx 840$ kJ/kg at 78.4 °C).

For binary mixtures, an approximate effective latent heat is:

$$\lambda_{mix} = \sum_i x_i \, \lambda_i$$

The **overall heat-transfer rate** in a reboiler (assumed once-through, condensing steam side) is:

$$Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

where $U$ is the overall heat-transfer coefficient, $A$ is the heat-transfer area, and $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ is the log-mean temperature difference between the heating medium (e.g., steam) and the boiling liquid.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Simple Batch Distillation Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Feed Tank - Liquid Mixture] --> B[Pre-Heater]
    B --> C[Distillation Still / Reboiler]
    Qheat[Heat Source - Steam or Electric] --> C
    C --> D[Boiling Liquid - Two Phases in Equilibrium]
    D --> E[Vapor Line - Rising Volatiles]
    E --> F[Condenser - Heat Removal Qcond]
    Fcool[Cold Water / Refrigerant] --> F
    F --> G[Condensate Receiver - Distillate]
    G --> H[Reflux Splitter]
    H --> I[Reflux Return to Still]
    H --> J[Distillate Product to Storage]
    C --> K[Residue / Bottoms Withdrawal]
    K --> L[Bottoms Product to Storage]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Liquid feed is pre-heated and charged to the still. Heat is supplied to drive vaporization. The vapor leaves the still rich in the more volatile component, is condensed, and the resulting condensate is split: a portion is returned as **reflux** (to improve separation), and the rest is withdrawn as the **distillate product**. The liquid left behind in the still – depleted in volatiles – is the **bottoms product**.
